verses.flow: The words of Jeremiah son of Hilkiah, of the priests who were in Anathoth in the land of Benjamin,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Anathoth (modern Ras el-Kharrubeh) was a Levitical city in Benjamin (Josh 21:18) approximately 3 miles northeast of Jerusalem. Jeremiah's priestly lineage likely descended from Abiathar, whom Solomon exiled to Anathoth (1 Kgs 2:26-27), creating a family with both priestly heritage and a grievance against the Jerusalem establishment. HEBREW: "Words" (devarim, H1697) can mean both spoken words and acts/events — the title thus encompasses both Jeremiah's oracles and the narrative of his life.

verses.flow: "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, and before you came forth from the womb I set you apart; a prophet to the nations I appointed you."
verses.note: HEBREW: "I knew you" (yedha'tikha, from yada, H3045) carries covenantal and intimate relational force far beyond mere cognitive awareness — the same verb used of covenant election in Amos 3:2 and Gen 4:1. "I set you apart" (hiqdashtikha, from qadash, H6942) means consecrated or sanctified — Jeremiah is priestly-prophetic, set apart before birth. TYPOLOGY: This pre-birth consecration anticipates the Servant of YHWH (Isa 49:1, 5) and is echoed in the calling of the Apostle Paul (Gal 1:15). Ultimately it points to the Son whose mission was ordained before the foundation of the world (1 Pet 1:20).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Then I said, "Ah, Lord YHWH! Behold, I do not know how to speak, for I am only a youth."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ah, Lord YHWH" (Ahah, Adonai YHWH) — this exclamation of protest appears also in Moses' call (Exod 4:10) and Gideon's call (Judg 6:22), forming a recognizable prophetic-call convention. "I am only a youth" (na'ar, H5288) may indicate Jeremiah was a teenager or young adult, though the term can denote subordinate social status. TYPOLOGY: The pattern of reluctant prophet called by divine initiative — Moses, Gideon, Isaiah, Jeremiah — is fulfilled in the one who did not shrink from his mission (John 18:4-8).

verses.flow: Then YHWH stretched out his hand and touched my mouth, and YHWH said to me, "Behold, I have put my words in your mouth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine touching of the prophet's mouth is the physical enactment of verbal inspiration — YHWH himself is the source and guarantor of the prophetic word. Compare Isaiah's lips touched by the coal (Isa 6:7). This underscores the high view of Scripture: the words are simultaneously Jeremiah's and YHWH's (2 Tim 3:16, 2 Pet 1:21).

verses.flow: See, I have appointed you this day over the nations and over the kingdoms, to uproot and to pull down, to destroy and to overthrow, to build and to plant."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The six-fold commission — uproot, pull down, destroy, overthrow, build, plant — frames the entire book of Jeremiah structurally. The ratio of four negative to two positive verbs reflects the preponderance of judgment in Jeremiah's ministry, though hope and restoration (chs. 30-33) are never absent. TYPOLOGY: The prophetic office here described — tearing down and building up kingdoms — ultimately finds its fulfillment in the Messiah, who destroys the works of darkness and establishes an everlasting kingdom (Dan 2:44, Luke 1:33).

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying, "What do you see, Jeremiah?" And I said, "A rod of an almond tree I see."
verses.note: HEBREW: The vision of the "rod of an almond tree" (maqqel shaqed, H8247) is a wordplay: shaqed (almond) sounds nearly identical to shoqed (watching/wakeful, H8245), the word YHWH uses in v. 12. The almond was called the "wakeful tree" in Hebrew because it blooms first in late winter. This is a classic biblical pun (paronomasia) employed to anchor the prophetic message in vivid sensory imagery.

verses.flow: Then YHWH said to me, "You have seen well, for I am watching over my word to perform it."
verses.note: HEBREW: "I am watching over my word" (shoqed ani al-devari) — the wordplay from v. 11 is resolved here. YHWH's watchfulness (shoqed) guarantees the certain fulfillment of every prophetic word he has spoken. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a cornerstone text for the doctrine of predictive prophecy and divine faithfulness — YHWH is not an absentee deity but an actively vigilant sovereign who ensures his declared purposes come to pass (Isa 55:11).

verses.flow: Then YHWH said to me, "From the north the evil shall be let loose upon all the inhabitants of the land.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "From the north the evil shall be let loose" — the "foe from the north" in Jeremiah's early oracles (chs. 4-6) likely referred initially to the Scythians, but the ultimate historical fulfillment was the Babylonian empire under Nebuchadnezzar. Mesopotamian armies, even those coming from the east, approached Palestine from the north via the Fertile Crescent, making "north" the standard directional term for imperial threat. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Some scholars note that in ANE cosmology, the north (tsaphon) was the mountain of divine assembly (cf. Isa 14:13, Ps 48:2); the ironic inversion of a destructive force coming from the sacred north may carry theological freight.

verses.flow: And I — behold, I have made you this day a fortified city, an iron pillar, and bronze walls, against the whole land, against the kings of Judah, its officials, its priests, and the people of the land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The three metaphors — fortified city, iron pillar, bronze walls — together form an image of absolute divine protection surrounding the prophet. Jeremiah will indeed face imprisonment, beatings, and a cistern (chs. 20, 37-38), yet he will not be destroyed. This is not a promise of comfort but of survival for the sake of the prophetic mission. TYPOLOGY: The image of YHWH's servant as impervious to the schemes of enemies anticipates the suffering Servant who, though afflicted, accomplishes his mission indestructibly (Isa 50:7, Luke 4:30).

verses.flow: "Go and cry in the ears of Jerusalem, saying: Thus says YHWH: I remember concerning you the kindness (hesed) of your youth, the love of your betrothals, your walking after me in the wilderness, in a land not sown.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "hesed" (Strong's H2617) denotes covenant loyalty, lovingkindness, and steadfast love — here applied to Israel's early devotion to YHWH in the wilderness period. The "betrothals" (arunotayik) uses betrothal/marriage imagery that frames the Sinai covenant as a divine marriage, a motif Jeremiah and Hosea share extensively. TYPOLOGY: The wilderness faithfulness of early Israel anticipates the Bride of Christ imagery in the NT (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 19:7), where the Church is called to a similar devoted following.

verses.flow: Israel was holy (qodesh) to YHWH, the firstfruits of his harvest; all who devoured him incurred guilt, evil came upon them," declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qodesh" (holy, set apart, Strong's H6944) combined with "firstfruits" (reshit, Strong's H7225) portrays Israel as YHWH's consecrated portion — the sacrificial firstfruits that belong exclusively to God. THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes a principle of covenant protection: those who violate YHWH's holy property bear guilt. TYPOLOGY: Israel as firstfruits anticipates Christ as the "firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep" (1 Cor 15:20) and the Church as a "kind of firstfruits" (Jas 1:18).

verses.flow: And I brought you into a land of garden-orchards to eat its fruit and its good things. But when you came in, you defiled my land and made my inheritance (nachalah) an abomination.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nachalah" (Strong's H5159) denotes inherited land-portion, a deeply covenantal term for the land YHWH gave to Israel as his own estate. The charge of defiling the land connects to Levitical purity law (Lev 18:25-28), where the land itself vomits out those who corrupt it. THEOLOGICAL: The land's sanctity is grounded in its belonging to YHWH, not Israel — a theological point that remains central to the exile theology throughout Jeremiah.

verses.flow: Has a nation changed its gods, even though they are not gods? But my people have changed their glory (kabod) for that which does not profit.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The nations' "gods" here (elohim, Strong's H430) may refer to the divine council members allotted to the nations at Babel (Deut 32:8-9), making the comparison pointed: even those elohim do not abandon their assigned role, yet Israel abandoned YHWH himself. "Kabod" (glory/honor, Strong's H3519) contrasts YHWH's weightiness with the insubstantial "worthlessness" (lo ya'il).

verses.flow: Be appalled, O heavens, at this; be shocked and utterly desolate, declares YHWH.
For my people have committed two evils: they have forsaken me, the fountain of living waters (mayim chayyim), and hewed out for themselves cisterns, broken cisterns that cannot hold water.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Mayim chayyim" (living waters, flowing/fresh spring water) is a recurring image for YHWH as the true source of life and covenant blessing (cf. Jer 17:13; John 4:10-14; 7:37-38). The cistern metaphor contrasts divine provision with human-constructed religious substitutes. TYPOLOGY: Jesus directly applies this image to himself in John 4:10-14 and 7:37-38, claiming to be the very fountain Jeremiah says Israel abandoned.

verses.flow: Your evil will discipline you, and your apostasies (meshubah) will reprove you. Know and see that it is evil and bitter, your forsaking YHWH your God; the fear of me is not in you, declares the Lord YHWH of hosts (tsebaoth).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Meshubah" (apostasy/backsliding, Strong's H4878) is a characteristic Jeremianic term for Israel's repeated turning away, derived from the root shub (to return/turn). "Tsebaoth" (hosts/armies, Strong's H6635) designates YHWH as commander of heavenly and earthly armies, emphasizing his sovereign power in the very context of issuing a legal accusation. THEOLOGICAL: Israel's discipline comes from its own apostasy — a pattern of natural covenant consequence (Deut 28-29).

verses.flow: For long ago I broke your yoke; I burst your bonds; but you said, 'I will not serve.' Yes, on every high hill and under every green tree you bowed down, playing the whore (zanah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Zanah" (to play the whore/commit fornication, Strong's H2181) is the standard OT term for covenant unfaithfulness framed as sexual betrayal. The high places and green trees are well-attested in ANE archaeology as sites of Asherah and Baal worship. HISTORICAL: Archaeological discoveries at sites like Megiddo, Arad, and Hazor confirm the widespread practice of high-place worship, including cult stands and Asherah poles, consistent with Jeremiah's accusation.

verses.flow: Yet I planted you as a choice vine, wholly of pure seed. How then have you turned degenerate before me into a wild vine, a foreign (nokri) vine?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "choice vine" (gephen soreq) image connects to Isaiah's vineyard song (Isa 5:1-7) and anticipates Jesus's self-identification as the "true vine" (John 15:1). Israel's degeneration into a foreign vine contrasts with the faithful Remnant and ultimately with Christ, who perfectly fulfills what Israel failed to be. HEBREW: "Nokri" (foreign/alien, Strong's H5237) emphasizes not merely change but a fundamental foreignness — Israel has become alien to her own covenantal identity.

verses.flow: who say to a tree, 'You are my father,' and to a stone, 'You gave birth to me.' For they have turned their back to me, and not their face. But in the time of their trouble they say, 'Arise and save us!'
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Worshiping a tree as "father" and a stone as mother inverts the covenant relationship — the creature becomes creator. This is precisely what Paul identifies as the root of idolatry in Rom 1:21-25. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The call to idols to "arise and save" echoes the court scene of Ps 82:8, where YHWH alone is the true judge who can arise to save; the idols are implicitly shown to be powerless pretenders.

verses.flow: But where are your gods that you made for yourself? Let them arise, if they can save you in the time of your trouble; for as many as your cities are your gods, O Judah.
'Why do you contend with me? You have all transgressed against me, declares YHWH.
In vain I have struck your children; they received no discipline (musar). Your own sword devoured your prophets like a ravaging lion.
O generation, consider the word of YHWH. Have I been a wilderness to Israel, or a land of thick darkness? Why then do my people say, "We are free to roam; we will come to you no more"?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's rhetorical question — "Have I been a wilderness to Israel?" — directly inverts the exodus narrative. The wilderness was the place of Israel's testing and provision; YHWH provided through it. The accusation is therefore of ingratitude compounded by amnesia. STRUCTURE: This verse begins a new rhetorical unit (vv. 31-37) shifting from metaphor to direct accusation, moving toward the chapter's legal climax.

verses.flow: Can a virgin forget her ornaments, or a bride her attire? Yet my people have forgotten me days without number.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The bride-and-ornaments imagery anticipates the New Covenant bridal motif where the Church adorns herself for the Messiah (Rev 21:2; Eph 5:27). The contrast between a bride's unfailing memory of her wedding attire and Israel's forgetting of YHWH heightens the scandal of their apostasy. HEBREW: The rhetorical question expects a strong negative answer — even the most forgetful human bride remembers; yet Israel's forgetting of YHWH is presented as a supernatural depth of ingratitude.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Israel's claim of innocence ("I have not sinned") represents the climax of covenantal self-deception. This is the very posture Paul describes in Rom 3:9-20 as universal human guilt before God. verses.flow: "If a man sends away his wife and she goes from him and becomes another man's, may he return to her again? Would not that land be greatly defiled? But you have played the harlot with many lovers — yet return to me," declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verse opens with a casuistic legal formula borrowed from Deuteronomy 24:1-4, where remarriage to a divorced wife after she has been with another man is explicitly prohibited as "an abomination (to'evah)." THEOLOGICAL: YHWH uses the very law he gave Israel to expose the absurdity of their situation — they have defiled the covenant land through spiritual adultery, yet he still calls them to return, pointing forward to the New Covenant's greater grace (Jer 31:31-34; Hos 2:14-23). The tension between legal impossibility and divine invitation is resolved only in the gospel.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to me in the days of Josiah the king: "Have you seen what faithless (meshubah) Israel has done? She has gone up on every high mountain and under every green tree, and has played the harlot there.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "days of Josiah" date this oracle to before 622 BC (the reform) or within that reform period. Josiah's reform was triggered by the discovery of the Torah scroll (2 Kings 22), yet Jeremiah indicates the northern kingdom's fate stands as a warning Judah failed to heed. HEBREW: "Meshubah" (H4878, from shuv, "to turn/return") means "turning away" or "apostasy," rendered "faithless" — the same root that forms the call to "return" in vv. 12, 14, 22, creating a profound wordplay: the "turning ones" are called to "turn back."

verses.flow: She saw that for all the adulteries of faithless Israel I had sent her away and given her a certificate of divorce. Yet faithless (meshubah) Judah her sister did not fear, but she also went and played the harlot.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The issuance of a "certificate of divorce" (sefer keritut, lit. "document of cutting off") to Israel (the northern kingdom) via the Assyrian exile is treated as a legally accomplished fact. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In the ancient Near Eastern context, covenant violations were witnessed by the divine council (cf. Deut 32:1; Ps 82); the divorce of Israel is thus a covenantal declaration before heavenly witnesses. TYPOLOGY: Paul's argument in Romans 11 about Israel's "casting off" and future restoration engages this very tradition.

verses.flow: "Return, O faithless children (banim shovavim)," declares YHWH, "for I am your master (ba'al); and I will take you, one from a city and two from a family, and I will bring you to Zion.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ba'al" here means "master/husband/owner" (H1167) and plays on the irony that Israel had chased the Baals (pagan deities) while YHWH was their true Ba'al in the covenantal sense (cf. Hos 2:16, where YHWH says "you will call me 'my Husband' and no longer call me 'my Ba'al'"). TYPOLOGY: "One from a city, two from a family" anticipates the remnant theology central to Paul's argument in Romans 9-11 and the ingathering of the elect (Matt 24:31).

verses.flow: And it shall be, when you are multiplied and fruitful in the land, in those days," declares YHWH, "they shall no more say, 'The ark of the covenant (aron berit) of YHWH.' It shall not come to mind, nor shall they remember it, nor shall they miss it, nor shall it be made again.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The eschatological obsolescence of the Ark of the Covenant is astonishing — Jeremiah, writing during the period of temple religion, declares that the ark itself will be superseded. TYPOLOGY: The ark as the throne/footstool of YHWH's presence is replaced by Jerusalem itself as "the throne of YHWH" (v. 17), pointing forward to the indwelling of the Spirit in the New Covenant community (1 Cor 3:16) and ultimately the New Jerusalem where God himself is the temple (Rev 21:22).

verses.flow: At that time they shall call Jerusalem the throne of YHWH, and all the nations shall be gathered to it, to the name of YHWH, to Jerusalem; and they shall no longer walk after the stubbornness (sherrirut) of their evil heart.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The throne of YHWH" language evokes the cosmic temple motif where YHWH rules from his heavenly council chamber; Jerusalem's eschatological elevation to "throne" status represents the merger of the heavenly and earthly realms. THEOLOGICAL: The gathering of "all nations (goyim)" to Jerusalem anticipates the Gentile inclusion of the New Covenant (Isa 2:2-4; Zech 14:16; Rev 21:24-26) and the lordship of the Messiah over all peoples.

verses.flow: "And I myself said, 'How I would set you among the sons, and give you a desirable land — the most beautiful inheritance (nachalah) of the nations!' And I thought you would call me, 'My Father,' and would not turn from following me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nachalah" (H5159) denotes covenant inheritance, a portion assigned by divine decree. The phrase "most beautiful inheritance of the nations" (tsvi nachalat tzivaot goyim) echoes the territorial assignment language of Deuteronomy 32:8-9, where YHWH allocated lands to the nations. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The land of Israel is YHWH's personal portion (Deut 32:9), making Israel's apostasy a direct affront to YHWH's cosmic territorial sovereignty.

verses.flow: Surely as a woman treacherously departs from her companion, so you have dealt treacherously with me, O house of Israel," declares YHWH.
A voice upon the bare heights is heard — the weeping and the supplications (tachanun) of the sons of Israel — because they have perverted their way; they have forgotten YHWH their God.
"Return, O faithless children (banim shovavim); I will heal your faithlessness (meshubah)." "Behold, we come to you, for you are YHWH our God.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse marks a dramatic liturgical turn — YHWH's call to "return" is answered by a congregational confession (vv. 22b-25), possibly reflecting a penitential liturgy used in worship. TYPOLOGY: The promise "I will heal your faithlessness (meshubah)" anticipates the New Covenant promise of inner transformation (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:26), accomplished through Christ's atoning work (1 Pet 2:24, "by his wounds you were healed," echoing Isa 53:5).

verses.flow: Surely in vain is the salvation that comes from the hills, the tumult on the mountains; surely in YHWH our God is the salvation of Israel.

verses.flow: But the shameful thing (haboshet) has devoured the labor of our fathers from our youth — their flocks and their herds, their sons and their daughters.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Haboshet" (H1322, "the shame/shameful thing") is a scribal dysphemism used to replace the name of Baal in certain texts (cf. Ish-bosheth = "man of shame" for Ish-Baal). HISTORICAL: The devouring of agricultural and familial wealth by the "shameful thing" refers to the enormous economic cost of Baal worship — including child sacrifice, sacred prostitution revenue devoted to cult sites, and the social dissolution that accompanied syncretism in Canaanite-influenced Israelite religion.

verses.flow: Let us lie down in our shame, and let our dishonor cover us; for we have sinned against YHWH our God, we and our fathers, from our youth even to this day; and we have not obeyed the voice of YHWH our God."
"If you return, O Israel," declares YHWH, "to me you shall return; and if you remove your detestable things from before my face, then you shall not wander.",
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "return" (shuv, H7725) appears twice in the opening clause — a Hebrew conditional construction emphasizing that genuine return to YHWH is prerequisite to covenant restoration. The double use of shuv is a characteristic Jeremianic wordplay underscoring total repentance. THEOLOGICAL: This verse opens chapter 4 as a continuation of the conditional covenant appeal begun in 3:12-14, linking the promise of restoration to genuine national repentance rather than mere ritual observance.

verses.flow: "And you shall swear, 'As YHWH lives,' in truth (emet), in justice (mishpat), and in righteousness (tsedaqah); then the nations shall bless themselves in him, and in him shall they glory."
verses.note: HEBREW: The triad "emet" (truth/faithfulness, H571), "mishpat" (justice, H4941), and "tsedaqah" (righteousness, H6666) frames covenant loyalty in its fullest dimensions. Swearing "as YHWH lives" was a binding oath formula. THEOLOGICAL: That the nations would "bless themselves" in YHWH through a restored Israel echoes the Abrahamic covenant promise (Gen 12:3, 22:18), pointing forward to the universal scope of Messiah's reign. TYPOLOGY: Paul cites the seed of Abraham blessing the nations as fulfilled in Christ (Gal 3:8, 16), making this oath formula anticipatory of the New Covenant community.

verses.flow: "Circumcise yourselves to YHWH, and remove the foreskins of your heart, O men of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem; lest my wrath go forth like fire and burn with none to quench it, because of the evil of your doings."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Circumcise yourselves to YHWH" employs the verb mul (H4135) applied not to the flesh but to the "orlah" (foreskin, H6190) of the heart — a radical interiorization of the covenant sign. THEOLOGICAL: This is a key text in the trajectory from physical circumcision to the circumcision of the heart promised in Deut 30:6 and fulfilled in the New Covenant (Rom 2:29; Col 2:11). The command anticipates Jer 31:31-34 and the internalization of Torah. TYPOLOGY: Paul explicitly interprets heart circumcision as accomplished by the Spirit in Christ (Phil 3:3), establishing this verse as a direct messianic anticipation.

verses.flow: "A lion has gone up from his thicket, and a destroyer of nations has set out; he has gone out from his place to make your land a waste; your cities will be ruins without inhabitant."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "A lion from his thicket" refers to Nebuchadnezzar and the Babylonian armies advancing from the north. The lion was a standard symbol of Babylon and its royal power in ANE iconography. The "destroyer of nations" language reflects the Neo-Babylonian imperial campaigns of the late 7th and early 6th centuries BC. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The framing of Babylon as YHWH's instrument against the nations reflects the theology of divine sovereignty over empires found across the Latter Prophets — earthly conquerors act as agents of YHWH's council decrees.

verses.flow: Then I said: "Ah, Lord YHWH, surely you have utterly deceived this people and Jerusalem, saying, 'Peace (shalom) shall be yours,' while the sword reaches to the very soul (nephesh)."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's accusation that YHWH "deceived" (nasha, H5377) the people is a bold prophetic lament — not sinful accusation, but honest wrestling with the contradiction between the false prophets' "shalom" promises and the coming sword. This tension between true and false prophecy is central to Jeremiah's ministry (cf. Jer 23; 28). HEBREW: The word "nephesh" (H5315, soul/life-force/throat) here carries the sense of the very center of one's being — the sword does not merely threaten externally but reaches the deepest core of existence.

verses.flow: At that time it will be said to this people and to Jerusalem: "A scorching wind from the bare heights in the wilderness toward the daughter of my people — not to winnow and not to cleanse,
"A wind fuller than these comes for me; now I will also speak judgments against them."
"Behold, he comes up like clouds, and his chariots like the whirlwind; his horses are swifter than eagles — woe to us, for we are ruined!"
"Wash your heart from evil, O Jerusalem, that you may be saved. How long shall your wicked thoughts lodge within you?"

verses.flow: "Make mention to the nations — behold, proclaim against Jerusalem — 'Watchers come from a distant land; they lift their voice against the cities of Judah.",
"Like keepers of a field they are against her all around, because she has been rebellious against me," declares YHWH.
"Your way and your doings have brought these things upon you. This is your evil; how bitter it is! It has reached to your very heart."
My anguish, my anguish! I writhe in pain! Oh, the walls of my heart! My heart is beating wildly; I cannot keep silent; for I hear the sound of the ram's horn (shofar), the alarm of war.
verses.note: HEBREW: The opening "me'ai me'ai" (my bowels, my bowels) reflects the ancient Hebrew understanding of the viscera as the seat of deep emotion — what modern readers would call the heart. This is one of the most emotionally raw passages in all of Jeremiah, revealing the prophet's genuine inner anguish. THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's suffering as he announces judgment prefigures the suffering of Messiah who also wept over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44). The prophet embodies the sorrow of YHWH himself over his people's destruction.

verses.flow: Disaster upon disaster is called out, for the whole land is laid waste; suddenly my tents are laid waste, my curtains in a moment.
"How long must I see the standard and hear the sound of the ram's horn (shofar)?"

verses.flow: "For my people are foolish; they know me not. They are stupid children; they have no understanding. They are wise in doing evil, but to do good they know not."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The charge that the people are "wise in doing evil, but know not how to do good" is a devastating inversion of wisdom theology. True wisdom (chokmah) in the Hebrew tradition is inseparable from the fear of YHWH (Prov 1:7); here the people have acquired a perverse expertise in evil while remaining ignorant of covenant faithfulness. HEBREW: "Know me not" (lo yeda'uni) is a covenant term — yada (H3045) in Hebrew denotes intimate relational knowing, not merely intellectual awareness. This is covenant abandonment at its deepest level.

verses.flow: I looked on the earth, and behold, it was formless and void (tohu wabohu); and to the heavens, and they had no light.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tohu wabohu" (H8414 + H922) is the exact phrase used in Gen 1:2 for the pre-creation formless void. Jeremiah deliberately echoes creation language to portray the coming judgment as a kind of cosmic de-creation — a reversal of YHWH's ordering of the world. THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most theologically profound passages in all of the prophets. The undoing of creation order signals that covenant violation has cosmic consequences — sin does not merely harm individuals but unravels the structure of ordered existence. TYPOLOGY: The apocalyptic tradition in the NT (Rev 21:1) draws on this de-creation motif, pointing to a final re-creation in Christ that will permanently reverse the curse.

verses.flow: I looked, and behold, the fruitful land was a desert, and all its cities were laid waste before YHWH, before his fierce anger (charon-aph).
For thus says YHWH: "The whole land shall be a desolation; yet I will not make a full end.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Yet I will not make a full end" (lo e'eseh kalah) is a characteristic Jeremianic divine restraint formula appearing also in 5:10, 18; 30:11; 46:28. Even in maximum judgment, YHWH preserves a remnant — a testimony to his covenant faithfulness (hesed) and the indestructibility of his redemptive purpose through the Davidic line. TYPOLOGY: This divine "not a full end" is the basis for Paul's argument in Romans 11 that God has not rejected his people Israel, and anticipates the New Covenant restoration of Jer 30-33.

verses.flow: For I heard a cry like that of a woman in labor, anguish like that of one bearing her first child — the cry of the daughter of Zion gasping for breath, stretching out her hands: "Woe to me! For my soul (nephesh) faints before the killers."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Daughter of Zion" (bat-Tsion) is a recurring poetic personification of Jerusalem and its covenant community, common in the Latter Prophets and Lamentations. The birth-pang imagery used here of judgment recurs in the NT as a metaphor for the birth-pangs of the new age (Matt 24:8; Rom 8:22). TYPOLOGY: The gasping, stretching Daughter of Zion foreshadows both the historical destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BC and its eschatological renewal — the same figure appears in Micah 4:10 in a context of exile followed by redemption, ultimately pointing to the restoration accomplished through Messiah's atoning work.

verses.flow: Roam to and fro through the streets of Jerusalem, and look now and take note, and seek in her broad places whether you can find a man, whether there is one who does justice and seeks faithfulness (emunah), that I may pardon her.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "one who does justice" renders the Hebrew 'oseh mishpat' (H4941), meaning one who executes or practices right judgment. "Faithfulness" renders emunah (H530), denoting steadfastness, reliability, and truthfulness rather than mere intellectual belief. The search for even one righteous person echoes Abraham's intercession for Sodom (Gen 18:23-32), and proleptically foreshadows the one perfectly righteous Man (Rom 3:10). TYPOLOGY: This verse anticipates the ultimate intercession of the Messiah, the only one who perfectly "does justice and seeks faithfulness," whose righteousness becomes the ground of pardon (Isa 53:11; 2 Cor 5:21).

verses.flow: And though they say, "As YHWH lives," surely they swear falsely.

verses.flow: O YHWH, do not your eyes look for faithfulness (emunah)? You have struck them, but they felt no anguish; you have consumed them, but they refused to take correction. They have made their faces harder than rock; they have refused to return.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Faithfulness" again renders emunah (H530). The verb "felt no anguish" (chalu, H2342) carries the sense of being made sick or writhing in pain — they were struck yet felt no moral agony. "Harder than rock" uses selah (H5553), evoking an image of complete spiritual calcification. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's chastisements are designed to produce repentance; their failure to do so indicts the depth of Judah's corruption. This hardening dynamic parallels Pharaoh's heart (Exod 7-11) and the judicial hardening described in Rom 11:7-8.

verses.flow: Therefore a lion from the forest shall strike them down; a wolf of the deserts shall devastate them. A leopard is lying in wait over their cities; everyone who goes out from them shall be torn in pieces, because their transgressions are many, their apostasies (meshuvot) are great.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The three predators — lion, wolf, and leopard — likely correspond typologically to the successive empires that would threaten Judah: Assyria, Babylon, and Persia/other enemies. ANE texts frequently employed animal imagery for conquering powers. The "wolf of the deserts" (ze'ev aravot) may evoke the Babylonian marauders who swept through the arid steppe regions. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The plural "their apostasies are great" (meshuvotam rabbu) — meshuvah (H4878) denotes a turning away or backsliding, derived from shuv (to return/turn). The severity of divine judgment through enemy nations reflects the outworking of the curses of the covenant (Deut 28:49-52).

verses.flow: How can I pardon you? Your children have forsaken me and have sworn by those who are no gods. When I had fed them to the full, they committed adultery and trooped to the house of a prostitute.
They were well-fed lusty stallions, each neighing for his neighbor's wife.
Shall I not punish them for these things? declares YHWH; and shall I not avenge myself on a nation such as this?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question "Shall I not punish them?" (halo' el-elleh efqod) is a divine assertion of covenant justice. YHWH as the righteous judge cannot leave sin unpunished; this is the necessary counterpart to his hesed (steadfast love). The phrase recurs verbatim in v. 29, forming a deliberate literary bracket (inclusio) around the central section of the chapter. STRUCTURE: Verses 9 and 29 function as a refrain, structurally dividing the chapter into two movements of accusation and judgment.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says YHWH, the God of hosts (Elohei Tseva'ot): "Because you have spoken this word, behold, I am making my words in your mouth a fire, and this people wood, and the fire shall devour them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "God of hosts" renders Elohei Tseva'ot (H6635), the title invoking YHWH's command over the heavenly armies and all cosmic powers. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title Tseva'ot ("armies/hosts") reflects YHWH's sovereignty over the divine council and the angelic armies (cf. Ps 89:8; 1 Kgs 22:19). TYPOLOGY: The image of YHWH's word as fire in Jeremiah's mouth (cf. Jer 20:9; 23:29) prefigures the Pentecostal fire of Acts 2 and the word of Christ as a two-edged sword (Rev 1:16; Heb 4:12).

verses.flow: Behold, I am bringing against you a nation from afar, O house of Israel, declares YHWH. It is an enduring nation, it is an ancient nation, a nation whose language you do not know, nor can you understand what they say.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "nation from afar" is Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar, whose empire was east and north of Judah but is described as "from afar" to emphasize its alien and overwhelming character (cf. Jer 6:22; Deut 28:49). The Babylonians were indeed an "ancient nation" (goy me'olam), with a civilization stretching back millennia. "Whose language you do not know" echoes the covenant curses of Deut 28:49, confirming this judgment as a direct fulfillment of Mosaic covenant sanctions.

verses.flow: Their quiver is like an open tomb; they are all mighty warriors.

verses.flow: And when your people say, "Why has YHWH our God done all these things to us?" you shall say to them, "As you have forsaken me and served foreign gods in your land, so you shall serve foreigners in a land that is not yours."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The principle of poetic justice (lex talionis on a national scale) governs this verse: as Judah served foreign gods in their own land, so they shall serve foreigners in a foreign land. This is covenant lawsuit language — YHWH is enforcing the terms of the Sinaitic covenant (Deut 28:36, 64). TYPOLOGY: The pattern of exile and eventual restoration foreshadows the greater exile of humanity from Eden and the ultimate restoration through the Messianic King (Jer 23:5-8; Isa 11:11-12).

verses.flow: Declare this in the house of Jacob, proclaim it in Judah:
"Hear this, O foolish and senseless people, who have eyes but do not see, who have ears but do not hear.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Eyes but do not see, ears but do not hear" is the language of judicial spiritual blindness, drawing on the Mosaic warning of Deut 29:4. This verse is directly invoked by Jesus in his explanation of parabolic teaching as judgment upon covenant-breaking Israel (Matt 13:13-15; Mark 8:18), and by Paul in Rom 11:8. TYPOLOGY: The unseeing eyes and unhearing ears of Israel find their ultimate remedy in the Messianic Servant who "opens the eyes of the blind" (Isa 42:7; John 9:39).

verses.flow: Do you not fear me? declares YHWH. Do you not tremble before me? I placed the sand as the boundary for the sea, a perpetual decree, and it cannot pass over it; though the waves toss, they cannot prevail; though they roar, they cannot pass over it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's command over the sea — setting the sand as an eternal boundary — is an assertion of creatorial sovereignty that shames Israel's idolatry. The sea in ANE cosmology was a symbol of chaos (cf. Ps 89:9-10; Job 38:8-11), and YHWH's mastery of it was a mark of supreme divine authority. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The motif of YHWH subduing the chaotic waters underlies the divine council's celebration of his kingship in Ps 93 and 96-99, and stands in contrast to Baal's mythological struggle with Yam.

verses.flow: But this people has a stubborn and rebellious heart; they have turned aside and gone away.
They do not say in their hearts, Let us fear YHWH our God, who gives the rain in its season, the autumn rain and the spring rain, and keeps for us the weeks appointed for the harvest.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Autumn rain" renders yoreh (H3138), the early rains of October-November that soften the ground for plowing and planting. "Spring rain" renders malqosh (H4456), the latter rains of March-April that swell the grain before harvest. HISTORICAL: Palestinian agriculture was entirely dependent on these two rain seasons; their withholding was explicitly a covenant curse (Deut 11:17; 28:24). The "appointed weeks of harvest" (shavuot chuqqot qatsir) likely refers to the seven weeks between Passover and Pentecost, underscoring the liturgical-agricultural calendar of Israel.

verses.flow: Your iniquities have turned these away, and your sins have kept good from you.
For wicked men are found among my people; they lurk like fowlers lying in wait; they set a trap; they catch men.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Fowlers" renders yaqush (H3353), a bird-trapper or fowler. The image of wicked men lurking like hunters setting snares for human prey evokes Prov 1:10-19 and Ps 10:8-10. THEOLOGICAL: The inversion here is stark — those who should have been shepherds of the people (leaders, merchants, priests) have become predators. This social exploitation of the vulnerable is among the gravest covenant sins (cf. Amos 8:4-6; Mic 2:1-2).

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: The prophets prophesy falsely, and the priests rule at their direction; and my people love to have it so — but what will you do when the end comes?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing verse identifies a triple corruption: false prophets, complicit priests, and a people who love the deception. This is the covenant community's total systemic failure. The prophets do not merely err — they "prophesy falsely" (nibbu'u baseqer); the priests "rule at their direction" (yirdu al-yadeyhem), meaning under the prophets' authority rather than YHWH's Torah. TYPOLOGY: The question "What will you do when the end comes?" (u'mah ta'asu le'achritah) is eschatologically charged, pointing beyond the immediate Babylonian crisis to the ultimate Day of YHWH (cf. Amos 5:18-20; Rev 6:17).

verses.flow: Flee for safety, O sons of Benjamin, from the midst of Jerusalem! Blow the trumpet in Tekoa, and raise a signal over Beth-haccherem, for disaster (ra'ah) looms from the north, and a great shattering.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Tekoa was approximately 10 miles south of Jerusalem (modern Khirbet Tequa), and Beth-haccherem is likely identified with Ramat Rachel between Jerusalem and Bethlehem — elevated sites well-suited for signal fires. The irony is sharp: Benjamin's territory, which flanked Jerusalem to the north and east, is told to flee south, reversing normal directions of safety. The "disaster from the north" (ra'ah min-tsafon) is a recurring motif in Jeremiah (1:14; 4:6) referencing the Babylonian threat.

The command to "hew down her trees" echoes siege warfare practices attested in Assyrian reliefs and the Deuteronomic warnings of Deut 20:19-20, making it all the more striking that YHWH now commands what the Torah restricted for enemies.

verses.flow: To whom shall I speak and give warning, that they may hear? Behold, their ear is uncircumcised, and they are not able to give heed; behold, the word of YHWH has become to them a reproach; they take no delight in it.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "uncircumcised ear" ('arlah oznam) is a striking anatomical metaphor implying that the organ of hearing has its own foreskin — a barrier preventing reception of the covenant word. Circumcision was the sign of covenant inclusion (Gen 17), so an uncircumcised ear signals covenant exclusion and hardness of heart. This language is echoed in Acts 7:51 where Stephen charges the Sanhedrin with being "uncircumcised in heart and ears."

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The false prophets' cry of "shalom, shalom" when there is no shalom is one of the most concentrated indictments of false prophecy in the Hebrew Bible. Shalom (Strong's H7965) encompasses far more than "peace" — it denotes wholeness, completeness, covenant wellbeing. To pronounce shalom where YHWH has pronounced judgment is to contradict the divine word and heal the wound "lightly" (qalal, superficially). This passage is echoed in Ezek 13:10 and anticipates Jesus's warning about false prophets in Matt 7:15.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "Stand at the crossroads and look; ask for the ancient paths (netivot olam), where the good way is, and walk in it; and find rest (margeah) for your souls. But they said, 'We will not walk in it.'"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "ancient paths" (netivot olam) invite Israel to return to the covenantal way of life established by YHWH — a Deuteronomic ideal of obedience to Torah as the path of life and rest. The promise of "rest for your souls" (margeah la-nafshechem) is remarkably echoed in Jesus's invitation in Matt 11:29 ("you will find rest for your souls"), suggesting a conscious typological allusion: Jesus presents himself as the embodiment of the ancient path, the true fulfillment of Torah-rest. HEBREW: "netivot olam" — the paths of antiquity, the ways of the ages, implying both time-tested wisdom and the covenantal heritage.

verses.flow: "I set watchmen (tsofim) over you, saying, 'Give heed to the sound of the trumpet!' But they said, 'We will not give heed.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "watchmen" (tsofim, Strong's H6822) placed over Israel recall Ezekiel's extended watchman theology (Ezek 3:17; 33:7), where the prophet himself is appointed as YHWH's lookout for the nation. The repeated refusal — "we will not give heed" — is the quintessential covenant rebellion formula, echoing the wilderness generation's hardness catalogued in Ps 95:7-11 and cited in Heb 3:7-11 as a warning to the new covenant community.

verses.flow: Hear, O earth: behold, I am bringing disaster upon this people, the fruit of their thoughts (machshevot), because they have not given heed to my words, and as for my Torah (instruction) — they have rejected it."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Machshevot" (Strong's H4284) — plans, thoughts, devices — emphasizes that the disaster is the organic fruit of Israel's own interior scheming and rejection of YHWH's Torah. The word "Torah" here should not be flattened to "law" alone; it carries the sense of instruction, teaching, and covenantal direction. Rejection of Torah is rejection of YHWH's own self-disclosure.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says YHWH: "Behold, I will lay stumbling blocks before this people; and fathers and sons together shall stumble over them; the neighbor and his friend shall perish."
Thus says YHWH: "Behold, a people is coming from the land of the north, a great nation is stirring from the farthest parts of the earth.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "people from the land of the north" almost certainly refers to Babylon, whose armies approached Judah via the Fertile Crescent from the north even though Babylon lay to the northeast. This geographical convention of "the north" as the direction of divine judgment is rooted in both topography and ancient Near Eastern cosmic geography, where chaos and threatening powers descended from the northern mountains. Compare the "foe from the north" in Jer 1:14; 4:6; 10:22.

verses.flow: "I have made you a tester (bachon) among my people, a fortifier, that you may know and test their way."
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "bachon" (Strong's H974 root: bachan) means an assayer or tester of metals — one who applies fire to ore to determine its purity. Jeremiah is cast here in the dual role of metallurgical tester and fortified tower (some manuscripts read "fortified one," batsur). The metaphor anticipates the extended smelting image of vv. 28-30, where the refining process yields only dross.

verses.flow: "All of them are the most stubborn of rebels; going about with slanders; they are bronze and iron — all of them act corruptly."
"The bellows blow fiercely; lead is consumed by the fire; in vain the refining goes on, for the wicked are not separated.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The failed smelting process is a devastating image of total covenant corruption: even the intense purifying fire of divine judgment cannot extract righteousness from the nation because the wicked are not separated out. Lead was used in antiquity as a flux to draw off impurities, but here even this fails. The image implies not merely moral failure but near-ontological corruption — Israel has become so alloyed with wickedness that the refining process itself is rendered futile, anticipating the New Covenant's promise of inner transformation (Jer 31:31-34; Ezek 36:26).

verses.flow: Rejected silver they are called, for YHWH has rejected them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Rejected silver" (kesef nim'as) is the culminating verdict of the chapter and of the entire refining metaphor. YHWH's declaration that he has "rejected" (ma'as, Strong's H3988) his own people echoes the covenant curse language of Lev 26 and Deut 28-29. Yet this rejection is never final in Jeremiah's larger theological framework — the same book that pronounces this verdict also announces the New Covenant (Jer 31:31-34) and the restoration of the people (Jer 33). The chapter thus embodies the prophetic dialectic of judgment and hope that runs through the entire book.

verses.flow: The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "Temple Sermon" of Jeremiah 7 is one of the most significant prophetic addresses in the Hebrew canon, delivered likely during the reign of Jehoiakim (cf. Jer 26:1-6, which provides the historical setting). The sermon's prose parallel in ch. 26 indicates it provoked a death threat against Jeremiah, illustrating the political volatility of prophetic critique during this period.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: "Amend your ways and your deeds, and I will let you dwell in this place."
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "amend your ways" uses the Hebrew verb yasav (H3190, to be good/right) in Hiphil — "make good your ways." The repetition of "ways and deeds" (darkekhem v'ma'allelekhem) is a covenant-lawsuit formula characteristic of Deuteronomistic speech, calling Israel back to the Sinai covenant stipulations.

verses.flow: "Do not trust in the lying words that say, 'The temple (hekal) of YHWH, the temple of YHWH, the temple of YHWH are these.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triple repetition "the temple of YHWH, the temple of YHWH, the temple of YHWH" (hekal YHWH x3) reflects a magical-incantatory formula the people employed, treating the physical Temple as an automatic guarantee of divine protection regardless of moral conduct. This is a form of false sacramentalism — trusting in the sign rather than the covenant Lord signified.

verses.flow: "For if you truly amend your ways and your deeds, if you truly execute justice (mishpat) between a man and his neighbor,"

verses.flow: "if you do not oppress the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, and do not shed innocent blood in this place, and do not walk after other gods to your own harm,"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse summarizes the heart of covenant social ethics: protection of the triad of the vulnerable (ger/sojourner, yatom/fatherless, almanah/widow). This triad appears throughout the Torah (Deut 10:18; 14:29) and the Prophets as a benchmark of covenant faithfulness. Shedding innocent blood likely refers to corrupt judicial practices and possibly child sacrifice.

verses.flow: "and then come and stand before me in this house which is called by my name, and say, 'We have been delivered!' — only to go on doing all these abominations?"
"Has this house, which is called by my name, become a den of robbers in your eyes? Behold, I myself have seen it, declares YHWH."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus directly quotes this verse in his cleansing of the Temple (Matt 21:13; Mark 11:17; Luke 19:46), combining it with Isaiah 56:7 ("house of prayer"). By citing Jeremiah's Temple sermon, Jesus invokes the full prophetic context of divine judgment on corrupt Temple worship, implicitly warning of the Temple's coming destruction (70 AD), just as Jeremiah warned of the Babylonian destruction.

verses.flow: "But go now to my place that was in Shiloh, where I caused my name to dwell at first, and see what I did to it on account of the evil of my people Israel."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Shiloh (modern Khirbet Seilun) was the location of the Tabernacle during the period of the Judges (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1-4). Its destruction by the Philistines after the capture of the ark (c. 1050 BC) is not narrated directly in Scripture but is referenced here and in Psalm 78:60. Archaeological evidence from Shiloh confirms a major destruction layer consistent with the 11th century BC.

verses.flow: "As for you, do not pray for this people, nor lift up a cry or a prayer on their behalf, and do not intercede with me, for I will not hear you."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This remarkable prohibition against intercession signals that Israel has crossed a covenantal threshold of judgment — even prophetic intercession is now foreclosed. Moses and Samuel were paradigmatic intercessors (cf. Jer 15:1), yet even they could not now avail. This marks a solemn escalation in the divine lawsuit and anticipates the new covenant necessity (Jer 31:31-34) since the old covenant has been so comprehensively broken.

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The worship of the "queen of heaven" reflects the underlying reality of the divine council framework: elohim (spiritual beings) were allotted to the nations at Babel (Deut 32:8 LXX/DSS), and Israel's apostasy here consists of worshipping these subordinate divine beings rather than YHWH, the Most High. Such beings are real entities, but they are not to receive worship belonging to YHWH alone.

verses.flow: "For in the day that I brought your fathers out of the land of Egypt, I did not speak to them or command them concerning burnt offerings and sacrifices."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse has generated significant theological debate. The statement that YHWH did not command sacrifices at the Exodus does not contradict Leviticus but rather prioritizes the relational-obedience framework of the covenant (v. 23) over ritual performance. The Hebrew lo... al (not... concerning) may bear the nuance "not primarily" or "not as the basis of." The prophetic tradition consistently subordinates cult to covenant ethics (cf. Amos 5:21-24; Isa 1:11-17; Mic 6:6-8).

verses.flow: And death shall be chosen rather than life by all the remnant that remains of this evil family, in all the places remaining where I have driven them, declares YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The preference of death over life inverts the covenantal structure of Deuteronomy, where life and blessing were set before Israel as the reward of obedience (Deut 30:19). The phrase "evil family" (mishpachah ha-ra'ah) characterizes the covenant people corporately as having forfeited their identity. (Deut 30:15-19)

verses.flow: And you shall say to them, Thus says YHWH: Do men fall and not rise again? Does one turn away and not return?
Why then has this people turned away — Jerusalem — in perpetual turning away? They hold fast to deceit; they refuse to return.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: I have given heed and listened, but they did not speak rightly; no man relents of his evil, saying, "What have I done?" Everyone turns to his own course, like a horse rushing headlong into battle.
Even the stork in the heavens knows her appointed times, and the turtledove and the swallow and the crane keep the time of their coming; but my people do not know the ordinance (mishpat) of YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ordinance" translates mishpat (Strong's H4941), carrying the senses of judgment, legal decision, and customary practice. The contrast is striking: migratory birds obey an instinct embedded in creation by YHWH, while Israel, who possesses the revealed mishpat of YHWH, is less perceptive than an animal. (Isa 1:3)

verses.flow: How can you say, "We are wise, and the Torah (torah) of YHWH is with us"? But behold, the lying pen of the scribes has made it into a lie.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torah" (torah, Strong's H8451) refers here not merely to law in a narrow juridical sense but to the whole body of divine instruction. The "lying pen of the scribes" (et shaqer soferim) suggests that official scribal activity had distorted or mishandled the written Torah, perhaps through selective interpretation or false supplementation. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a sobering indictment of religious intellectualism divorced from genuine obedience — having the text but corrupting its meaning.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: The wise men are put to shame; they are dismayed and taken. Behold, they have rejected the word (dabar) of YHWH, so what wisdom is in them?

words.g: from a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: Therefore I will give their wives to others and their fields to new owners; for from the least even to the greatest, everyone is making gain unjustly; from the prophet even to the priest, everyone deals falsely.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: They have healed the wound of my people lightly, saying, "Peace (shalom), shalom!" when there is no shalom.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shalom" (Strong's H7965) encompasses completeness, wholeness, peace, and well-being — a word far richer than the English "peace." The threefold repetition ("shalom, shalom") mimics the false prophets' incantatory reassurances. THEOLOGICAL: The hollow proclamation of shalom without covenant faithfulness is a recurring prophetic target (Mic 3:5; Ezek 13:10), directly anticipating the false peace proclaimed before the fall of Jerusalem. TYPOLOGY: Paul echoes this theme in 1 Thess 5:3, where "peace and security" precedes sudden destruction.

verses.flow: Were they ashamed when they committed abomination? No, they were not at all ashamed, nor did they know how to blush. Therefore they shall fall among those who fall; at the time I visit them they shall stumble, says YHWH.

verses.flow: "I will utterly consume them," declares YHWH. "There are no grapes on the vine, nor figs on the fig tree; even the leaf is withered, and what I have given them has passed away from them."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The imagery of a barren vine and fig tree without fruit is a consistent biblical symbol of covenant failure and divine judgment. Jesus' cursing of the fig tree (Mark 11:12-14, 20-21) draws upon this prophetic tradition, enacting judgment on unfruitful Israel. (Hos 9:10; Luke 13:6-9)

verses.flow: "For behold, I am sending among you serpents, vipers which cannot be charmed, and they shall bite you," declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of serpents that "cannot be charmed" signifies the absolute and inescapable nature of the coming judgment — no human wisdom or ritual technique will deflect it. HISTORICAL: Snake charming was a recognized practice in the ancient Near East; the image would have resonated as indicating an unprecedented and unstoppable threat. (Ps 58:4-5)

verses.flow: My grief (magonah) is beyond healing; my heart is sick within me.

verses.flow: Behold, the sound of the cry of the daughter of my people from a distant land: "Is YHWH not in Zion? Is her King not in her?" "Why have they provoked me to anger with their carved idols and with their foreign vanities?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question "Is YHWH not in Zion?" captures the profound crisis of the exile: the people had presumed upon YHWH's unconditional presence in the Temple (Jer 7:4). YHWH's counter-question regarding their idolatry answers why His presence has withdrawn. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The term "vanities" (hevelim) for foreign gods reflects the Deuteronomic polemic against the territorial deities assigned to the nations (Deut 32:21), who are "no-gods." (Jer 7:4; Ps 82:1)

verses.flow: "The harvest is past, the summer is ended, and we are not saved."

verses.flow: For the wound of the daughter of my people am I wounded; I am in mourning; horror has seized me.
Is there no balm (tsori) in Gilead? Is there no physician there? Why then has the health of the daughter of my people not been restored?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "balm of Gilead" (tsori, Strong's H6875) was a renowned medicinal resin traded throughout the ancient Near East (Gen 37:25). The rhetorical lament — that a known remedy exists yet the patient is unhealed — points to willful refusal of covenant restoration. THEOLOGICAL: This verse stands as one of the most poignant expressions of prophetic grief in Scripture. The "Great Physician" motif finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ, who provides what no earthly balm can — full atonement and healing of the wound of sin. (Matt 9:12; Rev 22:2)

verses.flow: Oh that my head were waters, and my eye a fountain of tears, that I might weep day and night for the slain of the daughter of my people!
verses.note: HEBREW: The chapter opens with what is often called the "weeping prophet's" lament. "Daughter of my people" (bat-ammi, H1323+H5971) is a personification of the nation as a beloved daughter, intensifying the pathos. The wish for a wilderness lodging (v.2) reflects total despair — Jeremiah would rather abandon the covenant community than witness its corruption.

verses.flow: Oh that I had in the wilderness a lodging place of wayfarers, that I might leave my people and go away from them! For they are all adulterers, an assembly of treacherous ones.
And they bend their tongue as their bow — falsehood and not faithfulness (emunah) has prevailed in the land; for from evil to evil they proceed, and YHWH they do not know, declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Bend their tongue as their bow" is a vivid metaphor — the tongue is weaponized. "Emunah" (H530) denotes faithfulness, reliability, and covenant loyalty, not merely intellectual belief. The phrase "they do not know YHWH" (lo-yada' et-YHWH) is covenantal in force — yada' (H3045) implies intimate relational knowledge, not mere cognition. (Hos 4:1)

verses.flow: Let each man beware of his neighbor, and put no trust in any brother; for every brother supplants ('aqov ya'aqov), and every neighbor goes about as a slanderer.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Supplants" renders the wordplay on the name Jacob (Ya'aqov, H3290) — 'aqov ya'aqov means literally "heel-supplants heel," recalling Jacob's deceptive nature (Gen 27:36). The society has become universally Jacobian in the worst sense — treachery has become the norm among brothers. (Gen 25:26)

verses.flow: And each man deceives his neighbor, and truth they do not speak; they have trained their tongue to speak falsehood; they weary themselves to do iniquity.

verses.flow: Your dwelling is in the midst of deceit; through deceit they refuse to know Me, declares YHWH.
Therefore thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, I will refine them and test them — for what else can I do because of the daughter of my people?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The refining metaphor (tzaraf, H6884 — to smelt/refine metal) presents divine judgment as purification rather than mere punishment. YHWH is portrayed as a refiner who tests through affliction to produce faithfulness. This imagery runs throughout the prophets and into the NT. (Mal 3:3, 1 Pet 1:7)

verses.flow: Their tongue is a deadly arrow; it speaks deceit. With his mouth each speaks peace (shalom) to his neighbor, but inwardly he sets his ambush.

verses.flow: Over the mountains I will take up weeping and wailing, and over the pastures of the wilderness a lamentation (qinah), for they are laid waste so that no man passes through, and the lowing of cattle is not heard; both the birds of the heavens and the beasts have fled and are gone.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Lamentation" renders qinah (H7015), the technical term for the Hebrew dirge meter (3+2 stress pattern) characteristic of the book of Lamentations. Jeremiah employs this form here deliberately. The desolation of both land and animals recalls the reversal of creation order — judgment undoes shalom.

verses.flow: And YHWH says: Because they have forsaken My law (Torah) which I set before them, and have not listened to My voice nor walked in it,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The indictment pivots on abandonment of Torah (H8451 — instruction, law). This is covenantal breach language drawn from Deuteronomy's curse structure (Deut 28-29). YHWH's response in scattering (v.16) directly fulfills the Deuteronomic curses for covenant infidelity. (Deut 29:24-28)

that they may make haste and take up a wailing over us, that our eyes may run down with tears and our eyelids gush with water.

verses.flow: For a voice of wailing is heard from Zion: How we are ruined! We are greatly ashamed, because we have left the land, because our dwellings have cast us out.
Yet hear the word of YHWH, O you women, and let your ear receive the word of His mouth; and teach your daughters a wailing, and each her neighbor a lamentation.

verses.flow: For death has come up through our windows; it has entered our palaces — to cut off the children from the street and the young men from the public squares.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Death has come up through our windows" may reflect a Canaanite mythological motif — the god Mot (Death) entering through windows, known from the Ugaritic Baal Cycle. Jeremiah may be invoking and subverting pagan imagery: the very death-deity his people worshipped now invades their homes. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This demythologizes death as a divine power subordinate to YHWH's judgment.

verses.flow: Speak — thus declares YHWH: The corpses of men shall fall like dung upon the open field, like grain cut by the reaper, with none to gather them.
Thus says YHWH: Let not the wise man boast in his wisdom, and let not the mighty man boast in his might; let not the rich man boast in his riches;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse and v.24 stand as one of the OT's most celebrated wisdom-critiques. The triad of wisdom, might, and riches represents the totality of human self-sufficiency. Their negation is answered by knowing YHWH — yada' (relational, covenantal knowledge). Paul quotes this passage in 1 Cor 1:31 and 2 Cor 10:17 in the context of the cross subverting human wisdom. (1 Cor 1:31)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: but let him who boasts boast in this — that he understands and knows Me, that I am YHWH who exercises lovingkindness (hesed), justice (mishpat), and righteousness (tzedaqah) in the earth. For in these things I delight, declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: Three key terms define YHWH's character: hesed (H2617 — covenant lovingkindness, steadfast love), mishpat (H4941 — justice, right judgment), tzedaqah (H6666 — righteousness, rightness of moral order). These three together summarize the covenantal ethics YHWH enacts "in the earth" — not merely in heaven. TYPOLOGY: Christ embodies all three perfectly as the one in whom hesed, mishpat, and tzedaqah meet at the cross. (Mic 6:8, Rom 3:26)

verses.flow: Behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will visit upon all the circumcised who are yet uncircumcised —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Uncircumcised who are yet uncircumcised" is a deliberately paradoxical phrase — Judah bears the physical sign but lacks the heart reality. The prophets consistently prioritize circumcision of heart (Deut 10:16, Jer 4:4) over the external rite. Paul draws directly on this prophetic tradition in Romans 2:28-29. The list of nations in v.26 who practice physical circumcision (Edomites, Ammonites, Arab tribes) shows the sign alone saves no one. (Rom 2:28-29, Deut 10:16)

verses.flow: Hear the word that YHWH speaks to you, O house of Israel.
Thus says YHWH: "To the way of the nations do not learn, and at the signs of the heavens be not dismayed, for the nations are dismayed at them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's prohibition against following the "way of the nations" (derek haggoyim) reflects the covenantal distinctiveness of Israel. The "signs of the heavens" likely refers to astral divination, a widespread ANE practice. Deut 4:19 warns Israel not to worship heavenly bodies assigned to other nations, linking this passage to divine council theology. (Deut 4:19, Isa 47:13)

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For the customs (chuqqot) of the peoples — they are vanity (hebel): for wood one cuts from the forest, the work of the hands of the craftsman with the axe.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word chuqqot (Strong's H2708) is the plural of choq, meaning "statute" or "prescribed custom." Its use here sarcastically applies covenantal language to pagan practice. Hebel (H1892), meaning "breath" or "vapor," is the same word used repeatedly in Ecclesiastes for vanity or futility. The idol is described as the product of human craft, not divine origin.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: With silver and with gold they beautify it; with nails and with hammers they fasten it, and it does not totter.
They are like a pillar of cucumber-field — they cannot speak; they must indeed be carried, for they cannot walk. Be not afraid of them, for they cannot do evil, neither is it in them to do good."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The polemic against idols reaches its sharpest edge here — they cannot speak, walk, do evil, or do good. This anticipates Psalm 115:4-8 and Isaiah 44:9-20. The phrase "like a pillar of a cucumber-field" (tomer miqshah) depicts a scarecrow-like figure, utterly passive and powerless. The irony is that the nations fear (yare') these objects while Israel is commanded not to fear them.

verses.flow: There is none like you, O YHWH; you are great, and great is your name in might (gevurah).

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Who would not fear you, O King of the nations? For to you it is fitting; for among all the wise ones of the nations and among all their kingdoms there is none like you.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: But they are altogether brutish and foolish; the instruction of idols — it is but wood!
Beaten silver is brought from Tarshish, and gold from Uphaz, the work of the craftsman and of the hands of the goldsmith; violet and purple is their clothing — all of them the work of skilled men.
But YHWH is the true (emet) God (Elohim); he is the living God (Elohim Chayyim) and the everlasting King. At his wrath the earth quakes, and the nations cannot endure his indignation.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 10 stands as the theological climax of the chapter's idol polemic. YHWH is called Elohim emet ("God of truth/reality"), distinguishing him ontologically from false gods. "Living God" (Elohim Chayyim) and "everlasting King" (Melekh Olam) assert his eternal sovereignty over all creation and history. This verse has no Aramaic parallel in some LXX traditions, suggesting it may have been a later MT expansion. (Dan 6:26, Matt 16:16, Rev 15:3)

verses.flow: Thus shall you say to them: "The gods who did not make the heavens and the earth — they shall perish from the earth and from under these heavens."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Verse 11 is one of only two passages in the book of Jeremiah written in Aramaic (the other is none; this is unique in Jeremiah), suggesting it functioned as a ready-made refutation of idolatry for use in international or diaspora contexts. The Aramaic phrase "elahayya di-shemayya" mirrors the language of the exile community. Its declaration that these gods "shall perish" echoes Psalm 82:7 where the divine council members are judged to die like men. (Ps 82:7, Dan 2:11)

verses.flow: He who made the earth by his power (koach), who established the world by his wisdom (chokhmah), and by his understanding (tevunah) stretched out the heavens —
verses.note: HEBREW: The triad of divine attributes — koach (power, H3581), chokhmah (wisdom, H2451), and tevunah (understanding, H8394) — appears together also in Prov 3:19-20. These are the same attributes by which Wisdom in the wisdom tradition mediates creation. The participle form of the verbs ("the one who made...who established...who stretched out") presents YHWH's creative acts as ongoing, defining characteristics rather than mere past events. (Prov 3:19, Ps 104:2)

verses.flow: when he utters his voice, there is a tumult of waters in the heavens, and he causes the mist to ascend from the ends of the earth; lightnings for the rain he makes, and he brings out the wind from his treasuries.
Every man is brutish and without knowledge; every goldsmith is put to shame by his idol, for his molten image is a lie (sheqer), and there is no breath (ruach) in them.
verses.note: HEBREW: Ruach (H7307), here translated "breath," can also mean "spirit" or "wind." Its absence in idols is damning: they possess none of the animating divine breath that YHWH breathed into Adam (Gen 2:7). The word sheqer (H8267, "lie" or "falsehood") is a strong moral condemnation — idolatry is not merely futile but deceitful, a false representation of reality. (Gen 2:7, Isa 44:20)

verses.flow: They are vanity (hebel), a work of delusion; in the time of their visitation (pekuddah) they shall perish.
Not like these is the Portion (chelev) of Jacob, for he is the one who formed all things, and Israel is the tribe of his inheritance — YHWH of hosts (Tzva'ot) is his name.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "Portion of Jacob" (chelev Ya'akov) identifies YHWH himself as Israel's inheritance in contrast to the territorial gods of the nations. This language reflects the Deuteronomy 32:8-9 worldview where YHWH assigned the nations to the sons of God but kept Israel as his own allotment. The title "YHWH Tzva'ot" (YHWH of Hosts/Armies) evokes his leadership over the heavenly host. (Deut 32:8-9, Ps 82:1, Ps 16:5)

verses.flow: Gather up your bundle from the ground, O you who dwell under siege!

verses.flow: I know, O YHWH, that the way of a man is not in himself, that it is not in a man who walks to direct his steps.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This confession of human moral inability — "the way of a man is not in himself" — is one of the most profound anthropological statements in the Old Testament. It acknowledges that self-direction in the moral and spiritual realm is beyond human capacity, anticipating New Testament teachings on human will and divine sovereignty. Jeremiah speaks as a representative voice for the nation in its exile. (Prov 16:9, Rom 9:16)

verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Correct me, O YHWH, but with justice (mishpat); not in your anger, lest you make me few.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's petition for correction "with justice (mishpat), not in anger" reflects the covenant lawsuit framework: he does not ask to escape discipline but to receive it in measured proportion. The fear that divine anger would "make him few" alludes to Israel's reduction through exile and judgment. This prayer models intercessory submission within the covenant relationship. (Lam 3:39-40, Heb 12:10)

verses.flow: Pour out your wrath upon the nations that do not know you, and upon the peoples that call not on your name; for they have devoured Jacob — yes, they have devoured him and consumed him, and have made his habitation desolate.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This closing imprecation, directed against the devouring nations, is quoted nearly verbatim in Psalm 79:6-7, suggesting a shared liturgical tradition or direct literary dependence. The prayer for divine wrath against those who "call not on your name" anticipates the eschatological judgment of the nations in prophetic and apocalyptic literature. The image of "devouring Jacob" and making his habitation desolate points to Babylon's destruction of Jerusalem as a paradigm case. (Ps 79:6-7, Rev 16:1, Zeph 3:8)

verses.flow: And say to them: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Cursed is the man who does not hear the words of this covenant,
verses.note: HEBREW: The formula "Thus says YHWH" (koh amar YHWH) is the classic prophetic messenger formula, establishing Jeremiah as a covenant lawsuit (rib) herald. The curse formula here echoes Deuteronomy 27:26, grounding this passage firmly in the Sinai covenant structure. The Hebrew 'arur ("cursed") denotes a state of separation from divine blessing and protection.

verses.flow: which I commanded your fathers in the day I brought them out of the land of Egypt, from the iron furnace, saying: Hear my voice and do them, according to all that I command you; and you shall be to me a people, and I will be to you God,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The phrase "iron furnace" (kur ha-barzel) is a vivid metaphor for the intense suffering of Egyptian bondage, appearing also in Deuteronomy 4:20 and 1 Kings 8:51. The covenant formula "you shall be to me a people, and I will be to you God" is the classic bilateral covenant declaration (the berith relationship) binding Israel to YHWH as his treasured possession (segullah).

verses.flow: in order that I may establish the oath which I swore to your fathers, to give to them a land flowing with milk and honey, as at this day." And I answered and said: "Amen, YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's "Amen" (Hebrew: amen, meaning "so be it" or "truly") is a rare prophetic assent to the Sinai covenant demands, underscoring his own personal identification with the covenant. The oath sworn to the patriarchs — the land flowing with milk and honey — shows the Abrahamic covenant undergirds the Sinaitic, covenantal blessing being conditioned on obedience.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to me: "A conspiracy has been found among the men of Judah and among the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Hebrew qesher ("conspiracy" or "binding plot") implies a deliberate, coordinated rebellion against YHWH's covenant. This is not mere negligence but willful apostasy, heightening the gravity of Judah's sin. Covenant breaking in the ancient Near East was tantamount to treason against the suzerain, with corresponding capital consequences.

verses.flow: They have turned back to the iniquities of their forefathers, who refused to hear my words; and they went after other gods to serve them. The house of Israel and the house of Judah have broken my covenant which I made with their fathers."

verses.flow: Therefore thus says YHWH: "Behold, I am bringing evil upon them which they shall not be able to escape; and they shall cry out to me, but I will not listen to them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "I will not listen to them" (lo eshma alehem) represents a stark reversal of the covenant relationship; YHWH, who called Israel to hear (shema), now will not hear their cry. This is a judicial consequence of covenantal abandonment — the same divine ear once open to covenant prayer is now closed under divine judgment. Compare the similar declaration in Micah 3:4.

verses.flow: And the cities of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem shall go and cry out to the gods to whom they make offerings — but they will not save them at all in the time of their evil.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The reference to gods (elohim) to whom Judah makes offerings is consistent with the Hebrew Bible's acknowledgment of the existence of other supernatural beings in YHWH's cosmos, here depicted as utterly powerless to save. These entities, recognized in passages like Psalm 82 and Deuteronomy 32:8-9 as members of the divine council, are shown to be impotent in the face of coming judgment, delegitimizing their worship entirely.

verses.flow: Therefore do not pray for this people, or lift up a cry or prayer on their behalf; for I will not hear them in the time they cry out to me in their time of evil.
What right does my beloved have in my house, while she carries out her wicked schemes? Can vows and holy flesh avert your evil from upon you? Surely then you would rejoice."
verses.note: HEBREW: This verse is notoriously difficult in the MT; the Hebrew is terse and possibly corrupt at points. "My beloved" (yedidati) is a term of intimate endearment used of Israel as YHWH's covenant partner, carrying overtones of the marriage metaphor prominent throughout the prophets. "Holy flesh" (basar ha-qodesh) refers to sacrificial meat, suggesting Judah thought cultic ritual alone could secure divine favor — a form of magical thinking rejected by the prophets.

verses.flow: And YHWH made it known to me and I knew; then you showed me their deeds.
