verses.flow: But I was like a gentle lamb led to the slaughter; and I did not know it was against me that they devised schemes, saying: "Let us destroy the tree with its fruit, and let us cut him off from the land of the living, that his name be remembered no more."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse is widely recognized as one of the most direct messianic prefigurations in Jeremiah — the "Servant of YHWH" typology. The image of a "gentle lamb led to the slaughter" (seh aluph yuval la-tevach) anticipates Isaiah 53:7 and finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus the Messiah, who was likewise betrayed by those close to him and killed unjustly. The men of Anathoth's plot against Jeremiah foreshadows the Sanhedrin's conspiracy against Christ (Matt 26:3-4). (Isa 53:7; John 1:29; Acts 8:32)

verses.flow: But YHWH of hosts judges with righteousness (tsedek), tests the heart and the mind (kelayot); let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you I have committed my cause.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "tests the heart and the mind" literally reads "tests the kidneys and the heart" (bochen kelayot va-lev). In Hebrew anthropology, the kelayot (kidneys, rendered "mind" or "inward parts") were considered the seat of deepest emotion and moral conscience, while the lev (heart) was the seat of will and intellect. Together they represent the totality of the inner person known only to YHWH. (Ps 7:9; Rev 2:23)

verses.flow: "Therefore thus says YHWH concerning the men of Anathoth, who seek your life, saying: 'Do not prophesy in the name of YHWH, or you will die by our hand' —
therefore thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, I will punish them; the young men shall die by the sword, their sons and their daughters shall die by famine,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The judgment against the men of Anathoth — sword for young men, famine for children — employs the classic covenant curse formulary of Deuteronomy 28, demonstrating that YHWH's judgment is not arbitrary but covenantally structured. The precision of the punishment (targeting the very community that sought to silence YHWH's prophet) illustrates the lex talionis principle operating within covenant faithfulness.

verses.flow: and there shall be no remnant left of them; for I will bring evil upon the men of Anathoth in the year of their punishment."
Righteous art thou, O YHWH, when I contend with thee; yet let me speak judgments (mishpatim) with thee: Why does the way of the wicked prosper? Why are all those who deal treacherously at ease?
verses.note: HEBREW: The opening word "righteous" (tsaddiq, H6662) establishes the tension: Jeremiah affirms YHWH's justice even as he challenges it — a form of covenant lawsuit (rib, H7378). The term "mishpatim" (judgments/legal decisions) frames the entire complaint as a formal legal dispute before the divine court, reflecting ancient Near Eastern covenant litigation patterns. THEOLOGICAL: This is the second of Jeremiah's "confessions" (cf. Jer 11:18-23; 15:10-21), a uniquely intimate genre of prophetic lament that anticipates the psalms of individual complaint. The prophet's willingness to argue with YHWH stands in the tradition of Abraham (Gen 18) and Moses (Exod 32).

words.m: verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Thou hast planted them, and they have taken root; they grow, they have even brought forth fruit. Thou art near in their mouth, but far from their inward parts (kilyotam).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Kilyotam" (their kidneys/inward parts, H3629) refers to the seat of deepest thought and emotion in Hebrew anthropology, roughly equivalent to "heart" in modern usage. The contrast between lip-confession and inner reality is a recurring prophetic critique (cf. Isa 29:13). THEOLOGICAL: This verse anticipates the New Covenant promise of Jer 31:33, where the law will be written on the heart (lev), resolving the very problem Jeremiah identifies here — outward religion without inward transformation.

verses.flow: But thou, O YHWH, knowest me; thou seest me, and thou hast tried my heart toward thee. Pull them out like sheep for the slaughter, and set them apart for the day of killing.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The imagery of sheep set apart for slaughter echoes sacrificial language and anticipates the Suffering Servant of Isa 53:7, who "as a sheep before its shearers is silent." Ironically, Jeremiah who prays for his enemies' slaughter becomes himself the type of the innocent sufferer. THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's imprecatory prayer reflects the raw honesty of covenant prayer; it does not contradict the NT call to love enemies but rather demonstrates the legitimate cry for divine justice that underlies the Cross itself.

verses.flow: How long shall the land mourn, and the grass of every field wither? From the evil of those who dwell in it, the beasts and the birds have been swept away, because they have said, "He will not see our latter end."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The mourning of the land and withering of vegetation reflects a covenantal curse pattern established in Deuteronomy 28-29, where disobedience brings agricultural devastation. The phrase "beasts and birds swept away" echoes Hos 4:3, suggesting a shared prophetic tradition of creation-mourning under divine judgment. THEOLOGICAL: The land itself (eretz) is a covenant participant in Hebrew theology — it responds to sin and obedience, a concept alien to modern Western thought but foundational to the Mosaic covenant framework.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: If you have run with footmen and they have wearied you, how will you contend with horses? And if in a land of peace (shalom) you trust, how will you do in the swelling of the Jordan?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is YHWH's direct response to Jeremiah's complaint, and notably it does not answer the theodicy question but instead calls the prophet to greater endurance. The rhetorical questions intensify in scale: footmen to horses, a land of peace to the flooding Jordan. HISTORICAL: "The swelling (pride) of the Jordan" (ge'on ha-Yarden) refers to the dense jungle thicket lining the Jordan River, a dangerous wilderness zone inhabited by lions (cf. Jer 49:19), used metaphorically for the intensifying trials ahead — the Babylonian invasion.

words.g: and good in the widest sense

verses.flow: For even your brothers and the house of your father, even they have dealt treacherously with you; even they have called a full company after you. Do not believe them, though they speak smooth words to you.

verses.flow: I have forsaken my house; I have abandoned my inheritance (nachalati); I have given the beloved of my soul into the hand of her enemies.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nachalati" (my inheritance, H5159) is a covenant term describing the land and people YHWH claimed as his special possession (cf. Deut 32:9). The threefold confession — "forsaken," "abandoned," "given over" — represents a divine lament of devastating emotional force. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH here speaks as one betrayed by his beloved, using the language of familial abandonment. "Beloved of my soul" (yedidut nafshi) is uniquely tender language; YHWH grieves the judgment he must enact, a pathos that becomes fully incarnate in Christ weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44).

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: My inheritance has become to me like a lion in the forest; she has lifted up her voice against me; therefore I have hated her.

verses.flow: Is my inheritance to me like a speckled bird of prey? Are the birds of prey all around against her? Go, gather all the beasts of the field; bring them to devour.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Some interpreters read "speckled bird of prey" (ayit tsavua) as a reference to Israel's anomalous status among the nations, attracting hostile supernatural and political attention. The call to "gather all the beasts of the field" may reflect the divine council's mobilization of hostile nations as instruments of judgment, a pattern seen in Deut 32:8 where nations are assigned to "sons of God." HISTORICAL: The image of birds of prey circling a carcass was a common ANE war oracle, signifying total defeat; similar imagery appears in Assyrian and Babylonian omen texts.

verses.flow: Many shepherds have destroyed my vineyard; they have trampled my portion; they have made my pleasant portion (chelkat chemdati) into a desolate wilderness.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of YHWH's "vineyard" (kerem) destroyed by unfaithful shepherds directly anticipates the Parable of the Wicked Tenants in Matt 21:33-45, where Jesus identifies himself as the heir sent to reclaim what belongs to YHWH. HEBREW: "Chelkat chemdati" (my pleasant/delightful portion) combines chelqah (H2513, an allotted plot of land) with chemdah (H2532, delight/desire), conveying an intensely personal sense of ownership — this is YHWH's cherished estate, not merely real estate.

verses.flow: They have made it a desolation; it mourns before me, desolate. The whole land is made desolate, because no man lays it to heart.
Upon all the bare heights in the wilderness the destroyers have come, for the sword of YHWH devours from one end of the land to the other end of the land; there is no peace (shalom) for all flesh.

verses.flow: They have sown wheat and reaped thorns; they have exerted themselves to no profit. And they shall be ashamed of their harvests because of the fierce anger (charon aph) of YHWH.

Thus says YHWH concerning all my evil neighbors who touch the inheritance that I have given to my people Israel to inherit: Behold, I will pluck them up from their land, and I will pluck up the house of Judah from their midst.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's judgment extends beyond Israel to the surrounding nations — a startling universalization of covenant consequence. The nations that profaned Israel's inheritance are themselves subject to YHWH's sovereign plucking (natasht, H5428), the same verb used for uprooting Israel. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In the framework of Deut 32:8-9, nations had divine "sons of God" as their overseers; the judgment of these nations implies judgment also on those spiritual authorities who misled them, a theme developed in Ps 82 and Isa 24:21-22.

verses.flow: And it shall be, after I have plucked them up, I will again have compassion (rachamim) on them, and I will bring them back, each man to his inheritance and each man to his land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The promise of restoration after judgment follows the classic covenantal pattern of Deut 30:1-10. Crucially, YHWH's compassion (rachamim, from rechem meaning womb — a maternal tenderness) extends even to the pagan neighbors, not just Israel, anticipating the universal scope of NT salvation. TYPOLOGY: The phrase "I will again have compassion" and the restoration of each to his own inheritance foreshadows the eschatological regathering of all peoples under Messiah (Eph 1:10; Rev 21:24-26), when the nations' inheritance is restored under the Son's direct rule.

verses.flow: And it shall be, if they will indeed learn the ways of my people, to swear by my name, "As YHWH lives," even as they taught my people to swear by Baal, then they shall be built up in the midst of my people.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The condition set before the nations — learning Israel's ways and swearing "as YHWH lives" — represents nothing less than conversion and covenant incorporation. Former enemies who worshipped Baal may be "built up in the midst of my people," a remarkable early statement of Gentile inclusion in the covenant community. TYPOLOGY: This verse anticipates Paul's argument in Romans 11 and the grafting of Gentiles into the olive tree of Israel; the nations' swearing by YHWH's name prefigures the NT confession of Jesus as Lord (Phil 2:10-11), through whom all nations are invited into covenant relationship.

verses.flow: Thus said YHWH to me: "Go and acquire for yourself a linen loincloth (ezor), and put it around your waist, and do not put it in water."
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew term ezor (Strong's H232) denotes a waist-cloth or loincloth of linen, the same material used for priestly garments (Lev 16:4). The command not to put it in water is significant: it remains ceremonially unsoiled yet still subject to corruption, pointing to Israel's consecrated status now turned to ruin. TYPOLOGY: The linen garment evokes priestly and royal imagery, anticipating the people's calling to be a kingdom of priests (Exod 19:6) now forfeited.

verses.flow: "Take the loincloth that you have acquired, which is around your waist, and arise, go to the Euphrates and hide it there in the cleft of the rock."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Hebrew text uses "Perat" (פְּרָת), the Euphrates River, approximately 400 miles from Jerusalem — an enormous journey. Some scholars propose a nearer site (Parah, Josh 18:23), but the Masoretic text consistently implies the Euphrates, making the sign-act either literal or visionary in nature, underscoring the Babylonian connection.

verses.flow: So I went to the Euphrates and dug, and I took the loincloth from the place where I had hidden it; and behold, the loincloth was ruined — it was fit for nothing.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The ruined (shachat, H7843) loincloth becomes the central emblem of the entire chapter. The verb shachat carries the sense of total corruption and destruction, the same root used for the corruption of the earth before the flood (Gen 6:11-12). Israel, once fit for intimate covenant purpose, has been rendered utterly useless through prolonged spiritual apostasy.

verses.flow: Then the word of YHWH came to me, saying,
"Thus says YHWH: Even so I will ruin the pride (ge'on) of Judah and the great pride of Jerusalem.
verses.note: HEBREW: Ge'on (H1347) denotes pride, majesty, or swelling arrogance — a word used both positively of YHWH's own majesty (Exod 15:7) and negatively of human hubris. Here its ironic use condemns Judah for the very arrogance that will be stripped away. THEOLOGICAL: The judgment mirrors the sin: a people who swelled with pride are "ruined" as the loincloth was ruined.

verses.flow: This evil people who refuse to hear my words, who walk in the stubbornness of their heart and have gone after other gods to serve them and to bow down to them — let them be like this loincloth, which is fit for nothing.

For as the loincloth clings to the waist of a man, so I caused all the house of Israel and all the house of Judah to cling to me, declares YHWH, to be for me a people and a name and a praise and a glory (tiferet); but they did not hear.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse encapsulates the covenant purpose of Israel — to cling (davaq, H1692) to YHWH as a loincloth clings to the body, an image of intimate, inseparable union. The covenant formula "a people and a name and a praise and a glory" (cf. Deut 26:19) is recapitulated here, magnifying the tragedy of Israel's failure to hear. TYPOLOGY: This clinging language anticipates the New Covenant (Jer 31:31-34) wherein the law is written on the heart, ensuring a permanent attachment.

verses.flow: And I will dash them one against another, even fathers and sons together, declares YHWH. I will not pity nor spare nor have compassion (racham), so as to keep me from destroying them.
verses.note: HEBREW: Racham (H7355), rendered "compassion," derives from the root rechem meaning "womb," conveying the deep, mother-like tenderness that YHWH ordinarily extends to his people. Its negation here is among the most severe statements of divine judgment in Jeremiah — compare Hosea 1:6 (Lo-Ruhamah). THEOLOGICAL: The triple negation — no pity, no sparing, no compassion — echoes the covenant curse language of Deuteronomy 28.

verses.flow: Give glory to YHWH your God before he brings darkness, and before your feet stumble on the twilight mountains, and while you look for light he turns it to deep shadow (tzalmavet) and makes it deep gloom.
verses.note: HEBREW: Tzalmavet (H6757), traditionally rendered "shadow of death," is a compound of tzal (shadow) and mavet (death). It occurs famously in Psalm 23:4. Here it describes the catastrophic reversal of light to darkness as divine judgment falls. THEOLOGICAL: The call to "give glory to YHWH" before darkness falls is a final window of repentance — one of several such appeals in Jeremiah that go unanswered.

verses.flow: But if you will not hear it, my soul will weep in secret places because of your pride; and my eye will weep bitterly and run down with tears, because the flock of YHWH has been taken captive.
Say to the king and the queen mother (gevirah): "Humble yourselves, sit down; for your crowns have come down — the crown of your splendor."
verses.note: HEBREW: Gevirah (H1377), "queen mother," is a technical royal title in Judah's monarchy, denoting not the king's wife but his mother, who held official status and a seat of authority (cf. 1 Kgs 15:13; 2 Kgs 24:12). This likely refers to Nehushta, mother of Jehoiachin (2 Kgs 24:8). HISTORICAL: The specific mention of the gevirah alongside the king reflects the ancient Near Eastern institution of the queen mother as a formal court position with real political influence.

verses.flow: Can the Cushite change his skin, or the leopard his spots? Then also you can do good who are accustomed to do evil.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse constitutes one of the most explicit statements of moral inability in the Old Testament prophetic corpus. The rhetorical questions — implying "no" — assert that Israel's habituated evil has rendered moral transformation humanly impossible. TYPOLOGY: Paul's argument in Romans 8:7-8 that the natural mind cannot submit to God's law resonates deeply with this text. The impossibility of self-reformation points forward to the need for the new heart of Ezekiel 36:26 and the New Covenant.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: "And I will scatter them like stubble passing away before the wind of the wilderness.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: This is your lot, the portion measured out to you from me, declares YHWH, because you have forgotten me and trusted in falsehood (sheqer).
verses.note: HEBREW: Sheqer (H8267), "falsehood" or "lie," is used throughout Jeremiah to describe idols (cf. Jer 10:14; 16:19), false prophets (Jer 14:14), and the general spiritual deception into which Judah has fallen. Trusting in sheqer is the antithesis of trusting in YHWH, who is the God of truth (emet). THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "portion measured out" (manah, H4490) employs inheritance language — the lot Israel chose by abandoning YHWH becomes their permanent inheritance: destruction.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: I myself will lift up your skirts over your face, and your shame will be seen —

your adulteries and your neighings, the lewdness of your prostitution, on the hills, in the field. I have seen your abominations. Woe to you, O Jerusalem! How long will it yet be before you are made clean?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The final lament — "How long will it yet be before you are made clean?" — preserves a remaining ember of redemptive hope even in the harshest judgment oracle. The question is not rhetorical despair but a genuine prophetic longing for restoration. TYPOLOGY: The cleansing language anticipates Ezekiel's promise of sprinkling with clean water (Ezek 36:25) and ultimately the purification wrought by Messiah's atoning work (Heb 10:22), the only answer to the moral impossibility declared in v. 23.

verses.flow: The word of YHWH that came to Jeremiah concerning the matters of the drought (batstsaroth):
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "batstsaroth" (H1226) derives from the root batsar, meaning to cut off or restrain, here describing a severe drought brought by divine withholding of rain — a covenantal curse explicitly threatened in Deut 28:23-24. The plural form may indicate successive or multiplied drought events. This superscription functions as a title for the larger literary unit spanning chapters 14-15.

verses.flow: Judah mourns, and her gates languish; they are dark (qadar) upon the ground, and the cry of Jerusalem goes up.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "qadar" (H6937) means to be dark or to go into mourning, often used of darkened skies or mourning garments. Jerusalem's "gates" represent her civic and commercial life; their languishing signals total societal collapse. The imagery of darkening and mourning personifies the city as a grieving woman, a common prophetic motif.

verses.flow: And their nobles send their young ones for water; they come to the cisterns, they find no water; they return with their vessels empty; they are ashamed and confounded and cover their heads.
On account of the ground that is cracked, because no rain has fallen on the land, the farmers are ashamed; they cover their heads.
Even the doe in the field forsakes her newborn fawn because there is no grass.

verses.flow: And the wild donkeys stand on the bare heights; they pant for air like jackals; their eyes fail because there is no vegetation.
Though our iniquities testify against us, O YHWH, act for the sake of Your name; for our backslidings (meshuboth) are many; against You we have sinned.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's intercessory lament pivots on the phrase "act for the sake of Your name" — a profound appeal not to human merit but to YHWH's own covenantal reputation (shem, H8034). This pattern of appeal recurs throughout Scripture (Ps 79:9; Ezek 36:22) and becomes the basis of all grace-based petition. The confession of "meshuboth" (backsliding, H4878, from shub, to turn) acknowledges their cyclical apostasy.

verses.flow: O Hope of Israel, its Savior in time of distress, why should You be like a stranger in the land, like a traveler who turns aside to lodge for a night?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The title "Hope (miqveh, H4723) of Israel" occurs only in Jeremiah (17:13; 50:7) and carries the dual sense of hope and gathering pool of water — an especially poignant image in a drought context. The complaint that YHWH acts as a mere "stranger" (ger, H1616) inverts the covenantal relationship; Israel was the alien welcomed by YHWH, not the other way around.

verses.flow: Why should You be like a man bewildered, like a mighty warrior who cannot save? Yet You, O YHWH, are in our midst, and Your name is called over us; do not forsake us.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH concerning this people: "Even so they have loved to wander; they have not restrained their feet; and YHWH does not accept them. Now He will remember their iniquity and punish their sins."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's refusal to accept the people despite their lament reflects the principle that repentance must be genuine and sustained, not merely crisis-driven. The phrase "YHWH does not accept them" uses the verb ratsah (H7521), the same word for divine pleasure in acceptable sacrifice, here negated. This verse sets up the divine prohibition on intercession in v. 11.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to me: "Do not pray for the welfare (tov) of this people.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prohibition against intercession for this people (cf. 7:16; 11:14; 15:1) is one of the most severe divine verdicts in the prophetic literature. It signals that the point of no return has been reached in YHWH's patience with Judah. Even Moses and Samuel — paradigmatic intercessors — are named in 15:1 as ineffective if they were to stand before YHWH for this people.

verses.flow: Though they fast, I will not hear their cry; and though they offer burnt offering (olah) and grain offering (minchah), I will not accept them. But by the sword, by the famine, and by the pestilence I will consume them."
verses.note: HEBREW: The "olah" (H5930, burnt offering) and "minchah" (H4503, grain offering) represent the full range of cultic worship — both burnt sacrifices and agricultural offerings. Their rejection demonstrates that ritual without covenantal fidelity is worthless before YHWH (cf. Isa 1:11-15; Amos 5:21-24). The triad of "sword, famine, and pestilence" becomes a recurring formula of judgment throughout Jeremiah.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to me: "The prophets are prophesying falsehood (sheqer) in My name; I did not send them, nor did I command them, nor did I speak to them. A false vision and divination and worthlessness and the deceit of their own heart they are prophesying to you.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sheqer" (H8267) means falsehood, lie, or deception, and is the dominant word for false prophecy in Jeremiah (occurring over 30 times in the book). THEOLOGICAL: The criteria for false prophecy here are threefold: unauthorized sending, absence of divine command, and absence of divine speech. This passage is foundational for distinguishing true from false prophetic office — the true prophet speaks only what YHWH commands and is authenticated by divine commission (cf. Deut 18:20-22).

verses.flow: And the people to whom they are prophesying shall be thrown out into the streets of Jerusalem, victims of famine and sword, with none to bury them — them, their wives, their sons, and their daughters. For I will pour out their evil upon them.

verses.flow: And you shall say to them this word: 'Let my eyes run down with tears night and day, and let them not cease, for the virgin daughter of my people is shattered with a great breach, with a very grievous blow.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jeremiah's weeping over the "virgin daughter of my people" anticipates the weeping of Christ over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44; Matt 23:37). The prophet as suffering intercessor who shares the grief of divine judgment upon his own people is a powerful type of the Messiah, who also bore the sorrow of national rejection and impending destruction.

verses.flow: Have You utterly rejected Judah? Does Your soul loathe Zion? Why have You struck us down so that there is no healing for us? We look for peace (shalom), and there is no good; for a time of healing, and behold, terror.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical questions "Have You utterly rejected Judah?" and "Does Your soul loathe Zion?" use the strongest Hebrew verbs of rejection — ma'as (H3988) and ga'al (H1602) — pushing against the limits of covenantal despair. The people still appeal to the covenant relationship even in extremity, demonstrating that covenant memory outlasts covenant faithfulness. This tension is only resolved in the New Covenant promise of Jer 31:31-34.

verses.flow: We acknowledge, O YHWH, our wickedness, the iniquity of our fathers, for we have sinned against You.
Do not spurn us, for Your name's sake; do not dishonor the throne of Your glory; remember, do not break Your covenant (berith) with us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The appeal "do not break Your covenant (berith, H1285) with us" is remarkable — the people acknowledge their own sin (v. 20) yet ground their plea entirely in YHWH's sworn faithfulness, not in their own merit. The "throne of Your glory" (kisse' kevodekha) refers to the Jerusalem temple as YHWH's earthly throne room, connecting covenant, temple, and divine presence. This becomes a christological theme in the NT as Jesus is the new temple and dwelling of divine glory (John 1:14; 2:19-21).

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperfect 1st common plur

verses.flow: Are there any among the false gods (hebel) of the nations that can give rain? Or can the heavens give showers? Are You not He, O YHWH our God? And we hope in You, for You are the One who does all these things.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The challenge "are there any among the false gods (hebel) of the nations that can give rain?" uses "hebel" (H1892, vanity, breath, nothingness) — the same word as in Ecclesiastes — to demote the pagan deities to cosmic irrelevance. This is a polemic against Baal, the Canaanite storm god specifically associated with rain and fertility. YHWH alone controls the heavenly storehouses of rain (Job 38:22-28), demonstrating His exclusive sovereignty over creation over against the claims of territorial deities.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to me, "Even if Moses and Samuel were to stand before me, my soul would not be toward this people. Send them out from before my face, and let them go forth."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Moses and Samuel are singled out as the supreme intercessors in Israel's history — Moses having interceded at Sinai (Exod 32:11-14) and Samuel at Mizpah (1 Sam 7:9; 12:23). Even these paradigmatic mediators could no longer avert the divine judgment, signaling that the covenant has reached a terminal point. This language anticipates the one Mediator who surpasses all (1 Tim 2:5).

verses.flow: "And it shall be, when they say to you, 'Where shall we go forth?' then you shall say to them, Thus says YHWH: Those for death, to death; and those for the sword, to the sword; and those for the famine, to the famine; and those for captivity, to captivity."
verses.note: HEBREW: The fourfold formula — death, sword, famine, captivity — mirrors the covenant curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28. The Hebrew structure is terse and categorical: "those for death, to death" (lam-mawet lam-mawet), forming a rhetorical pattern of grim finality. Compare the four horsemen imagery in Revelation 6.

verses.flow: "And I will make them a horror (za'avah) to all the kingdoms of the earth, on account of Manasseh son of Hezekiah king of Judah, for what he did in Jerusalem."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Manasseh's reign (698–643 BC) is explicitly held responsible for Judah's ultimate fall (2 Kgs 21:10-15; 23:26-27; 24:3-4). He reversed Hezekiah's reforms, instituted child sacrifice at Tophet, erected Asherah poles, and filled Jerusalem with innocent blood. The Deuteronomistic History regards his sin as the point of no return for the southern kingdom.

verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "I am weary of relenting" (nichamtī, from naham) is theologically striking — it uses the same root applied elsewhere to God's "repenting" or "relenting" (cf. Exod 32:14). Here YHWH declares that the capacity for covenant forbearance has been exhausted. The verb nichamtī carries emotional weight, implying deep grief alongside resolved judgment.

verses.flow: "Their widows are more numerous before me than the sand of the seas; I have brought against them, against the mother of a young man, a destroyer at noonday; I have caused anguish and terror to fall upon her suddenly."

verses.flow: "She who bore seven has languished; she has breathed out her soul; her sun has gone down while it was yet day; she has been ashamed and put to shame. And their remnant I will give to the sword before their enemies — the declaration of YHWH."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "She who bore seven has languished" invokes the imagery of Hannah's song (1 Sam 2:5), now inverted — where Hannah celebrated divine reversal of barrenness, here the fruitful mother is brought to desolation. The sun going down at midday echoes Amos 8:9, a sign of eschatological-type judgment reversing creation order.

verses.flow: Woe to me, my mother, that you bore me — a man of strife and a man of contention to all the earth! I have not lent, nor have men lent to me; yet all of them curse me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse opens Jeremiah's fourth "confession" (laments), in which he agonizes over his prophetic calling. His self-description as "a man of strife and contention" ironically mirrors his mission of proclaiming strife to a rebellious nation. These confessions (chapters 11–20) are unparalleled in the prophetic literature for their raw spiritual honesty and are precursors to the lament Psalms tradition.

verses.flow: You, O YHWH, you know; remember me and visit me, and take vengeance for me upon my persecutors. In your long-suffering ('erek 'appayim) do not take me away; know that for your sake I bear reproach.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Long-suffering" translates erek appayim (literally "long of nostrils/anger"), the classic phrase for divine patience found in the Sinai self-revelation (Exod 34:6). Jeremiah here inverts this attribute — he pleads that God's patience not extend to tolerating his enemies' persecution. The tension between divine patience and the prophet's cry for justice is theologically profound.

verses.flow: Your words were found, and I ate them; and your word became to me a joy and the rejoicing (sason) of my heart; for I am called by your name, O YHWH God of hosts (tseva'ot).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Your words were found, and I ate them" closely parallels Ezekiel's commission to eat the scroll (Ezek 3:1-3) and anticipates John's consumption of the little scroll (Rev 10:9-10). The ingestion of the divine word signifies total internalization of the prophetic message. The title "YHWH God of hosts (tseva'ot)" — literally "YHWH of armies" — invokes the divine council and heavenly warrior imagery.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says YHWH: "If you return, then I will restore you; before me you shall stand. And if you bring forth what is precious from what is worthless, you shall be as my mouth. They shall turn to you, but you shall not turn to them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's call for Jeremiah to "return" (shub) is remarkable — the very word used for Israel's call to repentance is here directed at the prophet himself. The condition "if you bring forth what is precious from what is worthless" defines authentic prophetic ministry: discerning and speaking only what is truly from YHWH, not mixing divine truth with human complaint. This verse is a recommissioning after Jeremiah's crisis of faith.

verses.flow: "And I will make you to this people a fortified wall of bronze; and they will fight against you, but they shall not prevail over you; for I am with you to save you and to deliver you — the declaration of YHWH."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of a "fortified bronze wall" directly echoes Jeremiah's original commissioning in 1:18, forming a literary inclusio within the first major section of the book. YHWH's promise of protection despite opposition anticipates the New Testament pattern of apostolic ministry sustained through suffering (2 Cor 4:8-9). The unconditional divine presence — "I am with you" — grounds the promise, not Jeremiah's personal strength.

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to me, saying:
"You shall not take for yourself a wife, and you shall not have sons or daughters in this place."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's celibacy is a prophetic sign-act — a lived parable of divine judgment. His refusal to marry or have children embodied the message that the coming catastrophe made normal family life futile in this place. This is unique among the writing prophets and underscores the severity of the Babylonian crisis. (Jer 25:10; Matt 19:12)

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH concerning the sons and concerning the daughters who are born in this place, and concerning their mothers who bear them, and concerning their fathers who beget them in this land:
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "in this place" (ba-makom ha-zeh) functions as a technical designation for Jerusalem and Judah throughout Jeremiah, implying a covenantal specificity of judgment upon the chosen land. The oracle extends to the whole generation, not just Jeremiah personally.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: "Deaths of sicknesses they shall die; they shall not be mourned, and they shall not be buried; they shall be as dung upon the face of the ground. And by sword and by famine they shall be consumed, and their corpse shall be food for the birds of the heavens and for the beasts of the earth."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Dying without proper burial and being left as carrion was considered a catastrophic disgrace in ancient Near Eastern culture, equivalent to a curse. Assyrian and Babylonian inscriptions frequently threatened enemies with exactly this fate. Covenant curses in Deuteronomy 28:26 include this motif directly. (Deut 28:26; Rev 19:17-18)

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH: "Do not enter the house of mourning, and do not go to lament, and do not grieve for them, for I have taken away my peace (shalom) from this people — the declaration of YHWH — even lovingkindness (hesed) and mercies (rachamim).
verses.note: HEBREW: Three key covenant terms appear together: shalom (H7965 — wholeness, peace, well-being), hesed (H2617 — covenant loyalty, lovingkindness), and rachamim (H7356 — compassion, from rechem, "womb," connoting deep maternal tenderness). YHWH declares he has withdrawn all three — an inversion of covenant blessing. (Lam 3:22; Hos 2:19)

For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: "Behold, I am causing to cease from this place, before your eyes and in your days, the voice of rejoicing and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The cessation of the voice of the bridegroom and the bride is a recurring judgment motif in Jeremiah (7:34; 25:10) and is directly echoed in Revelation 18:23 regarding Babylon's fall, suggesting a typological relationship between Jerusalem's judgment and eschatological Babylon. (Rev 18:23; Jer 25:10)

verses.flow: Then you shall say to them: "Because your fathers forsook me — the declaration of YHWH — and walked after other gods and served them and bowed down to them, and me they forsook and my Torah (torah) they did not keep.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's indictment traces the sin back through generations — the fathers' apostasy is compounded by the sons' greater wickedness (v.12). The word torah here may refer broadly to YHWH's instruction and covenant stipulations rather than narrowly to a written text, though by Jeremiah's time the Deuteronomic Torah was known. (Deut 28:45; Neh 9:16-18)

verses.flow: So I will hurl you from off this land into the land that you have not known, you nor your fathers; and there you shall serve other gods day and night, because I will not show favor to you."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The threat that Israel will serve "other gods" in exile is theologically loaded. In the worldview of Deuteronomy 32:8-9 and the divine council framework, the nations were allotted to the oversight of divine beings (bene Elohim). Exile among the nations meant coming under the dominion of those spiritual powers — a reversal of Israel's privileged status under YHWH's direct rule. (Deut 4:19-20; Deut 32:8-9; Dan 10:13)

verses.flow: "Therefore, behold, days are coming — the declaration of YHWH — when it shall no more be said, 'As YHWH lives who brought up the children of Israel from the land of Egypt,'
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The New Exodus theme emerges here and in 23:7-8. The return from Babylonian exile will be so magnificent that it will eclipse the Exodus from Egypt as the defining act of divine redemption. This trajectory points forward to the ultimate New Exodus accomplished by Messiah. (Isa 43:18-19; Matt 2:15; 1 Cor 10:1-4)

verses.flow: "Behold, I am sending for many fishermen — the declaration of YHWH — and they shall fish them up. And afterward I will send for many hunters, and they shall hunt them from every mountain and from every hill and from the clefts of the rocks.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The images of fishermen and hunters capturing prey are military metaphors for the Babylonian armies, who would systematically sweep through the land leaving no fugitives. Babylonian royal inscriptions boast of hunting and fishing expeditions as symbols of dominion. The reversal in vv.14-16 moves from restoration promise back to judgment, creating a complex literary structure. (Amos 4:2; Hab 1:14-15)

verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "double" (mishneh, H4932) does not necessarily mean a mathematically doubled punishment but rather "the full equivalent" or "that which corresponds to" — a complete and thorough recompense. The defiling of the land with carcasses (pigre) of idols echoes Levitical pollution language. (Isa 40:2; Rev 18:6)

verses.flow: O YHWH, my strength and my stronghold, and my refuge in the day of distress, to you shall the nations come from the ends of the earth and shall say: "Surely our fathers have inherited falsehood, vanity and things in which there is no profit.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's personal lament becomes a prophetic oracle of eschatological hope — the nations will ultimately recognize the bankruptcy of their idols and turn to YHWH. This universalistic note stands in tension with the judgment oracles and anticipates the New Covenant's global scope. (Isa 2:2-4; Zech 8:20-23; Rom 15:9-12)

verses.note: HEBREW: The declaration "they shall know that my name is YHWH" (yeda'u ki shemi YHWH) is a recognition formula common to both Jeremiah and Ezekiel (over 70 times in Ezekiel alone). It frames divine judgment and restoration as ultimately revelatory acts — YHWH acts in history so that both Israel and the nations come to acknowledge his identity and sovereign lordship. (Exod 6:7; Ezek 36:23; Rev 15:4)

verses.flow: The sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of diamond it is engraved upon the tablet of their heart, and upon the horns of your altars,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Pen of iron" ('et barzel) and "point of diamond" (tsipporen shamir) — the imagery of indelible inscription suggests sin so deeply etched it cannot be erased. The "tablet of the heart" (luach libbam) contrasts sharply with Jeremiah's own New Covenant promise (31:33) where YHWH writes His law on this same tablet. THEOLOGICAL: The horns of the altars were the most sacred points of the altar of sacrifice; sin's inscription there indicates that even false worship is permanently recorded before God.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: Cursed is the man who trusts in man and makes flesh his arm, and whose heart turns away from YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The word "cursed" ('arur) echoes the covenant curse language of Deuteronomy 27-28. "Makes flesh his arm" uses basar (flesh) as the Hebrew antithesis of ruach (spirit), contrasting human frailty with divine power (Isa 31:3). This verse sets up the wisdom-like contrast of vv. 5-8, structurally parallel to Psalm 1, presenting two ways — the way of human dependence versus trust in YHWH.

verses.flow: Blessed is the man who trusts in YHWH, whose trust is YHWH.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The blessed man of v. 7 who trusts fully in YHWH finds its ultimate fulfillment in Messiah Jesus, who as the Son of Man placed complete trust in the Father (John 5:19, 30). THEOLOGICAL: The word "blessed" (baruk) here is a declarative pronouncement of covenant favor, mirroring Psalm 1:1. The repetition "whose trust IS YHWH" (lit. YHWH is his trust) makes YHWH Himself the object and ground of confidence.

verses.flow: For he shall be like a tree planted by water, that sends out its roots by the stream, and does not fear when heat comes, for its leaf remains green; and it is not anxious in the year of drought, for it does not cease to bear fruit.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of the tree planted by water recalls Psalm 1:3 almost verbatim, and Ezekiel's messianic cedar (Ezek 17:22-24). HISTORICAL: Lush, water-fed trees were a powerful image of life and blessing in the Ancient Near East, particularly against the backdrop of Judah's semi-arid landscape where perennial streams were rare and precious.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The heart is deceitful ('aqov) above all things, and it is desperately sick — who can know it?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Deceitful" translates 'aqov (H6121), from the same root as the name Ya'akov (Jacob), meaning "to supplant" or "to be crooked." This is a profound wordplay: the heart is Jacob-like — a supplanter and deceiver. "Desperately sick" ('anush) means incurably ill, beyond human remedy. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is foundational to the biblical doctrine of total depravity; the heart's self-deception runs so deep that only YHWH can probe and know it (v. 10), pointing toward the necessity of the New Covenant heart transformation (31:33; Ezek 36:26).

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct

verses.flow: I, YHWH, search the heart and test the mind (lit. kidneys; kelayot), to give to every man according to his ways, according to the fruit of his deeds.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Mind" translates kelayot (H3629), literally "kidneys," which in Hebrew anthropology were considered the seat of deep emotions and inner moral life, equivalent to what we might call the conscience or innermost being. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's searching of heart and kidneys is a divine prerogative (cf. Ps 7:9; Rev 2:23), distinguishing Him absolutely from idols and establishing the certainty of divine judgment based on true inner character, not outward performance.

verses.flow: Like a partridge that gathers a brood it did not hatch, so is he who makes riches but not by justice; in the midst of his days they will leave him, and in his end he will be a fool.
A throne of glory, set on high from the beginning, is the place of our sanctuary.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a sudden doxological exclamation — likely the prophet's or the community's voice — affirming YHWH's heavenly throne as the true and eternal sanctuary. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "throne of glory set on high from the beginning" resonates with the heavenly temple imagery found throughout the prophets (Isa 6; Ezek 1) where YHWH presides over the divine assembly. It contrasts with the defiled earthly sanctuary mentioned throughout this chapter.

verses.flow: O YHWH, the hope (mikveh) of Israel — all who forsake you shall be put to shame; those who turn away from you shall be written in the earth, for they have forsaken YHWH, the fountain of living waters (mayim chayyim).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hope" translates mikveh (H4723), which also means a reservoir or gathering of waters — a beautiful double entendre immediately reinforced by "fountain of living waters" (maqor mayim chayyim). Those who forsake YHWH the water-source are metaphorically "written in the earth" (dust), contrasting with those written in heaven (cf. Luke 10:20). TYPOLOGY: Jesus explicitly applies this "living water" language to Himself in John 4:10-14 and 7:37-38, presenting Himself as the ultimate fulfillment of YHWH as Israel's fountain.

verses.flow: Heal me, O YHWH, and I shall be healed; save me, and I shall be saved, for you are my praise.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a model of prophetic lament prayer. The double petition "heal me / save me" followed immediately by "I shall be healed / I shall be saved" reflects absolute confidence in YHWH's sufficiency. "You are my praise" (tehillati) suggests that YHWH Himself is the content and ground of Jeremiah's boasting, not any personal vindication.

verses.flow: Yet they did not listen or incline their ear, but stiffened their neck, so as not to hear and not to receive instruction (musar).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Musar" (H4148) — instruction, discipline, or correction — carries connotations of covenantal training and reproof, used extensively in Proverbs for wisdom's formation. The phrase "stiffened their neck" (qashah 'et-'orpam) is a standard covenant-rebellion idiom in the Hebrew Bible (Exod 32:9; Deut 9:6), describing stubborn, willful resistance to YHWH's authority.

verses.flow: then there shall enter through the gates of this city kings and princes who sit on the throne of David, riding in chariots and on horses, they and their princes, the men of Judah, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem; and this city shall be inhabited forever.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The promise of Davidic kings entering Jerusalem's gates on the Sabbath, with the city inhabited "forever" (le'olam), carries messianic overtones. The fulfillment of an eternal Davidic reign is realized in Messiah Jesus (Luke 1:32-33; Rev 22:5). THEOLOGICAL: The conditional structure of vv. 24-26 reveals that covenant blessing — even the Davidic throne's continuity — was tied to Sabbath obedience, illustrating the integrated nature of Israel's covenant responsibilities.

verses.flow: But if you do not listen to me, to keep the Sabbath day holy and not to carry a burden and enter through the gates of Jerusalem on the Sabbath day, then I will kindle a fire in its gates, and it shall devour the palaces of Jerusalem and shall not be quenched."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The prophecy of fire devouring Jerusalem's palaces was literally fulfilled in 586 BC when Nebuchadnezzar's armies burned the city (2 Kgs 25:9; Jer 52:13). THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "shall not be quenched" (lo tikh'beh) uses the same language as YHWH's unquenchable judgment fire, signaling this is not mere military defeat but covenantal curse (cf. Deut 32:22). The Sabbath, as the sign of the Mosaic covenant (Exod 31:13), becomes here the hinge on which national survival turns.

verses.flow: And the vessel that he was making was marred in the hand of the potter; so he made it again into another vessel, as it seemed right in the eyes of the potter to make it.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb translated "marred" is shachath (Strong's H7843), meaning to ruin or spoil. The "wheels" ('obhnayim, H70) is literally "two stones," the dual form reflecting the double stone disc of an ancient potter's wheel. The image is not of accidental failure but of the potter's sovereign authority to rework clay that does not yield properly. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the hermeneutical key to the chapter: God's sovereign freedom is not capricious but responsive — the clay's yielding or resistance shapes what the potter makes of it, within the potter's overarching will. (Rom 9:20-21)

verses.flow: "O house of Israel, am I not able to do to you as this potter?" — declares YHWH. "Behold, like the clay in the hand of the potter, so are you in my hand, O house of Israel."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The potter-clay analogy establishes divine sovereignty over Israel specifically and nations generally. Paul applies the same imagery in Romans 9:20-21 to argue for God's absolute freedom in election. Importantly, the context here is national and covenantal, not merely individual predestination. HEBREW: The verb yatsar (H3335), "to form/fashion," is the same verb used of God forming (yatsar) Adam in Genesis 2:7, deepening the creatorial overtones of this sovereign claim. (Isa 45:9), (Rom 9:20)

verses.flow: "At one moment I may speak concerning a nation or concerning a kingdom, to uproot and to pull down and to destroy it;"

verses.flow: "but if that nation turns back from its evil concerning which I have spoken, I will relent (nacham) of the evil that I intended to do to it."
verses.note: HEBREW: The word nacham (H5162), rendered "relent," is a profound term denoting divine reconsideration in response to human change — not fickleness but a genuine responsiveness within God's covenantal character. It does not imply that God was wrong but that his stated intention was conditionally framed. THEOLOGICAL: Verses 7-10 form a chiastic theological principle: divine decrees of judgment or blessing are not unconditional — they hinge on the nation's response. This principle governs much of the prophetic literature and is explicitly invoked in Jonah 3:10. (Jon 3:10), (Jer 26:3)

verses.flow: "And now, speak please to the men of Judah and to the inhabitants of Jerusalem, saying, 'Thus says YHWH: Behold, I am fashioning evil against you, and devising a plan against you. Turn back, each one from his evil way, and make good your ways and your deeds.'"

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: "But they will say, 'It is hopeless! For we will walk after our own plans, and each of us will act according to the stubbornness (sheririyth) of his evil heart.'"
verses.note: HEBREW: The term sheririyth (H8307), "stubbornness," derives from a root suggesting hardening or persisting in a chosen course; it is a recurring word in Jeremiah for the hardened self-determination of the apostate heart (Jer 3:17; 7:24; 9:14). THEOLOGICAL: The people's response is a tragic refusal of grace — they acknowledge the prophetic call but declare it futile before they have tried to obey. This anticipates the new covenant promise in Jeremiah 31:33, where God will inscribe the Torah on the heart so that such stubbornness is overcome. (Jer 31:33)

verses.flow: Therefore, thus says YHWH: "Ask among the nations — who has heard such things? The virgin of Israel has done a most horrible thing (sha'arurith)."
verses.note: HEBREW: Sha'arurith (H8186) is an intensive form meaning something that causes horror or makes one's hair stand on end — extreme abomination. The phrase "virgin of Israel" (bethulath Yisra'el) is a personification of the nation in its covenant relationship to YHWH, emphasizing the gravity of the betrayal. THEOLOGICAL: Israel's apostasy is presented as uniquely shocking because no nation abandons its gods the way Israel has abandoned YHWH. The rhetorical question to the nations presupposes they would be horrified by such faithlessness even among pagan peoples. (Jer 2:10-11)

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: "Does the snow of Lebanon forsake the rock of the open field? Do the foreign flowing waters run dry, the cool waters that flow?"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Lebanon's snow-capped peaks (Mount Hermon) and cold mountain streams were famous and constant features of the ancient Levantine landscape. The imagery draws on observable natural constancy to shame Israel's unnatural inconstancy toward YHWH. HEBREW: The verse is textually difficult; the MT reading is compressed and the exact referents of "rock of the field" and "foreign waters" are disputed, but the contrast is clear: nature is more faithful to its design than Israel is to its covenant Lord.

verses.flow: "Like the east wind (qadiym) I will scatter them before the enemy; I will show them the back and not the face in the day of their calamity."
verses.note: HEBREW: The qadiym (H6921), the east wind, is the sirocco — the hot, desiccating desert wind that withers vegetation and brings destruction. Its use here is deliberately chosen as a symbol of devastating judgment. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Scattering before the enemy as divine judgment (showing the back, not the face) reflects the withdrawal of divine presence and protection — the reversal of the Aaronic blessing's "YHWH make his face shine upon you" (Num 6:25-26). (Num 6:25), (Deut 32:20)

verses.flow: Then they said, "Come, let us devise schemes against Jeremiah, for instruction (torah) shall not perish from the priest, nor counsel from the wise, nor the word from the prophet. Come, let us smite him with the tongue and let us not give heed to any of his words."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The three offices named — priest (torah/instruction), wise man (counsel/etsah), and prophet (word/dabar) — represent the three classical channels of divine communication and community guidance in ancient Israel. The conspirators' logic is that these institutional channels will continue without Jeremiah, making him expendable. TYPOLOGY: This conspiracy against the prophet who speaks truth foreshadows the conspiracy against Jesus by priests, elders, and scribes. The attempt to silence the word of YHWH through the mouth of his messenger is a recurring pattern in redemptive history. (Matt 26:3-4), (Jer 11:19)

verses.flow: Shall evil be repaid for good? For they have dug a pit for my soul. Remember how I stood before you to speak good concerning them, to turn away your wrath from them.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jeremiah's intercessory stance — standing before YHWH to speak good on behalf of those who now seek his destruction — is a striking type of Christ's intercessory suffering. He prayed for those who persecuted him, yet ultimately calls for justice. This tension between intercession and imprecation reflects the complexity of prophetic identity as mediator. THEOLOGICAL: The "pit" (shuchah) dug for Jeremiah's soul recalls Jeremiah 11:19 and the broader motif of the righteous sufferer whose enemies plot his destruction — a motif climaxing in the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53. (Isa 53:9), (Luke 23:34)

verses.flow: Yet you, YHWH, you know all their counsel against me for death; do not cover over their iniquity, and their sin do not blot out before your face; let them be made to stumble before you — in the time of your anger, deal with them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's imprecatory prayer calls for divine justice rather than personal vengeance — he commits the matter to YHWH's judgment. The request not to "blot out" their sin stands in inverse parallel to Moses's intercession (Exod 32:32) and reflects the prophet's recognition that some sins, if persisted in, must meet their full covenantal consequence. HEBREW: The verb kaphar (H3722), here used negatively ("do not cover over"), is the same root as the noun kippur in Yom Kippur — the Day of Atonement. Jeremiah prays that no atonement cover these sins, a sobering use of sacrificial language. (Exod 32:32), (Ps 69:27-28)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Thus said YHWH: "Go and buy a potter's earthen flask, and take some of the elders of the people and some of the elders of the priests,
verses.note: HEBREW: The "earthen flask" (baqbuq, Strong's H1228) is a narrow-necked clay bottle, distinct from the potter's vessel in Jer 18. The root may be onomatopoetic, imitating the gurgling sound of liquid poured from it. The act of purchasing a specific object for prophetic symbolism reflects the Hebrew prophetic tradition of acted-out signs (otot), where the physical object becomes an extension of the divine word. TYPOLOGY: The deliberate breaking of the vessel (v.11) foreshadows irreversible judgment — a pattern fulfilled ultimately in the destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BC and again in AD 70 (Luke 19:43-44).

verses.flow: And you shall say: Hear the word of YHWH, O kings of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem. Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing upon this place a disaster (ra'ah) so great that the ears of everyone who hears of it will tingle.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ra'ah" (Strong's H7451) carries the full semantic range of evil, disaster, and calamity simultaneously. "The ears of everyone who hears it will tingle (tsalal, H6750)" is a phrase also used in 1 Sam 3:11 and 2 Kgs 21:12, forming a literary connection marking events of supreme covenantal rupture. THEOLOGICAL: The formula "YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel" (YHWH tseva'ot elohei Yisrael) is a full throne-room title invoking both divine kingship over the heavenly council and YHWH's specific covenantal relationship with Israel.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: and have built the high places of Baal to burn their sons in fire as burnt offerings to Baal, which I did not command, nor speak of, nor did it enter into my heart —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is among the most theologically explicit divine repudiations of child sacrifice in the Hebrew Bible. The triple negation — "I did not command, nor speak of, nor did it enter into my heart" — constitutes an absolute divine disavowal, directly refuting any claim that Molech-worship or Baal-tophet rituals were acceptable variations of Yahwistic religion. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Baal here functions as a rival divine patron, a member of the nations' allotted spiritual hierarchy (cf. Deut 32:8-9), whose worship in Israel constitutes a direct act of covenant treason against YHWH.

verses.flow: therefore, behold, days are coming, declares YHWH, when this place shall no more be called Topheth, or the Valley of the Son of Hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Topheth" (tofet, H8612) is of disputed etymology; it may derive from a root meaning "hearth" or "place of burning," or may be a deliberate scribal vocalization with the vowels of "boshet" (shame, H1322) as a theological insult. "Ge-Ben-Hinnom" (Valley of the Son of Hinnom) becomes "Gei Hahareg ah" (Valley of Slaughter), a devastating eschatological renaming. HISTORICAL: The Valley of Hinnom (ge-hinnom) is the geographic origin of the Greek word "Gehenna," used by Jesus as a metaphor for final judgment (Matt 5:22, 29-30).

verses.flow: And I will make void the counsel (etsah) of Judah and Jerusalem in this place, and I will cause them to fall by the sword before their enemies and by the hand of those who seek their life. And I will give their corpses as food for the birds of the heavens and for the beasts of the earth.
verses.note: HEBREW: "I will make void (baqaq, H1238) the counsel (etsah, H6098)" — a wordplay on the baqbuq flask purchased in v.1. The same root used for the flask is used for the act of emptying out. STRUCTURE: This verbal connection between the flask (baqbuq) and the voiding (baqaq) is a deliberate literary device tying the prophetic symbol directly to its interpretation, reinforcing that the sign-act is not merely illustrative but participates in the reality it announces.

verses.flow: And I will make this city a desolation and a hissing (shereqah); everyone who passes by it will be appalled and will hiss on account of all its wounds.

verses.flow: And I will cause them to eat the flesh of their sons and the flesh of their daughters, and each one shall eat the flesh of his neighbor, in the siege and in the distress with which their enemies and those who seek their life shall distress them.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The siege cannibalism threatened here echoes the covenant curses of Deuteronomy (Deut 28:53-57) with precise verbal parallels, indicating Jeremiah is consciously invoking the Mosaic covenant lawsuit. Archaeological and extrabiblical sources confirm that siege cannibalism occurred during the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem in 588-586 BC, as also narrated in Lamentations 4:10. THEOLOGICAL: The fulfillment of these curses confirms YHWH's faithfulness to the terms of the Sinai covenant, even in judgment, underscoring that the disaster is covenantally structured, not arbitrary.

verses.flow: and shall say to them: Thus says YHWH of hosts: So I will break this people and this city, as one breaks a potter's vessel, so that it cannot be mended again. And in Topheth they shall bury, until there is no more place to bury.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of a vessel broken beyond repair (ein l'rafa'ah — "so that it cannot be healed/mended") anticipates the New Covenant logic of Jer 31:31-34: the old covenant administration, like the flask, is broken irreparably, requiring not repair but a new creation. HEBREW: "Ein l'rafa'ah" (H7495) — "without healing/mending" — uses the same root (rapha) as divine healing elsewhere, here negated to signal a judgment that transcends ordinary restoration and requires eschatological intervention.

verses.flow: The houses of Jerusalem and the houses of the kings of Judah shall be defiled like the place of Topheth — all the houses upon whose rooftops they have made offerings to all the host of the heavens, and have poured out drink offerings to other gods.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Offerings to "all the host of the heavens" (kol tseva elohim hashamayim) on rooftops reflects the worship of astral deities — the sun, moon, planets, and stars — understood in the ANE and in Second Temple literature as corresponding to angelic/divine beings governing celestial domains. This practice is also condemned in Deut 4:19, where YHWH's allotment of these heavenly bodies to the nations is noted, making Israel's worship of them a profound act of spiritual defection. HISTORICAL: Rooftop astral worship is archaeologically attested in the ancient Near East and is also referenced in Jer 44:17-19 in connection with the "Queen of Heaven."

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing upon this city and upon all its towns all the disaster (ra'ah) that I have spoken against it, because they have stiffened their neck so as not to hear my words.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing indictment — "they have stiffened their neck (hiqshu et-orpam) so as not to hear my words" — is a persistent Deuteronomic formula for covenant stubbornness (Deut 9:6, 13; Neh 9:16). STRUCTURE: Chapter 19 ends not with hope but with unmitigated judgment, and flows directly into chapter 20 where Jeremiah is publicly beaten and confined by Pashhur — the physical consequence of this prophetic proclamation, demonstrating the social cost of faithful covenant witness.

verses.flow: Now Pashhur the son of Immer, the priest, who was also chief officer in the house of YHWH, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Pashhur son of Immer was the chief officer (paqid) of the temple, responsible for order and security within the sanctuary precincts. This made him the religious-administrative antagonist to Jeremiah's prophetic ministry. The Immer priestly clan is attested in Ezra 2:37 and Neh 7:40. His role anticipates later temple authorities who opposed Jesus (Matt 21:23).

verses.flow: And it came to pass on the next day, that Pashhur released Jeremiah from the stocks. Then Jeremiah said to him, "YHWH has not called your name Pashhur, but Terror-on-Every-Side (Magor-Missabib).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Magor-Missabib" (מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב, Strong's H4036) means literally "terror from all around" or "terror-on-every-side." YHWH renaming Pashhur is an act of prophetic judgment — the name becomes a living oracle. This mirrors the renaming of Abram and Jacob as covenant acts, but here the new name signals doom rather than blessing.

verses.flow: O YHWH, you have enticed me, and I was enticed; you have overpowered me, and you have prevailed. I have become a laughingstock all the day; everyone mocks me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse opens one of Jeremiah's most anguished "confessions" (chapters 11-20). The Hebrew verb "pattah" (פָּתָה, H6601) carries the sense of seduction, enticement, or even deception — the same word used for the seduction of a virgin (Exod 22:16). Jeremiah does not accuse YHWH of sin but expresses the overwhelming, even coercive, nature of prophetic calling. This raw honesty in prayer is paradigmatic for lament theology.

verses.flow: For whenever I speak, I cry out — violence and destruction I proclaim! For the word of YHWH has become to me a reproach and a derision all day long.

verses.flow: If I say, "I will not mention him, and I will speak no more in his name," then there is in my heart as it were a burning fire shut up in my bones, and I am weary with holding it in, and I cannot.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "a burning fire shut up in my bones" (אֵשׁ בֹּעֶרֶת עָצוּר בְּעַצְמֹתָי) uses "atsor" (עָצוּר, H6113) meaning to be restrained, imprisoned, or shut in. The image portrays the word of YHWH as an uncontainable force within the prophet's physical being. This internal compulsion distinguishes the true prophet from the false one who speaks from his own heart (cf. Jer 23:16). TYPOLOGY: Paul's "woe to me if I do not preach the gospel" (1 Cor 9:16) echoes this same inescapable prophetic constraint.

verses.flow: For I have heard the whispering of many — Terror-on-Every-Side! "Denounce him! Let us denounce him!" say all my close friends, those who watch for my fall. "Perhaps he will be enticed, and we will prevail over him, and we will take our vengeance on him."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "Terror-on-Every-Side" (Magor-Missabib) is being turned back against Jeremiah by his enemies, who adopt the prophet's own oracle as a taunt. The phrase "close friends" (enoshei shelomi, lit. "men of my peace") who plot against him foreshadows both the suffering servant's betrayal (Ps 41:9) and Jesus being betrayed by Judas, one of his own (John 13:18).

verses.flow: But YHWH is with me as a mighty warrior (gibbor); therefore my persecutors will stumble and will not prevail. They will be greatly ashamed, for they have not succeeded — an everlasting dishonor that will never be forgotten.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Gibbor" (גִּבּוֹר, H1368) denotes a mighty warrior, champion, or hero — used of YHWH as the divine warrior throughout the Psalter (Ps 24:8) and of the messianic king in Isa 9:6 ("El Gibbor," Mighty God). YHWH's presence as Jeremiah's champion stands in sharp contrast to the false security Pashhur's authority represents. THEOLOGICAL: The certainty of divine vindication shifts the tone from lament to confidence, a movement that structures the lament psalms.

verses.flow: O YHWH of hosts, who tests the righteous, who sees the inmost parts and the heart, let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you I have committed my cause.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse closely parallels Jer 11:20 and reflects the covenantal role of YHWH as divine judge who weighs the "kidneys and heart" (kilyot v'lev) — the seat of the deepest inner life in Hebrew anthropology. The appeal to divine judgment rather than personal vengeance demonstrates Jeremiah's submission to covenantal justice, foreshadowing the suffering servant's similar trust (Isa 53:12; 1 Pet 2:23).

verses.flow: Sing to YHWH! Praise YHWH! For he has delivered the life (nephesh) of the needy from the hand of evildoers.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The sudden doxological outburst in verse 13 — "Sing to YHWH! Praise YHWH!" — is jarring after the lament, functioning as a liturgical hinge. It is immediately followed by the darkest curse of the chapter (vv. 14-18), suggesting either a textual dislocation or a deliberate contrast that mirrors the emotional whiplash of genuine prophetic experience. The "nephesh" (נֶפֶשׁ, H5315) of the needy is the whole self, not merely the soul.

verses.flow: Cursed be the day on which I was born! The day when my mother bore me — let it not be blessed!
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The cursing of one's birth-day (vv. 14-18) is one of the most radical lament speeches in all of Scripture, paralleled only by Job 3:3-10. The rhetorical strategy is significant: Jeremiah does not curse God, nor does he curse himself directly, but redirects the curse onto the day and the messenger. This preserves theological propriety while expressing the full depth of existential despair. Such lament anticipates the cry of dereliction from the cross (Matt 27:46).

verses.flow: Why did I come out from the womb to see toil and sorrow, and that my days should be spent in shame?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter ends on an unresolved note of "toil" (amal, עָמָל, H5999) and "shame" (boshet, בֹּשֶׁת, H1322) — with no divine response. This suspended ending is theologically deliberate: Jeremiah's suffering is not immediately explained or resolved. It points forward to the suffering servant who bears the sin and shame of the people without immediate vindication (Isa 53:3-5), and ultimately to the resurrection as the final answer to such unanswered lament.

verses.flow: "Please inquire of YHWH on our behalf, for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is making war against us. Perhaps YHWH will deal with us according to all his wonderful deeds (nifle'otav), so that he may go up away from us."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The diplomatic mission from Zedekiah to Jeremiah reflects standard ANE practice of consulting court prophets during military crisis. Nebuchadnezzar (Nebuchadrezzar in Hebrew, the more original Babylonian form) began his siege of Jerusalem circa 588 BC. The request invokes YHWH's "wonderful deeds" (nifle'otav), likely alluding to the miraculous deliverance of Jerusalem under Hezekiah in 701 BC (2 Kgs 19:35), hoping for a similar intervention.

verses.flow: 'Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Behold, I am about to turn back the weapons of war that are in your hands, with which you are fighting against the king of Babylon and against the Chaldeans who are besieging you outside the walls, and I will gather them into the midst of this city.

verses.flow: And I myself will fight against you with an outstretched hand (yad netuyah) and with a strong arm (zero'a chazaqah), even in anger and in wrath (chemah) and in great fury (qetzef gadol).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The formula "outstretched hand and strong arm" (yad netuyah / zero'a chazaqah) is a deliberate and devastating reversal of the Exodus language used of YHWH's redemption of Israel from Egypt (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 26:8). Here YHWH wields those same redemptive instruments now as instruments of judgment against his own people — the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28 fully activated.

verses.flow: And afterward, declares YHWH, I will give Zedekiah king of Judah, and his servants, and the people, and those who remain in this city from the pestilence, from the sword, and from the famine, into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and into the hand of their enemies and into the hand of those who seek their lives. He shall strike them down with the edge of the sword; he shall not pity them nor spare them nor have compassion (racham).'
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The threefold covenant curse triad of "sword, famine, and pestilence" appears repeatedly in Jeremiah (14:12; 24:10; 27:8) as the full enactment of the Deuteronomic curse sequence (Deut 28:21-22, 48-52). The explicit withdrawal of pity, sparing, and compassion (racham, H7355) signals the suspension of covenant mercy — a stark theological statement about the exhaustion of divine patience.

verses.flow: "And to this people you shall say: 'Thus says YHWH: Behold, I am setting before you the way of life (derech hacha'yim) and the way of death (derech hammavet).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "way of life" and "way of death" placed before the people echoes the covenant choice of Deuteronomy 30:15-19 and anticipates the eschatological two-ways theology of the NT (Matt 7:13-14). Paradoxically, here the "way of life" leads through surrender and apparent defeat — a pattern fulfilled typologically in Christ's own death as the path to life (John 12:24-25).

verses.note: HEBREW: The word shalal (H7998) means "spoil" or "plunder" taken in war. The phrase "his life shall be to him as a prize (shalal)" is a striking idiom meaning he will escape with his bare life as if it were plunder rescued from a battlefield. This phrase recurs in Jer 38:2 and 39:18, indicating a deliberate rhetorical pattern in the book.

verses.flow: O house of David! Thus says YHWH: Execute justice (mishpat) in the morning, and deliver him who has been robbed from the hand of the oppressor, lest my wrath go forth like fire, and burn with none to quench it, because of the evil of your deeds."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "house of David" address invokes the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7) and its royal obligations. The call to "execute justice (mishpat) in the morning" reflects the ancient Near Eastern duty of the king as enforcer of divine order — the earthly representative of the divine council's decrees. Failure at this covenantal responsibility is precisely what triggers the divine council's judgment decree against the dynasty (cf. Ps 82:2-4).

verses.flow: "Behold, I am against you, O inhabitant of the valley, O rock of the plain (tzur hammiyshor), declares YHWH — you who say, 'Who shall come down against us? Or who shall enter our dwelling?' —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "O inhabitant of the valley, O rock of the plain" likely refers to Jerusalem itself, situated between valleys (Kidron, Hinnom) yet positioned on elevated terrain. The rhetorical confidence — "who shall come down against us?" — mirrors the false security theology denounced throughout Jeremiah (7:4, "the temple of YHWH"). The irony is that YHWH himself will be the one who "comes down" against the city.

verses.flow: And I will punish you according to the fruit of your doings, declares YHWH, and I will kindle a fire in her forest (ya'ar), and it shall devour all that is round about her."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of fire kindled in Jerusalem's "forest" (ya'ar, H3293) may refer to the cedar paneling of the palace complex (cf. the "House of the Forest of Lebanon," 1 Kgs 7:2) or more broadly to the city's wealth and structures. Fire as divine judgment instrument is a recurring motif (Jer 17:27; 52:13), ultimately fulfilled in the Babylonian burning of Jerusalem in 586 BC. The phrase "fruit of your doings" (peri ma'alleykhem) underscores the organic connection between covenant unfaithfulness and its consequences.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: Do justice (mishpat) and righteousness (tsedaqah), and deliver from the hand of the oppressor him who has been robbed. And do no wrong; do no violence to the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, nor shed innocent blood in this place.
verses.note: HEBREW: "mishpat" (H4941) denotes lawful judgment and social order; "tsedaqah" (H6666) denotes righteousness that conforms to a covenant standard. Together they form a hendiadys representing the full administration of covenant justice, particularly toward the vulnerable — the ger (sojourner), yatom (fatherless), and almanah (widow), a triad representing the most legally exposed members of Israelite society. THEOLOGICAL: The king's role as covenant administrator is here made explicit; failure in social justice is treated as covenant breach, not merely moral failure. (Ps 72:1-4; Isa 1:17)

verses.flow: For if you will indeed do this thing, then there shall enter through the gates of this house kings who sit on the throne of David, riding in chariots and on horses — he and his servants and his people.
But if you will not heed these words, I swear by myself, declares YHWH, that this house shall become a desolation.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH swears by himself — a divine oath formula that invokes no higher authority than God's own being. The desolation of the palace is cast as a judicial consequence of covenant unfaithfulness, mirroring the conditional Mosaic covenant structure of Deuteronomy 28-30. (Heb 6:13)

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH concerning the house of the king of Judah: "You are a Gilead to me, the head of Lebanon. Yet surely I will make you a wilderness, cities not inhabited.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gilead and Lebanon were famous for their forests and natural abundance — invoking them as descriptions of the Davidic house highlights its greatness and what it stands to lose. Cedar imagery throughout this chapter evokes royal power and divine provision that is being squandered. THEOLOGICAL: The transformation from Gilead and Lebanon — verdant and towering — to utter wilderness dramatizes the reversal of blessing under covenant faithlessness.

verses.flow: And they shall answer: Because they forsook the covenant (berit) of YHWH their God, and worshiped other gods and served them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The abandonment of the covenant (berit, H1285) is identified as the root cause of national catastrophe. This verse functions almost as a liturgical explanation of judgment, voiced by foreign observers, making the shame of Israel' apostasy visible before the nations. (Deut 29:24-26)
