verses.flow: Weep not for the dead, nor grieve for him; weep bitterly for him who goes away, for he shall return no more to see the land of his birth.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "The dead" most likely refers to King Josiah, slain at Megiddo (609 BC), while "he who goes away" refers to Jehoahaz/Shallum, taken to Egypt by Pharaoh Neco (2 Kgs 23:30-34). The living exile is presented as more pitiable than the honored dead — a striking inversion of expected grief hierarchies.

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH concerning Shallum the son of Josiah, king of Judah, who reigned in place of Josiah his father, and who went forth from this place: "He shall return here no more,
but in the place where they exiled him, there shall he die, and this land he shall see no more."
Woe to him who builds his house without righteousness (tsedaqah) and his upper rooms without justice (mishpat), who makes his neighbor serve him for nothing and does not give him his wages,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The woe oracle against Jehoiakim presents building projects funded by unpaid labor as a direct violation of covenant law (Lev 19:13; Deut 24:14-15). The king's exploitation of his subjects is thus framed as covenant-breaking, not merely social injustice. TYPOLOGY: Jesus echoes this woe-oracle form in his denunciations of Jerusalem's leaders (Matt 23), suggesting familiarity with Jeremianic prophetic form.

verses.flow: Do you reign because you compete in cedar? Did not your father eat and drink and do justice (mishpat) and righteousness (tsedaqah)? Then it was well with him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The contrast between Jehoiakim and his father Josiah is stark. Josiah was the paradigmatic covenant-keeping king (2 Kgs 23:25), and his simple enjoyment of food and drink while doing justice is held up as true prosperity. "Competing in cedar" likely mocks Jehoiakim's grandiose building projects as vain self-aggrandizement.

verses.flow: He judged the cause of the poor and needy; then it was well. Is not this to know me? declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The identification of knowing YHWH with defending the cause of the poor is theologically profound. Knowledge of God (da'at Elohim) in Hebrew thought is relational and covenantal, not merely propositional; it manifests in covenant action. (Hos 4:1-2; 6:6; Matt 25:40)

verses.flow: But your eyes and your heart are fixed on nothing but your dishonest gain, and on shedding innocent blood, and on practicing oppression and violence.

Therefore thus says YHWH concerning Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah: "They shall not lament for him, saying, 'Ah, my brother!' or 'Ah, sister!' They shall not lament for him, saying, 'Ah, lord!' or 'Ah, his majesty!'
With the burial of a donkey he shall be buried — dragged and thrown outside the gates of Jerusalem."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The prophecy of Jehoiakim's burial "like a donkey" stands in tension with 2 Kings 24:6, which records he "slept with his fathers." Many scholars suggest he was killed in a coup or siege and denied proper burial, consistent with this prophecy and with 2 Chr 36:6.

verses.flow: The wind (ruach) shall shepherd all your shepherds, and your lovers shall go into captivity; then you shall be ashamed and confounded because of all your evil.
verses.note: HEBREW: "ruach" (H7307) here translated "wind" carries its characteristic ambiguity — breath, wind, or spirit. The play is intentional: the very breath/spirit of YHWH will "shepherd" (ra'ah, H7462) the false shepherds away. This ironic reversal — shepherds being shepherded to destruction — echoes the extended shepherd motif throughout Jeremiah and Ezekiel.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "signet ring" (chotam, H2368) image is extraordinarily potent — it represents delegated royal authority, identity, and covenant seal. YHWH declares that even Coniah's status as the divine signet would not prevent removal. This verse stands in striking typological contrast to Haggai 2:23, where Zerubbabel (Coniah's grandson) is restored to the signet-ring dignity, pointing forward to the Davidic restoration in Messiah. (Hag 2:23)

verses.flow: Is this man Coniah a despised, broken vessel, a vessel in which no one delights? Why are he and his children hurled and cast into a land which they do not know?
O land, land, land, hear the word of YHWH!
verses.note: HEBREW: The triple address "eretz, eretz, eretz" (land, land, land) is a powerful rhetorical device — a tricolon of intensification functioning as a summons. The land itself is called as a witness, recalling the cosmic witness formula of Deuteronomy 32:1 and Isaiah 1:2. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The summons of the land as witness may echo ancient treaty-witness convention in which heaven and earth (or cosmic entities) were invoked as covenant witnesses. (Deut 32:1; Isa 1:2)

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: Write this man down as childless, a man who shall not prosper in his days, for no man of his offspring shall prosper, sitting on the throne of David and ruling again in Judah.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse presents one of the most theologically complex problems in the Davidic genealogy. Coniah's line is cursed from the throne, yet Matthew 1:12 traces Jesus' legal lineage through Jeconiah (Coniah). The resolution lies in the virgin birth: Jesus' legal right to David's throne passes through Joseph (Coniah's line, Matt 1), but his biological line avoids the curse through Mary (tracing through Nathan, son of David, Luke 3:31). Thus the curse is honored while the promise is fulfilled through sovereign divine design. (Matt 1:12; Luke 3:31; Hag 2:23)

verses.flow: "Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter the sheep of my pasture!" declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The term "shepherds" (ro'im, H7462) refers to kings and political leaders of Israel and Judah who were charged with caring for God's covenant people. This oracle of woe follows the condemnation of Jehoiakim and Coniah in chapter 22 and sets up the messianic counter-vision of the ideal shepherd-king to come. Compare Ezekiel 34, where the same shepherd metaphor is developed at length. TYPOLOGY: Jesus explicitly identifies himself as the Good Shepherd who replaces failed under-shepherds (John 10:11-16), fulfilling this very oracle.

verses.flow: "Behold, days are coming," declares YHWH, "when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch (Tsemach), and he shall reign as king and act wisely, and he shall execute justice (mishpat) and righteousness (tsedaqah) in the land."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Branch" translates Tsemach (H6780), a term meaning a sprout or growth from a plant. This is a formal messianic title appearing also in Jeremiah 33:15, Zechariah 3:8, and 6:12, pointing to a specific royal figure from the Davidic line. TYPOLOGY: The "righteous Branch" is a direct messianic prophecy fulfilled in Jesus, who is the Son of David (Matt 1:1), reigning as the wise and just king. "Act wisely" (sakal, H7919) connotes both wisdom and success in carrying out one's purpose. THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of mishpat (justice) and tsedaqah (righteousness) is the covenantal standard for ideal kingship throughout the prophets (Isa 9:7; 11:4).

verses.flow: "In his days Judah will be saved, and Israel will dwell securely. And this is the name by which he will be called: 'YHWH is our righteousness (YHWH Tsidqenu).'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "YHWH Tsidqenu" (YHWH our Righteousness) is a remarkable divine name applied to the coming Davidic king, implying that the righteousness of the Messiah is in fact the righteousness of YHWH himself — an extraordinarily high Christological statement. TYPOLOGY: Paul's doctrine of justification identifies Christ as the one who "became to us righteousness from God" (1 Cor 1:30; 2 Cor 5:21), making this one of the most direct messianic-theological titles in the Hebrew Bible. HISTORICAL: The name may also be a deliberate wordplay on the name of the wicked King Zedekiah (Tsidqiyahu, meaning "YHWH is my righteousness"), contrasting the false claimant with the true righteous king to come.

verses.flow: Concerning the prophets: My heart is broken within me; all my bones tremble (shake). I am like a drunken man, and like a man overcome by wine, because of YHWH and because of his holy words.
verses.note: HEBREW: The section heading "concerning the prophets" (lanevi'im) introduces a distinct oracle unit running through v. 40. Jeremiah's visceral physical response — broken heart, trembling bones — reflects the Hebrew concept of prophetic pathos, the prophet's deep emotional participation in the divine grief over covenant unfaithfulness. THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between Jeremiah's genuine anguish and the false prophets' blithe assurances is a central theme of the unit.

verses.flow: "But in the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen a horrible thing: they commit adultery and walk in falsehood, and they strengthen the hands of evildoers, so that no one turns from his evil. All of them have become to me like Sodom, and its inhabitants like Gomorrah."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The comparison to Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 18-19) is among the gravest condemnations in the prophetic corpus, used elsewhere of Jerusalem only in Isa 1:9-10 and Lam 4:6. THEOLOGICAL: The false prophets of Jerusalem are judged more harshly than those of Samaria (v. 13) because they operate from the holy city, the seat of the Temple and the Davidic covenant. Strengthening "the hands of evildoers" (chazaq yedei) is a phrase of moral complicity indicating active enablement of sin.

verses.note: HEBREW: La'anah (H3939, wormwood) is a bitter herb used metaphorically throughout Scripture for the bitter consequences of sin and false teaching (Prov 5:4; Amos 5:7; Rev 8:11). verses.flow: "For who has stood in the council (sod) of YHWH to see and to hear his word? Who has attended to his word and heard it?"
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "council (sod) of YHWH" is a direct reference to the heavenly assembly — the divine council — in which YHWH deliberates with his heavenly court. The term sod (H5475) means a confidential circle or assembly. True prophets such as Amos (3:7) and Isaiah (6:1-8) were admitted to this council to receive authentic divine communication. THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question condemns the false prophets by exposing that they have no access to the genuine heavenly deliberations; they speak from their own imagination rather than from revealed divine counsel. Compare 1 Kings 22:19-23 where Micaiah sees the divine council in session.

verses.flow: "Behold, the storm (sa'ar) of YHWH! Wrath has gone forth, a whirling tempest; it will burst upon the head of the wicked."
"The anger of YHWH will not turn back until he has executed and accomplished the purposes of his heart. In the latter days (be'acharit hayamim) you will understand it clearly."
verses.note: HEBREW: "The latter days" (be'acharit hayamim, H319 + H3117) is an eschatological formula appearing across the prophets and Pentateuch (Gen 49:1; Num 24:14; Deut 4:30; Isa 2:2; Mic 4:1). It denotes a future time of decisive divine action, often associated with the restoration of Israel and the establishment of the Messianic kingdom. THEOLOGICAL: The statement that the people will "understand it clearly" in those days implies that the full significance of Jeremiah's oracles awaits eschatological fulfillment for its complete comprehension.

verses.flow: "But if they had stood in my council (sod), then they would have proclaimed my words to my people, and they would have turned them from their evil way and from the evil of their deeds."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The repetition of sod (council) from v. 18 forms a deliberate inclusion, framing the entire question of prophetic legitimacy around access to the divine assembly. A true prophet stands in the heavenly council (as Isaiah did in Isa 6, Micaiah in 1 Kgs 22, and Jeremiah himself implies), receives the divine word, and transmits it faithfully. THEOLOGICAL: The practical criterion of true prophecy is its transformative effect — genuine divine words turn people from evil (shub, H7725), while false words strengthen wickedness.

words.g: not gods in the ordinary sense

verses.flow: "Am I a God who is near," declares YHWH, "and not a God who is far off?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical questions asserting YHWH's immanence ("near") and transcendence ("far off") together form a declaration of divine omnipresence. The false prophets apparently operated as if YHWH were a localized deity who could not see their private machinations. This verse corrects both tendencies: YHWH is not so local that he can be manipulated, nor so remote that he is unaware of human affairs.

verses.flow: "The prophet who has a dream, let him tell the dream; but the one who has my word, let him speak my word faithfully. What is the straw to the wheat?" declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The contrast between "straw" (teben, H8401) and "wheat" (bar, H1250) is a vivid agricultural metaphor for the difference between the worthless content of dream-visions and the substantive reality of the authentic divine word (dabar). THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah does not condemn dreams categorically (cf. Num 12:6) but insists on the absolute qualitative superiority of direct prophetic utterance; a genuine dream must be reported as such, not falsely inflated into a divine oracle.

verses.flow: "Is not my word like fire?" declares YHWH, "and like a hammer that shatters the rock?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The dual metaphor of fire and a shattering hammer for the divine word (dabar, H1697) emphasizes its irreducible power and penetrating force. Fire consumes what is false and refined; the hammer breaks what seems impenetrable. TYPOLOGY: The author of Hebrews employs similar imagery in describing the living and active word of God as sharper than a two-edged sword (Heb 4:12), and the divine word's judging capacity is fully realized in the incarnate Word (John 1:1; Rev 19:15).

verses.flow: "And when this people, or a prophet or a priest asks you, 'What is the burden (massa) of YHWH?' you shall say to them, 'You are the burden, and I will cast you off,'" declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: Massa (H4853) means both "burden" and "oracle/utterance," derived from nasa (H5375, to lift or carry). The term was a technical vocabulary for prophetic pronouncements, particularly oracles of judgment (cf. Isa 13:1; 15:1; Nah 1:1; Hab 1:1). THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah employs a devastating wordplay: the people ask for the "burden-oracle" of YHWH, and YHWH responds that they themselves are the burden he is about to cast off (nasa + them as object). This reversal transforms a technical prophetic inquiry into a statement of divine rejection.

verses.flow: "And I will bring upon you everlasting reproach and everlasting shame (kelimah), which shall not be forgotten."
verses.note: HEBREW: Kelimah (H3639) denotes shame, disgrace, or humiliation — specifically the kind that comes from public exposure and dishonor. The modifier "everlasting" (olam, H5769) marks this judgment as permanent and irrevocable, in sharp contrast to the superficial peace and honor the false prophets sought to secure for themselves. THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with a profound irony: those who sought honor through false prophecy receive precisely the opposite — eternal, unforgettable shame — underscoring the covenantal principle that misrepresenting YHWH's word carries the most severe divine sanction.

verses.flow: YHWH showed me, and behold, two baskets of figs placed before the temple of YHWH, after Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had carried away captive Jeconiah the son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, and the princes of Judah, with the craftsmen and the smiths from Jerusalem, and had brought them to Babylon.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The vision is dated to after the first deportation of 597 BC, when Nebuchadnezzar (Nebuchadrezzar in Jeremiah's spelling) carried off Jeconiah (also called Jehoiachin or Coniah; 2 Kgs 24:10-16), the royal court, and the skilled artisan class. This represents the cream of Judah's society, which Babylon strategically extracted to deprive Judah of leadership and technical capacity. TYPOLOGY: The two baskets of figs function as a covenant sorting oracle — those who submit to YHWH's judgment through exile are positioned for restoration, anticipating the remnant theology central to the New Covenant (Rom 9:27).

verses.flow: And I will give them a heart to know me, that I am YHWH; and they shall be my people and I will be their God, for they shall return to me with their whole heart.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "a heart to know (lada'at) me" is among the most theologically charged in Jeremiah, anticipating the New Covenant promise of Jer 31:33-34 where Torah is written on the heart and all shall "know YHWH." The covenant formula "they shall be my people and I will be their God" (Hebrew: lehyot lahem le'elohim) is the core Sinaitic covenant declaration (Lev 26:12; Ezek 36:28), here re-issued as a promise of future renewal. TYPOLOGY: This interior transformation of the heart is fulfilled in Christ, whose Spirit effects the new birth and writes the law upon the heart of believers (2 Cor 3:3; Heb 8:10).

verses.flow: I will make them a horror (za'avah), an evil thing, for all the kingdoms of the earth — a reproach and a byword, a taunt and a curse, in all the places where I shall drive them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Za'avah" (Strong's H2189) denotes an object of trembling horror or terrifying dread, distinct from mere shame or reproach. The accumulation of five terms — horror, evil thing, reproach, byword, taunt, and curse — reflects the covenant curse ('alah) language of Deuteronomy 28:37, indicating that the fate of those who resist YHWH's discipline fulfills the enacted covenant sanctions. THEOLOGICAL: The scattering "to all the kingdoms of the earth" ironically inverts the Abrahamic blessing by which Israel was to be a blessing among the nations (Gen 12:2-3).

The word that came to Jeremiah concerning all the people of Judah, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah — that was the first year of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The fourth year of Jehoiakim (605 BC) corresponds precisely to the first year of Nebuchadnezzar II, synchronizing Judean and Babylonian chronologies. This was the year of the Battle of Carchemish, a pivotal turning point when Babylon decisively defeated Assyria and Egypt, reshaping the ancient Near East. The synchronism here is one of the most precise chronological anchors in the prophetic literature.

verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase translated "persistently" renders the Hebrew idiom hashkem v'shaloach (Strong's H7925 + H7971), literally "rising early and sending," a Jeremianic formula appearing over a dozen times in the book (Jer 7:13, 25; 11:7; 26:5; 29:19; 32:33; 35:14-15; 44:4). The idiom conveys urgent, repeated divine communication, underscoring that Israel's judgment is the result of prolonged, willful rejection, not ignorance.

verses.flow: behold, I am sending and taking all the families of the north — declares YHWH — and Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, my servant, and I will bring them against this land and against its inhabitants and against all these surrounding nations. And I will devote them to destruction (cherem), and make them a horror and a hissing and everlasting desolations.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's designation of Nebuchadnezzar as "my servant" (avdi, Strong's H5650) is theologically striking — a pagan king serves as an unwitting instrument of divine judgment. This reflects the biblical theology of divine sovereignty over all nations (Isa 10:5-7, where Assyria is YHWH's "rod of anger"). HISTORICAL: The term cherem (devoted to destruction) evokes the holy war tradition of Israel, here ironically applied to Israel itself and surrounding nations. The Babylonian campaigns devastated the entire Levantine corridor.

verses.flow: And this whole land shall become a ruin and a desolation, and these nations shall serve the king of Babylon seventy years.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "seventy years" prophecy is one of the most debated chronological statements in the Hebrew Bible, cited and interpreted in Daniel 9:2 and 2 Chronicles 36:21. Scholarly discussion centers on whether it counts from 605 BC (first deportation) to 539/538 BC (Cyrus decree), yielding approximately 66-70 years, or from 586 BC (Jerusalem's fall) to 516 BC (temple rededication), yielding exactly 70 years. The number may also carry symbolic weight as a conventional period of divine judgment in ANE literature.

verses.flow: Then after the fullness of seventy years, I will punish the king of Babylon and that nation — declares YHWH — the land of the Chaldeans, for their iniquity, and I will make it an everlasting desolation.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The judgment of Babylon after seventy years anticipates the broader eschatological pattern of the "fall of Babylon" motif in prophetic literature (Isa 13-14; Jer 50-51; Rev 17-18). The promise that Babylon itself would be judged demonstrates that YHWH's sovereignty is not exhausted in using nations as instruments of punishment — the instruments themselves remain accountable. This establishes a recurring NT pattern of judgment upon the oppressor.

verses.flow: For thus YHWH, the God of Israel, said to me: "Take from my hand this cup of the wine of wrath (chemah), and make all the nations to whom I send you drink it.
verses.note: HEBREW: The "cup of the wine of wrath" (kos yayin ha-chemah) is a powerful Hebrew image for divine judgment. Chemah (Strong's H2534) means burning wrath or fury. TYPOLOGY: This cup-of-wrath imagery permeates the prophets (Isa 51:17, 22; Ps 75:8; Hab 2:16; Ezek 23:31-33) and finds its ultimate typological resolution in Gethsemane, where Jesus prays that "this cup" might pass from him (Matt 26:39) — the cup being the wrath of God poured out for sin (Rom 3:25; 2 Cor 5:21).

and all the kings of the north, the near and the far — each one with his brother — and all the kingdoms of the earth that are upon the face of the ground; and the king of Sheshach shall drink after them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sheshach" is widely recognized as an Atbash cipher — a Hebrew code substituting letters in reverse alphabetical order — for "Babel" (Babylon). Aleph becomes Taw, Beth becomes Shin, etc., yielding sh-sh-k from b-b-l. Atbash also appears in Jeremiah 51:1 ("Leb-qamai" = Chaldea). HISTORICAL: The use of a cipher may indicate scribal caution under Babylonian surveillance, or it may serve as a literary device emphasizing Babylon's ultimate subjection to the same judgment she administers.

verses.flow: For behold, upon the city which is called by my name I am beginning to work evil, and shall you go entirely unpunished? You shall not go unpunished, for I am summoning a sword against all the inhabitants of the earth — declares YHWH of hosts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The principle stated here — that YHWH begins judgment at his own house — becomes an explicit NT theological axiom (1 Pet 4:17). If Jerusalem, the city "called by my name" (shem, Strong's H8034), is not spared, no nation can expect exemption. This grounds the universal scope of judgment (v. 26) in the logic of divine impartiality (Deut 10:17; Rom 2:11).

verses.flow: And you, prophesy against them all these words, and say to them: YHWH will roar from on high, and from his holy habitation he will lift up his voice; he will roar mightily against his fold (naveh), and shout like those who tread grapes against all the inhabitants of the earth.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH's roaring "from on high" from "his holy habitation" (me'on qadsho) evokes the cosmic mountain imagery of the divine council assembly, the heavenly court from which decrees over the nations go forth (cf. Ps 29:3-9; Amos 1:2; Joel 3:16; Mic 1:2-3). The image of YHWH roaring like one treading grapes against his naveh (pasture/fold) may carry the double sense of his own people and all the earth. THEOLOGICAL: The descent of divine speech from the heavenly realm to execute earthly judgment is a recurring theophanic pattern.

verses.flow: The clamor will reach to the ends of the earth, for YHWH has a controversy (riv) with the nations; he is entering into judgment with all flesh. As for the wicked, he has given them to the sword — declares YHWH.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The term riv (Strong's H7379, controversy/lawsuit) places YHWH in the role of divine plaintiff or judge in a cosmic legal proceeding against the nations. This is the language of the divine council court (cf. Ps 82:1, 8; Mic 6:1-2; Isa 3:13). The nations are not merely militarily defeated — they are found guilty in YHWH's heavenly tribunal. THEOLOGICAL: "He has given them to the sword" echoes the judicial verdict language of Psalm 82:7, where the divine beings are sentenced to die as men.

verses.flow: Wail, you shepherds, and cry out! Roll yourselves in the dust, you lords of the flock! For the days of your slaughter and your dispersions are fulfilled, and you shall fall like a precious vessel.
verses.note: HEBREW: The "shepherds" (ro'im, Strong's H7462) and "lords of the flock" (addirey ha-tson) are clearly political and royal figures — kings and nobles of the nations — employing the ubiquitous ANE metaphor of the king as shepherd (cf. Ezek 34; Zech 11). TYPOLOGY: The imagery of faithless shepherds who face slaughter anticipates the condemnation of Israel's own shepherds in Ezekiel 34 and ultimately the contrast with Jesus as the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) who lays down his life rather than fleeing.

verses.flow: And the peaceful (shalom) pastures are made desolate because of the fierce anger (charon aph) of YHWH.

verses.flow: Like a lion he has left his lair (sukkah), for their land has become a desolation because of the sword of the oppressor and because of the fierce anger of YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: Sukkah (Strong's H5521) typically refers to a booth or temporary shelter, but in this context clearly means the lair or thicket of a lion — a rare usage also found in Psalm 10:9 and Lamentations 2:6. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH departing his sukkah like a lion on the prowl is a devastating reversal of Sukkot (Feast of Booths) imagery, where God's protective presence dwells with his people. The divine protector has become the divine destroyer, a consequence of covenant breach (Lev 26:27-33; Deut 28:25-26). The double cause of desolation — "the sword of the oppressor" and "the fierce anger of YHWH" — holds in tension human agency and divine causation throughout the chapter.

verses.flow: In the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, this word came from YHWH, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "The beginning of the reign" likely refers to the accession year of Jehoiakim (609-608 BC), shortly after Josiah's death at Megiddo. This places the Temple Sermon early in Jehoiakim's reign, providing a historical anchor for the events described in Jer 7 (where the sermon's content is elaborated). Jehoiakim was installed by Pharaoh Necho rather than by popular will, setting a tense political backdrop.

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH: Stand in the court of the house of YHWH, and speak to all the cities of Judah that come to worship in the house of YHWH all the words that I command you to speak to them; do not hold back a word.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH commands Jeremiah to hold back nothing — a recurring prophetic commissioning motif (cf. Isa 58:1; Ezek 3:10-11). The phrase "do not hold back a word" (tigra' davar) underscores the inviolability of the prophetic word and the prophet's obligation of full fidelity to the divine message, regardless of consequence.

verses.flow: Perhaps they will listen, and each man will turn from his evil way, and I will relent (nacham) of the disaster that I am purposing to do to them, because of the evil of their deeds.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Relent" translates nacham (Strong's H5162), a term denoting a change of disposition or intent, often anthropomorphically describing YHWH's response to human repentance. It does not imply divine error but covenantal responsiveness. This same term structures the book of Jonah (3:9-10) and is central to understanding conditional prophecy throughout the Hebrew Bible.

verses.flow: then I will make this house like Shiloh, and this city I will make a curse (qelalah) to all the nations of the earth.'
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Shiloh was the site of the Tabernacle and the Ark of the Covenant in the pre-monarchic period (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1-4). Its destruction by the Philistines circa 1050 BC (evidenced archaeologically at Khirbet Seilun) made it a potent symbol of divine judgment upon a sacred site. YHWH uses this historical memory as a sober warning that no building — not even the Temple — is inviolably exempt from divine judgment.

verses.flow: Why have you prophesied in the name of YHWH, saying, 'This house shall be like Shiloh, and this city shall be desolated without an inhabitant'?" And all the people gathered against Jeremiah in the house of YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The crowd's reaction — seizing and threatening Jeremiah — reveals the dangerous conflation of national identity with the Temple's permanence. The belief in Zion's inviolability (rooted in the Davidic covenant tradition, Ps 46; 48) had hardened into a false theology. Jeremiah's message directly confronted this popular assumption, which is why it provoked such violent response.

verses.flow: Then the priests and the prophets spoke to the princes and to all the people, saying, "A sentence of death belongs to this man, for he has prophesied against this city, as you have heard with your own ears."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The irony is sharp — the very priests and prophets who should have championed covenant faithfulness now conspire to execute the true prophet. This false-prophet/true-prophet dynamic reaches its zenith in Jer 28 (Hananiah vs. Jeremiah). TYPOLOGY: Jesus faced a strikingly similar tribunal of priests and leaders who sought his death for speaking against the Temple (Matt 26:57-66; cf. John 2:19).

verses.flow: Only know for certain that if you put me to death, you will bring innocent blood upon yourselves, and upon this city, and upon its inhabitants; for in truth YHWH has sent me to you to speak all these words in your ears."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jeremiah's warning concerning "innocent blood" (dam naqiy) anticipates the NT theme profoundly: Pilate declared himself innocent of Jesus' blood (Matt 27:24), while Judas confessed he had betrayed "innocent blood" (Matt 27:4). Both the prophet and the Messiah stood before hostile authorities and warned of blood-guilt. Jeremiah here embodies the suffering-prophet archetype fulfilled in Christ.

verses.flow: "Micah of Moresheth prophesied in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah, and spoke to all the people of Judah, saying, 'Thus says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot): Zion shall be plowed as a field, and Jerusalem shall become a heap of ruins, and the mountain of the house a wooded height.'
verses.note: HEBREW: The citation of Micah 3:12 is remarkable as one of the very few places in the Hebrew Bible where a prophet explicitly quotes an earlier named prophet. "YHWH of hosts" renders YHWH Tseva'ot (H6635), the divine warrior title connoting YHWH's sovereignty over heavenly and earthly armies. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title Tseva'ot likely reflects YHWH's command over the divine council's host of heavenly beings (cf. 1 Kgs 22:19; Ps 89:6-8).

verses.flow: Did Hezekiah king of Judah and all Judah put him to death? Did he not fear YHWH and seek the face of YHWH, so that YHWH relented of the disaster that he had spoken against them? And we are doing great evil against our own souls."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Hezekiah's repentance is cited as the paradigmatic case of a king whose response averted prophesied disaster (cf. 2 Kgs 19; Isa 37-38). This confirms the conditional nature of prophetic judgment oracles and anchors nacham theology in historical precedent. The elders function here as constitutional legal voices drawing on covenantal memory to defend Jeremiah.

verses.flow: And there was also a man who prophesied in the name of YHWH — Uriah the son of Shemaiah from Kiriath-jearim — who prophesied against this city and against this land according to all the words of Jeremiah.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Uriah son of Shemaiah is unknown outside this passage, making this one of the most tantalizing figures in Jeremiah. His hometown Kiriath-jearim was where the Ark of YHWH rested before its transfer to Jerusalem (1 Sam 7:1-2; 2 Sam 6:2). His flight to Egypt and violent extradition parallels no other biblical episode and illustrates the lethal dangers faced by authentic prophets during Jehoiakim's reign.

verses.flow: But the hand of Ahikam the son of Shaphan was with Jeremiah, so that he was not given into the hand of the people to put him to death.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ahikam son of Shaphan belonged to one of the most significant families in late Judean history. His father Shaphan was the royal secretary who read the newly discovered Torah scroll to King Josiah (2 Kgs 22:8-10), and his son Gedaliah would later become governor of Judah after Jerusalem's fall (Jer 40:5). The Shaphan family's consistent protection of Jeremiah reveals a network of reform-minded officials who remained sympathetic to the prophetic word.

verses.flow: In the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, this word came to Jeremiah from YHWH, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The MT heading reads "In the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim" (cf. 26:1), but the body of the chapter (vv. 3, 12) consistently names Zedekiah as the reigning king. Most scholars regard "Jehoiakim" as a scribal error (cf. LXX which omits the heading entirely), placing the events firmly in Zedekiah's reign (597–586 BC), shortly before Babylon's final siege of Jerusalem.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Thus said YHWH to me: "Make for yourself bonds and yoke-bars, and put them upon your neck.
verses.note: HEBREW: The terms "bonds" (Hebrew: moser, Strong's H4147) and "yoke-bars" (Hebrew: mot, Strong's H4133) together describe the components of an ox's yoke — a wooden bar across the neck secured by leather straps. The act of physically wearing the yoke was a prophetic sign-act (Hebrew: ot, a symbolic deed conveying divine revelation), a dramatic form common to both Jeremiah and Ezekiel.

verses.flow: 'I myself made the earth, the man and the beast that are upon the face of the earth, by my great power and by my outstretched arm, and I give it to whomever it is right in my eyes to give it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's sovereignty claim over all the earth and its inhabitants is the theological foundation of the entire chapter. The phrase "by my great power and by my outstretched arm" (Hebrew: koach gadol uzroa netuyah) echoes the Exodus language of divine sovereignty (Deut 9:29), here applied not to Israel's deliverance but to YHWH's right to assign dominion to any king he chooses — including a pagan one.

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: 'And now I have given all these lands into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, my servant, and also the beasts of the field I have given him to serve him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Nebuchadnezzar is called YHWH's "servant" (Hebrew: eved, Strong's H5650), the same term applied to the patriarchs, Moses, and David. This remarkable designation indicates YHWH's use of pagan powers as instruments of covenantal discipline (cf. Isa 44:28 where Cyrus is similarly designated). TYPOLOGY: This pattern anticipates the NT's teaching that governing authorities are servants of God's purposes (Rom 13:1–4).

verses.flow: 'And all the nations shall serve him, and his son, and his son's son, until the time of his own land comes; then many nations and great kings shall make him serve them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The seventy-year limit implied by the three generations of Babylonian dominion (Nebuchadnezzar, his son, his grandson) corresponds to the prophesied seventy years of Jeremiah 25:11–12. YHWH's sovereignty extends even over the duration of imperial power — Babylon's dominion has a divinely fixed expiration.

verses.flow: 'And it shall be the nation and the kingdom which will not serve him — Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon — and which will not put its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon, with sword and with famine and with pestilence I will visit upon that nation, declares YHWH, until I have consumed them by his hand.

'But as for you, do not listen to your prophets, and to your diviners, and to your dreamers, and to your soothsayers, and to your sorcerers, who speak to you, saying, "You shall not serve the king of Babylon.",
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The list of false prophetic functionaries — "prophets" (nevi'im), "diviners" (qosemim), "dreamers" (cholmim), "soothsayers" (ononim), and "sorcerers" (mechasefim) — reflects the full range of ANE divination practices known from Mesopotamian and Canaanite contexts. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's prohibition against heeding them underscores the unique authority of the covenantal word delivered through his commissioned prophets, contrasted with these illegitimate mediators.

verses.flow: 'But the nation which shall bring its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon and serve him, that nation I will let remain upon its own land, declares YHWH, and they shall cultivate it and dwell in it.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The offer of life through submission stands as a mercy within judgment. YHWH does not delight in the destruction of nations; even under the covenant curse, a path of survival remains open. This principle of life-through-surrender anticipates the gospel paradox of finding life through yielding rather than self-assertion.

verses.flow: 'For I have not sent them,' declares YHWH, 'but they are prophesying falsely in my name, so that I will drive you out and you will perish, you and the prophets who are prophesying to you.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The formula "I have not sent them" (Hebrew: lo shelachtim, cf. 14:14–15; 23:21) is YHWH's definitive verdict against false prophecy. The prophets' offense is not merely incorrect prediction but fraudulent use of the divine name — speaking "in my name" (Hebrew: bishmi) without divine commission, a form of taking the name of YHWH in vain at the highest institutional level.

verses.flow: But if they are prophets, and if the word of YHWH is with them, let them now intercede before YHWH of hosts, that the vessels which are left in the house of YHWH, and in the house of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem, do not go to Babylon.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah issues a rhetorical challenge to the false prophets: if their message is true and the temple vessels are already returning, let them intercede (Hebrew: yifgeu-na, from paga, Strong's H6293 — to meet, encounter, intercede) that the remaining vessels not be taken. True prophets intercede; false prophets merely predict what the people want to hear. The contrast with Moses and Samuel as intercessors (Jer 15:1) is pointed.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "pillars" (Hebrew: amudim), the "sea" (Hebrew: yam — the great bronze basin), and the "stands" (Hebrew: mekonot) are all well-attested furnishings of Solomon's temple described in detail in 1 Kings 7:15–37. Their ultimate fate — carried to Babylon — is recorded in 2 Kings 25:13–17, fulfilling this very oracle.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The promise "I will bring them up and restore them to this place" (Hebrew: vahaalitim vehashibotim) encompasses both the literal return of temple vessels under Zerubbabel (Ezra 1:7–11) and, typologically, the broader restoration of all that exile had scattered. The verb "restore" (Hebrew: shub, Strong's H7725 — to return, repent, restore) is the great covenantal word of homecoming, pointing ultimately to the eschatological restoration of all things in Messiah (Acts 3:21).

verses.flow: And it came to pass in that same year, in the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim — in the fourth year, in the fifth month — Hananiah son of Azzur, the prophet who was from Gibeon, spoke to me in the house of YHWH, before the eyes of the priests and all the people, saying,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The opening verse contains a textual difficulty — the Masoretic Text reads "the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim," but the context of Zedekiah's reign (v. 1b) and the reference to the fourth year align with Zedekiah (cf. Jer 27:1). Most scholars regard "Jehoiakim" as a scribal error for "Zedekiah," consistent with the Septuagint tradition. Gibeon was a Benjaminite city north of Jerusalem (Josh 18:25), associated with the tabernacle in the early monarchy period (1 Kgs 3:4).

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Hananiah's oracle uses the full prophetic formula "thus says YHWH of hosts," making his false prophecy particularly dangerous — it directly mimics the authentic prophetic speech of Jeremiah (Jer 27:4). This illustrates the crisis of discernment at the heart of the chapter: the form of true prophecy can be perfectly replicated by a false prophet.

verses.flow: Within two years of days I will bring back to this place all the vessels of the house of YHWH, which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon took from this place and brought to Babylon.
And Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, and all the exiles of Judah who went to Babylon — I will bring them back to this place, declares YHWH; for I will break the yoke of the king of Babylon."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Jeconiah (also called Coniah or Jehoiachin) was deported to Babylon in 597 BC along with the first wave of exiles, including the temple treasures. Hananiah's promise of a two-year return directly contradicted Jeremiah's letter to the exiles urging them to settle in Babylon for seventy years (Jer 29:1-14). This clash represents one of the sharpest confrontations between true and false prophecy in the Hebrew Bible.

verses.note: HEBREW: The word shalom (H7965, שָׁלוֹם) here carries its full semantic range: not merely "peace" as absence of conflict, but wholeness, welfare, and covenantal flourishing. Hananiah's message was a message of shalom (Jer 28:11), which Deuteronomy 18:21-22 specifically addresses as the test of a prophet — fulfillment. Jeremiah implicitly invokes this Deuteronomic criterion to challenge Hananiah's credentials.

verses.flow: Then Hananiah the prophet took the yoke-bar from upon the neck of Jeremiah the prophet and broke it.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The symbolic act of breaking the wooden yoke-bar (Hebrew: מוֹטָה, motah) is a counter-act of prophetic theater. Jeremiah had worn the yoke as a enacted prophecy of Babylonian domination (Jer 27:2). Hananiah's breaking of it is a rival sign-act claiming divine authority. The replacement of wood with iron (v. 13) demonstrates that false prophetic opposition to God's word does not revoke it but intensifies its judgment — a pattern seen in the hardening of Pharaoh's heart (Exod 4-14).

verses.flow: "Go and speak to Hananiah, saying, Thus says YHWH: Yoke-bars of wood you have broken, but in their place you have made yoke-bars of iron.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The irony of divine sovereignty is stark here: Hananiah's act of breaking the wooden yoke, intended to liberate, results in a heavier yoke of iron. This reflects the broader biblical principle that resistance to God's appointed discipline brings greater rather than lesser bondage. The image anticipates the ultimate submission of nations under divine sovereignty, fulfilled eschatologically in Christ's kingship (Ps 2:8-9; Rev 19:15).

verses.flow: And Jeremiah the prophet said to Hananiah the prophet, "Hear now, Hananiah: YHWH has not sent you, and you have caused this people to trust in a lie.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "trust" (Hebrew: בָּטַח, batach, H982) used here implies a settled reliance or confident security. Hananiah caused the people to "trust" (batach) in a lie (shaqer, H8267) — the same word used throughout Jeremiah for the faithless confidence Israel placed in false gods and alliances. The irony is that the people's misplaced batach in Hananiah's oracle mirrors their misplaced batach in the temple itself (Jer 7:4).

verses.flow: Therefore thus says YHWH: Behold, I am sending you away from upon the face of the ground; this year you shall die, for you have spoken rebellion (sarah) against YHWH."
verses.note: HEBREW: The term sarah (H5627, סָרָה), translated "rebellion," derives from a root meaning to turn aside or apostatize. It is a grave theological charge implying not merely error but willful defection from YHWH's authority. The sentence — death within the year — directly applies the criterion of Deuteronomy 18:20: "the prophet who presumes to speak a word in my name that I have not commanded him to speak... that prophet shall die." THEOLOGICAL: The swift fulfillment of Jeremiah's word against Hananiah (v. 17) functions as a divine validation of Jeremiah's prophetic office within the narrative, providing the very kind of confirmatory sign that verse 9 had described.

verses.flow: And Hananiah the prophet died in that same year, in the seventh month.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The death of Hananiah in the seventh month of the same year (he had prophesied in the fifth month, v. 1) is recorded with stark economy. This rapid fulfillment vindicates Jeremiah's prophetic authority and applies the Deuteronomic test in real time. The narrative closure also serves as a solemn warning within the book of Jeremiah against all who speak presumptuously in the name of YHWH — a theme that resonates into the New Testament's warnings about false prophets (Matt 7:15; 2 Pet 2:1).

verses.flow: Now these are the words of the letter that Jeremiah the prophet sent from Jerusalem to the remnant of the elders of the exile, and to the priests, and to the prophets, and to all the people whom Nebuchadnezzar had carried into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon—
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This letter constitutes one of the earliest known examples of written prophetic communication to a diaspora community. Jeremiah writes after the 597 BC deportation (the second, under Jehoiachin/Jeconiah) but before the final destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BC. The LXX version of this chapter is significantly different in arrangement and shorter, reflecting the broader textual divergence between the Masoretic Text and the Septuagint of Jeremiah.

verses.flow: after Jeconiah the king and the queen mother and the court officials, the princes of Judah and Jerusalem, and the craftsmen and the smiths had departed from Jerusalem—
by the hand of Elasah the son of Shaphan and Gemariah the son of Hilkiah, whom Zedekiah king of Judah sent to Babylon, to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The diplomatic mission of Elasah and Gemariah to Babylon provides Jeremiah an opportune courier for the letter. Shaphan was the scribe who read the newly discovered Torah scroll to King Josiah (2 Kgs 22:8-10), making his son Elasah part of a family aligned with prophetic reform. Such diplomatic envoys between vassal states and Babylon were common in this period of Neo-Babylonian imperial administration.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, to all the exiles whom I have sent into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon:
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "whom I have sent into exile" (Hebrew: hegleyti, from galah, "to uncover, remove, go into exile") is theologically striking—YHWH claims sovereign agency over the exile, not merely permitting it but actively executing it. This destabilizes any notion that the Babylonian gods had triumphed; rather, Israel's own God orchestrated the deportation as covenant discipline (Deut 28:36, 64).

verses.flow: And seek the shalom (shalom) of the city where I have sent you into exile, and pray to YHWH on its behalf, for in its shalom you will have shalom.
verses.note: HEBREW: The keyword shalom (שָׁלוֹם, Strong's H7965) carries a meaning far richer than "peace"—it encompasses wholeness, completeness, flourishing, and covenantal well-being. The command to "seek the shalom" of Babylon is stunning in context, inverting the expected posture of the conquered. The reciprocal logic—"in its shalom you will have shalom"—ties the exiles' welfare providentially to civic engagement in a pagan city, anticipating Paul's counsel in 1 Tim 2:1-2.

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH: When seventy years are completed for Babylon, I will visit you, and I will fulfill to you my good word, to bring you back to this place.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "seventy years" (Hebrew: shivim shanah) is a precise covenantal number. Daniel understood this prophecy literally and prayed on its basis (Dan 9:2). The verb "visit" (Hebrew: paqad, Strong's H6485) is a covenant term meaning to attend to, inspect, or act decisively upon—used both in judgment and redemption (Exod 3:16; 4:31). This verse grounds Israel's hope not in circumstance but in the word of YHWH.

verses.flow: For I know the plans I am planning for you, declares YHWH, plans for shalom and not for evil, to give you a future and a hope.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is among the most frequently quoted verses in all of Scripture regarding divine providence and hope. The Hebrew machashavot (מַחֲשָׁבוֹת, "plans, thoughts, purposes") from chashav ("to think, devise, reckon") indicates intentional, pre-deliberated counsel. The "future and a hope" (Hebrew: acharit vetiqvah) literally means "an end/outcome and an expectation"—pointing forward to eschatological restoration. This verse has New Covenant dimensions: the fullest "future and hope" is found in Messiah Yeshua, in whom all divine purposes converge (2 Cor 1:20; Eph 1:9-10).

verses.flow: And you will seek me and find me, when you search for me with all your heart.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The condition of seeking YHWH "with all your heart" (Hebrew: bekhol-levavchem) resonates deeply with the Shema (Deut 6:5) and the promise of new-heart covenant in Jer 31:31-34. This verse anticipates the post-exilic renewal and ultimately points to the new covenant era when the Spirit enables the wholehearted seeking that the flesh could not sustain. See also (Matt 7:7-8; Heb 11:6).

verses.flow: And I will be found by you, declares YHWH, and I will restore your fortunes and gather you from all the nations and all the places where I have driven you, declares YHWH, and I will bring you back to the place from which I sent you into exile.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "I will restore your fortunes" (Hebrew: shavti et-shevutkem) is a covenant-restoration formula appearing repeatedly in the prophets (Jer 30:3; Ezek 16:53; Amos 9:14). The root shub ("to return, restore") creates an elegant wordplay: YHWH will "return their returning." The gathering from all nations foreshadows not only the post-Babylonian return but the eschatological regathering connected to Messiah's reign (Isa 11:11-12; Matt 24:31).

verses.flow: thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, I am sending against them sword, famine, and pestilence, and I will make them like vile figs that are so rotten they cannot be eaten.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of "vile figs that cannot be eaten" deliberately echoes Jeremiah's earlier vision of two baskets of figs (Jer 24), where the good figs represented the exiles who would be restored and the bad figs symbolized Zedekiah's circle remaining in Jerusalem. The very community that congratulated itself on avoiding exile is now pronounced the recipient of sword, famine, and pestilence—a triadic judgment formula repeated throughout Jeremiah.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, concerning Ahab the son of Kolaiah and Zedekiah the son of Maaseiah, who are prophesying to you falsely in my name: Behold, I am delivering them into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and he shall strike them down before your eyes.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ahab son of Kolaiah and Zedekiah son of Maaseiah are otherwise unknown outside this passage, but their fate—being roasted in fire by Nebuchadnezzar—is consistent with what we know of Babylonian judicial execution methods. Their names becoming a byword of divine judgment (v.22) reflects the ANE practice of using notorious fates as curse formulas. The false prophets' sexual immorality alongside false prophecy links moral and spiritual corruption as inseparable in covenantal terms.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: The verb "roasted" (Hebrew: qalah, Strong's H7033) describes a particularly brutal form of execution attested in ancient Near Eastern texts. Babylonian kings used fire as both punishment and political terror. That this event became embedded in the curse vocabulary of the exilic community demonstrates how deeply Jeremiah's verified predictions shaped Israelite oral and liturgical tradition.

verses.flow: "Send to all the exiles, saying, Thus says YHWH concerning Shemaiah of Nehelam: Because Shemaiah has prophesied to you, though I did not send him, and has made you trust in a lie,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "I did not send him" (Hebrew: lo shalachtiw) is Jeremiah's consistent criterion for distinguishing true from false prophecy—divine commission (Jer 14:14; 23:21). False prophets in Jeremiah are not always depicted as insincere; rather, their fundamental error is speaking beyond divine authorization. The judgment on Shemaiah that he would not "see the good" YHWH would do serves as a sobering eschatological warning: those who oppose God's redemptive purposes forfeit participation in them.

The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH, saying:
"Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Write for yourself all the words that I have spoken to you in a scroll.
verses.note: HEBREW: The command to "write in a scroll" (katab be-sefer) signals formal, authoritative documentation of divine promise. This section (chapters 30-33) is often called the "Book of Consolation" or "Little Book of Comfort," structurally distinct within Jeremiah as a concentrated unit of hope amid surrounding oracles of judgment. The LXX version differs in arrangement but preserves this consolatory material.

verses.flow: For behold, days are coming — declares YHWH — when I will restore the fortunes (shub shebut) of my people Israel and Judah, says YHWH, and I will bring them back to the land that I gave to their fathers, and they shall possess it."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Restore the fortunes" renders the idiom shub shebut (Strong's H7725 + H7628), literally "turn the turning" or "restore the restoration." This pregnant idiom occurs frequently in Jeremiah and carries covenantal restoration weight. THEOLOGICAL: The promise encompasses both Israel (northern) and Judah (southern) — a reunification theme echoing Ezekiel 37 and pointing beyond the partial return under Ezra to an eschatological gathering. verses.flow: These are the words that YHWH spoke concerning Israel and concerning Judah:

The contrast with shalom (peace, wholeness) highlights the absolute rupture of covenant well-being. HEBREW: pachad (Strong's H6343) denotes terror or sudden dread, often used for the fear that overwhelms before YHWH's judgment.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The time of distress for Jacob" (et tzarah hi le-Ya'aqov, Strong's H6869) is a foundational eschatological phrase. Jewish tradition and Christian premillennial exegesis both recognize this as referring to an end-time tribulation of unparalleled severity. The crucial pivot is the salvation promise: "yet out of it he shall be saved" — deliverance comes through, not before, the distress. (Dan 12:1, Matt 24:21, Rev 7:14)

The verb "raise up" (qum, Strong's H6965) in the Hiphil suggests a specific future act of divine appointment. David had died centuries before Jeremiah; this therefore points to a Davidic descendant — ultimately Yeshua the Messiah — who will reign over restored Israel. The present-tense reign of the risen Christ and his future visible kingdom both fall within this promise's scope. (Ezek 34:23-24, Luke 1:32-33, Acts 2:30)

verses.flow: For I am with you," declares YHWH, "to save you; for I will make a full end of all the nations among whom I scattered you, but of you I will not make a full end. I will discipline (yasar) you in just measure, and I will by no means leave you unpunished."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The paradox of verse 11 is theologically profound: YHWH will make a "full end" (kalah, Strong's H3617) of all nations but NOT of Israel — a promise guaranteeing Israel's indestructibility as a covenant people. Yet discipline (yasar, Strong's H3256) is not withheld, affirming both grace and moral seriousness. This combination of preservation and chastisement defines the entire exile-restoration pattern. (Amos 9:8, Rom 11:1-2)

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The description of the coming ruler (adir, Strong's H117, "majestic one, noble") who "draws near" to YHWH is strikingly priestly as well as royal. THEOLOGICAL: The final verse closes the chapter by asserting that full comprehension of YHWH's purposes awaits eschatological fulfillment — a hermeneutical key indicating that the chapter's promises are not exhausted by any historical return but await final-age realization.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Rachel's weeping for her children is quoted by Matthew (Matt 2:18) in reference to Herod's massacre of the innocents at Bethlehem. The fulfillment is typological: as Rachel's descendants were carried into exile, so the children of Bethlehem were slaughtered at the coming of the true son of Rachel's line. HISTORICAL: Ramah, just north of Jerusalem (modern er-Ram), was a deportation staging point where captives were assembled before the march to Babylon (Jer 40:1).

verses.flow: Is Ephraim my dear son? Is he a delightsome child? For as often as I speak against him, I do remember him still. Therefore my heart yearns for him; I will surely have mercy on him, declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "my heart yearns (hamu me'ai) for him" is visceral and anatomical — "me'ai" refers to the bowels or intestines, the seat of deep emotion in Hebrew anthropology. This is the language of maternal compassion used of God, paralleling Isaiah's use of "rahamim" (womb-love) in Isa 49:15. THEOLOGICAL: That God "speaks against" Ephraim and yet "remembers him still" demonstrates that divine judgment and divine compassion are not in opposition but are held together in the mystery of YHWH's paternal love.

verses.flow: How long will you waver, O faithless daughter? For YHWH has created a new thing on the earth: a woman shall encompass a man.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "A woman shall encompass (tesovev) a man" is one of the most debated phrases in Jeremiah. The Hebrew "tesovev" means to surround or encircle. Some interpret this as a reversal of normal social order (the vulnerable protecting the strong), others as a messianic allusion to a virgin conceiving (cf. Isa 7:14; Matt 1:23). The "new thing" language (berah chadashah) elsewhere in prophetic literature signals divine intervention in history at the eschatological level (Isa 43:19).

verses.flow: And it shall come to pass that as I have watched over them to pluck up and break down, to overthrow, destroy, and bring harm, so I will watch over them to build and to plant, declares YHWH.
In those days they shall no longer say: "The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The proverb of sour grapes (also cited in Ezek 18:2) reflects a fatalistic theology of inherited guilt that both Jeremiah and Ezekiel repudiate. The New Covenant era will usher in a era of individual moral accountability directly before God, while simultaneously not negating the reality of generational consequences documented elsewhere in Torah (Exod 20:5). (Ezek 18:1-4; Rom 2:6)

verses.flow: Behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will make a new covenant (berit chadashah) with the house of Israel and the house of Judah,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Berit chadashah" — the New Covenant — is the theological climax of the entire book of Jeremiah and one of the most significant prophetic utterances in the Hebrew Bible. This is the only occurrence of the explicit phrase "new covenant" in the Old Testament. The author of Hebrews quotes Jeremiah 31:31-34 in full (Heb 8:8-12) and returns to it in Heb 10:16-17, identifying Jesus as the mediator of this covenant. Jesus himself invokes it at the Last Supper (Luke 22:20; 1 Cor 11:25). STRUCTURE: The phrase "behold, the days are coming" (hinneh yamim ba'im) appears three times in this section (vv. 27, 31, 38), forming a structural triplet that frames three eschatological promises: renewed population, renewed covenant, renewed city.

verses.flow: For this is the covenant that I will make with the house of Israel after those days, declares YHWH: I will put my law (torah) within them, and I will write it on their hearts. And I will be their God, and they shall be my people.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The internalization of Torah — written on the heart (lev) rather than on stone tablets — represents the most radical transformation of the covenant relationship. Where Sinai produced an external code requiring priestly and levitical mediation, the New Covenant produces direct, interior, personal knowledge of YHWH. The Apostle Paul interprets this through the Spirit's ministry (2 Cor 3:3; Rom 8:4). The covenant formula "I will be their God, and they shall be my people" (H5971, am) is the Urformel, the foundational formula, recurring across all major covenant contexts in the OT (Exod 6:7; Lev 26:12; Ezek 36:28; Rev 21:3). HEBREW: "Torah" (H8451) here should not be narrowly reduced to legal code — it carries the full sense of divine instruction and revealed will.

verses.flow: And no longer shall each one teach his neighbor and each his brother, saying, "Know YHWH," for they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest, declares YHWH. For I will forgive their iniquity, and I will remember their sin no more.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "They shall all know me (yada' oti)" — the verb "yada'" carries the full weight of covenantal, intimate, experiential knowledge, not merely intellectual assent. The democratization of this knowledge across all classes ("from the least to the greatest") dismantles the mediatorial hierarchy of Levitical religion and anticipates the Pentecostal outpouring of the Spirit upon "all flesh" (Joel 2:28-29; Acts 2:17). The ground of the covenant is entirely YHWH's act of forgiveness — "I will forgive their iniquity" — making clear that the New Covenant is established by grace, not moral reformation.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH, who gives the sun for light by day and the fixed order of the moon and the stars for light by night, who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar — YHWH of hosts is his name:
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot)" invokes YHWH's authority over the heavenly armies — both celestial bodies and divine beings of the council. The "fixed order" (chuqqot) of sun, moon, and stars recalls the creational ordering of Genesis 1 and suggests that YHWH's faithfulness to Israel is as immutable as the cosmic order he sustains. (Ps 148:1-6; Isa 40:26)

verses.flow: "If this fixed order departs from before me, declares YHWH, then shall the offspring of Israel cease from being a nation before me forever."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The permanence of the cosmos becomes the pledge of YHWH's covenant faithfulness to Israel — a rhetorical form called the "cosmic witness oath." This does not teach ethnic Israel's unconditional political supremacy but rather that YHWH's redemptive purposes for Israel as a covenant people cannot be annulled. Paul engages this theme in Romans 9-11, concluding that "all Israel shall be saved" (Rom 11:26). (Rom 11:1-2)

verses.flow: The whole valley of the dead bodies and the ashes, and all the fields as far as the brook Kidron, to the corner of the Horse Gate toward the east, shall be sacred (kodesh) to YHWH. It shall not be plucked up or overthrown anymore forever.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The geographical boundaries described in verses 38-40 encompass Jerusalem from the northeast (Tower of Hananel, cf. Neh 3:1; 12:39) around to the Kidron Valley on the east — areas that were ceremonially unclean due to burial sites and refuse. THEOLOGICAL: The declaration that even the valley of corpses and ashes shall be "kodesh" (holy) to YHWH is eschatologically stunning — defilement itself is sanctified, and the boundary between sacred and profane is erased in the restored city. This anticipates the New Jerusalem where "nothing unclean will ever enter it" (Rev 21:27) and the entire city is holy. (Zech 14:20-21; Rev 21:1-4)

verses.flow: The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH in the tenth year of Zedekiah king of Judah, which was the eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "tenth year of Zedekiah" (ca. 588/587 BC) synchronizes with the "eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar," providing a precise historical anchor for the siege. Nebuchadnezzar began his reign in 605 BC; by his eighteenth year, Jerusalem was in its final death throes. The dual dating reflects both the vassal and imperial perspectives on reckoning time.

verses.flow: For Zedekiah king of Judah had shut him up, saying, "Why do you prophesy and say, 'Thus says YHWH: Behold, I am giving this city into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall capture it;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Zedekiah's imprisonment of Jeremiah illustrates the recurring biblical pattern of the prophet persecuted for faithful proclamation. Jeremiah's confinement does not silence YHWH's word; rather, it becomes the stage for a remarkable act of covenant faith. The irony is intentional: the king who rejected the word is about to witness its dramatic vindication through a land purchase.

verses.note: HEBREW: The term "right of redemption" (ge'ullah, Strong's H1353) reflects the Levitical land-redemption law (Lev 25:25-28), wherein a kinsman-redeemer (go'el) had both the right and obligation to buy back family land that had been forfeited. Jeremiah's purchase is thus a legal act rooted in Mosaic covenant economics, not merely a symbolic gesture.

verses.flow: Then Hanamel my uncle's son came to me in the court of the guard, in accordance with the word of YHWH, and said to me, "Buy my field that is in Anathoth, which is in the land of Benjamin, for the right of possession and redemption is yours; buy it for yourself." Then I knew that this was the word of YHWH.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jeremiah's role as kinsman-redeemer (go'el) foreshadows Christ's redemption of humanity. Just as Jeremiah paid silver to redeem land under siege, the Messiah pays the ultimate redemptive price to reclaim what belongs to YHWH. The Book of Ruth develops the same go'el theology, which the NT applies to Christ (Eph 1:14; Rev 5:9-10).

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Take these deeds, both this sealed deed of purchase and this open deed, and put them in an earthenware vessel, that they may last for a long time.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The command to preserve documents in clay or ceramic vessels reflects a well-attested ANE archival practice. Clay jars provided excellent preservation in the Palestinian climate; indeed, the Dead Sea Scrolls were preserved in similar earthenware jars at Qumran. The "sealed copy" and "open copy" represent a standard ancient Near Eastern legal document format (cf. the Elephantine papyri).

verses.flow: "Ah, Lord YHWH! It is you who made the heavens and the earth by your great power and by your outstretched arm! Nothing is too hard (pala) for you.
verses.note: HEBREW: The exclamation "Ah, Lord YHWH!" renders the Hebrew interjection 'Ahah Adonai YHWH' — a combination of the divine titles that appears frequently in Jeremiah (Jer 1:6; 4:10; 14:13). The word pala' (Strong's H6381), translated "too hard," means "to be extraordinary, marvelous, surpassing." The rhetorical force is that nothing lies beyond YHWH's extraordinary power. The same root appears in Isa 9:6 (Wonderful Counselor) and Gen 18:14.

verses.flow: You show lovingkindness (hesed) to thousands, and you repay the iniquity of fathers into the bosom of their children after them, O great and mighty God, whose name is YHWH of hosts,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's prayer echoes the Decalogue (Exod 20:5-6) and the Mosaic covenant formula (Exod 34:6-7), grounding his intercession in revealed covenant theology. The tension between YHWH's hesed (lovingkindness, covenant loyalty, Strong's H2617) shown to thousands and the punishment of iniquity across generations reflects the covenantal structure of blessing and curse — not arbitrary divine behavior but covenant faithfulness operating in both directions.

verses.flow: You have shown signs and wonders in the land of Egypt, and to this day in Israel and among all mankind, and have made for yourself a name, as at this day.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The reference to "signs and wonders in the land of Egypt... and to this day" acknowledges the ongoing revelatory function of the Exodus event in Israelite memory. The Exodus was the paradigmatic saving act of YHWH in the OT, serving as the basis for covenant appeal throughout the prophets (cf. Hos 11:1; Amos 2:10; Mic 6:4). Later prophets, including Jeremiah himself, spoke of a new exodus surpassing the first (Jer 23:7-8).

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The declaration "I am YHWH, the God of all flesh" asserts YHWH's sovereignty not only over Israel but over all humanity and, implicitly, over all the divine beings who were allotted authority over the nations (Deut 32:8-9; Ps 82). YHWH's rhetorical question — "Is anything too hard for me?" — echoes Gen 18:14 and establishes his unrivaled omnipotence over all created beings, human and heavenly alike.

verses.flow: They built the high places of Baal in the Valley of the Son of Hinnom, to offer up their sons and daughters to Molech, though I did not command them, nor did it enter into my mind, that they should do this abomination, to cause Judah to sin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Valley of the Son of Hinnom (Ge-Hinnom, the origin of the NT word "Gehenna") was the site of child sacrifice to Molech — the most severe covenant violation in the Mosaic law (Lev 18:21; 20:2-5). YHWH's emphatic statement that this "did not enter into my mind" (lo alah al-libbi) is a strong anthropopathism underscoring the utter foreignness of such worship to YHWH's character. Jesus later uses Gehenna as an image of final judgment (Matt 5:22, 29-30).

verses.flow: Behold, I will gather them from all the lands to which I drove them in my anger and in my wrath and in great indignation. I will bring them back to this place and make them dwell in safety (betach).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The promise to "gather them from all the lands" into which they were driven anticipates the eschatological regathering of Israel, a theme developed throughout the latter prophets (Isa 11:11-12; Ezek 36:24; 37:21). The word betach (Strong's H983), "safety/security," implies not merely physical safety but the condition of undisturbed covenant rest — the shalom of a people dwelling under YHWH's protection.

verses.flow: And they shall be my people, and I will be their God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "They shall be my people, and I will be their God" is the covenant formula (Bundesformel) that runs from the Abrahamic and Mosaic covenants through the Prophets into the New Covenant (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:28; 2 Cor 6:16; Rev 21:3). Its repetition here in the context of restoration signals that what YHWH promises is not merely political return but full covenant renewal.

verses.flow: I will make with them an everlasting covenant (brit olam), that I will not turn away from doing good to them. And I will put the fear of me in their hearts, that they may not turn from me.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "everlasting covenant" (brit olam, Strong's H1285 + H5769) announced here is the same covenant anticipated in Jer 31:31-34 and elaborated in Ezek 36:26-27. Its defining characteristic is that YHWH himself ensures its permanence by placing the fear of himself in the hearts of his people — an internalized transformation that the NT identifies with the work of the Holy Spirit (Heb 8:10-12; 10:16). This is not a renewal of Sinai but a new creation of covenant relationship.

verses.flow: Fields shall be bought for money, and deeds shall be signed and sealed and witnessed, in the land of Benjamin, in the places around Jerusalem, and in the cities of Judah, in the cities of the hill country, in the cities of the Shephelah, and in the cities of the Negeb. For I will restore their fortunes (shub shebut), declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "restore their fortunes" renders the Hebrew shub shebut (or shub shebut, Strong's H7725 + H7622), literally "to turn the turning" or "to return the captivity." This is a pregnant idiomatic phrase used throughout the OT for YHWH's eschatological reversal of judgment (Job 42:10; Ps 126:4; Ezek 16:53). The geographic specificity — Benjamin, Jerusalem, hill country, Shephelah, Negeb — emphasizes that the restoration is concrete and territorial, not merely spiritual metaphor.

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH who made it (the earth), YHWH who formed it to establish it — YHWH is his name:
verses.note: HEBREW: The verse employs three uses of the divine name in quick succession, emphasizing YHWH's identity as Creator and Establisher. The verb "formed" (yatsar, H3335) is the potter's word used in Gen 2:7 of Adam's creation, connecting this promise of restoration to new-creation theology. "Establish it" (hakhinah) suggests purposeful ordering, not random creation.

verses.flow: Call to me and I will answer you, and I will tell you great and hidden (unsearchable) things that you have not known.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Great and hidden (betsurot) things" — the word betsurot (H1219, from batsar) means inaccessible, fortified, or cut off — things beyond human reach or inquiry. This verse is a paradigmatic call-and-answer covenant promise, standing as the gateway to the remarkable restoration oracles that follow. It implies that divine revelation is the only means of knowing redemptive purposes.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Health and healing" (arukah, H724) literally means new flesh growing over a wound — a vivid metaphor for restoration and regeneration. The pairing of shalom (H7965) and emet (H571, truth/faithfulness) echoes the covenantal attributes of YHWH himself (cf. Ps 85:10). This points forward to the healing ministry of Jesus and the ultimate wholeness of the new creation (Rev 21:4).

verses.flow: And I will cleanse them from all the iniquity by which they have sinned against me, and I will pardon all their iniquities by which they have sinned against me and by which they have transgressed against me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The threefold language of cleansing, forgiving, and pardoning iniquity anticipates the New Covenant promise of Jer 31:34. The Hebrew chata't (sin), avon (iniquity), and pesha (transgression) together form a comprehensive vocabulary of human moral failure, and their complete removal signals the radical nature of the promised redemption.

the sound of joy and the sound of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride, the voices of those who sing, "Give thanks to YHWH of hosts, for YHWH is good, for his steadfast love (hesed) endures forever!" as they bring thank offerings into the house of YHWH. For I will restore the fortunes of the land as at the first, says YHWH.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The liturgical refrain "for his hesed endures forever" echoes Ps 136 and the dedication of Solomon's temple (2 Chr 7:3), suggesting that the restored community will re-enact the founding worship of Israel. The "voice of the bridegroom and the bride" is taken up eschatologically in Rev 18:23 as a marker of divine blessing, and its presence here signals full covenantal restoration. The thank offering (todah, H8426) implies worship out of salvation, not appeasement.

verses.flow: Behold, days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will fulfill the good word that I have spoken concerning the house of Israel and concerning the house of Judah.

verses.flow: In those days and at that time I will cause to sprout up for David a righteous Branch (tsemach), and he shall execute justice (mishpat) and righteousness (tsedaqah) in the land.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Righteous Branch" (tsemach tsaddiq, H6780/H6662) is a technical messianic title appearing also in Jer 23:5, Zech 3:8, and Zech 6:12. The Branch executes mishpat (justice, H4941) and tsedaqah (righteousness, H6666), the twin pillars of YHWH's own throne (Ps 89:14). This is fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the son of David, whose reign is characterized by perfect justice and righteousness (cf. Luke 1:32-33; Heb 1:8).

verses.flow: In those days Judah shall be saved, and Jerusalem shall dwell securely. And this is the name by which she shall be called: "YHWH is our righteousness (tsidqenu)."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Remarkably, the name "YHWH is our righteousness (tsidqenu)" — which in Jer 23:6 is the name of the Branch himself — is here applied to the city Jerusalem. This may indicate that the righteousness of the King is imputed to and embodied in his people and their dwelling place. The Apostle Paul's doctrine of imputed righteousness finds resonance here (2 Cor 5:21; Phil 3:9).

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH: David shall never lack a man to sit on the throne of the house of Israel,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The unconditional and eternal nature of the Davidic covenant is here restated in the face of apparent contradiction — the current king is in captivity. The "never lack a man" formula (lo-yikaret, H3772) is the language of covenant perpetuity, echoing 2 Sam 7:11-16. The NT sees ultimate fulfillment in Christ's eternal Davidic kingship (Acts 2:29-31; Rev 22:16).

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to Jeremiah, saying,
"Thus says YHWH: If you can break my covenant with the day and my covenant with the night, so that day and night will not come at their appointed time,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH grounds the certainty of his covenant promises in the fixed orders of the cosmos — the covenant with "day" and "night." This reflects the ancient Near Eastern cosmological understanding that celestial regularity is divinely decreed and maintained (cf. Ps 89:37; Gen 8:22). The implication is that breaking God's covenant with David would require dismantling creation itself — an impossibility, underscoring absolute covenant faithfulness.

verses.flow: As the host of heaven cannot be numbered and the sand of the sea cannot be measured, so I will multiply the offspring of David my servant and the Levites who minister to me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The dual promise of innumerable offspring for both David and the Levites invokes the patriarchal blessing language of Gen 15:5 and Gen 22:17. This conjunction of royal and priestly lines in one promise is significant: both find their ultimate fulfillment in Jesus Christ, who is simultaneously the Son of David (royal line) and the eternal High Priest after the order of Melchizedek (priestly line) (Heb 7:1-28; Rev 1:6).
