verses.flow: "Have you not observed what this people has spoken, saying, 'The two clans that YHWH chose, he has rejected them'? Thus they have despised my people, so that they are no more a nation in their sight.
Thus says YHWH: If I have not established my covenant with day and night and the fixed orders (chuqqot) of heaven and earth,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Fixed orders (chuqqot) of heaven and earth" points to the cosmic decrees by which YHWH governs creation, language resonant with Second Temple cosmological thought and the notion of heavenly tablets. The stability of the physical cosmos is invoked as the ultimate guarantee of covenant faithfulness, a rhetorical device that pushes the hearer to acknowledge that doubting God's covenant is as absurd as doubting sunrise.

verses.flow: then I will also reject the offspring of Jacob and of David my servant and will not choose from his offspring rulers over the offspring of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. For I will restore their fortunes and will have mercy (racham) on them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The final verse closes the chapter with the threefold patriarchal formula — Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — anchoring the Davidic and Levitical promises firmly within the Abrahamic covenant. The word racham (H7355, mercy/compassion) is the visceral, womb-rooted word for divine compassion, suggesting the most intimate and unconditional form of love. The restoration is not merely political but flows from the deepest covenantal affections of YHWH toward his people.

verses.flow: The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH, when Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and all his army and all the kingdoms of the earth under the dominion of his hand and all the peoples were fighting against Jerusalem and against all its cities, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The historical setting places this oracle during the final Babylonian siege of Jerusalem (588-586 BC), when Nebuchadnezzar's forces had effectively encircled Judah. The phrase "all the kingdoms of the earth under the dominion of his hand" reflects the vast Neo-Babylonian empire's client-king network and echoes ANE conquest ideology in which great kings commanded coalitions of subordinate rulers.

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Go and speak to Zedekiah king of Judah and say to him, Thus says YHWH: Behold, I am giving this city into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall burn it with fire.

And you yourself shall not escape from his hand, but you shall surely be seized and delivered into his hand; and your eyes shall see the eyes of the king of Babylon, and he shall speak with you mouth to mouth, and to Babylon you shall go.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prophecy of Zedekiah's face-to-face meeting with Nebuchadnezzar was precisely fulfilled (Jer 52:10-11; 2 Kgs 25:6-7). YHWH's sovereign foreknowledge of the exact manner of capture — "mouth to mouth" — underscores his absolute governance over the destinies of kings, a central covenantal theme in Jeremiah (cf. Jer 1:10).

verses.flow: Yet hear the word of YHWH, O Zedekiah king of Judah: Thus says YHWH concerning you — you shall not die by the sword.

In shalom (shalom) you shall die; and as the burnings of spices were made for your fathers, the former kings who were before you, so they shall make a burning for you, and they shall lament for you, saying, "Alas, O lord!" For I myself have spoken the word, declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "shalom" (H7965, from the root shalem, meaning wholeness, completeness, peace) here signifies dying without violent end, a qualified mercy. The royal burial spice-burning (serepah) was a distinct honor for Judahite kings (2 Chr 16:14; 21:19). YHWH's personal guarantee — "I myself have spoken" — uses the emphatic first-person pronoun 'ani', stressing divine commitment to even this limited promise of grace to an unfaithful king.

verses.flow: when the army of the king of Babylon was fighting against Jerusalem and against all the remaining cities of Judah — against Lachish and against Azekah, for these were the remaining fortified cities among the cities of Judah.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Lachish and Azekah were the last two fortified Judahite cities still holding out against Babylon, confirmed dramatically by the Lachish Letters (ostraca found in the Lachish gate, early 6th century BC), in which one letter mentions that the signal fires of Azekah could no longer be seen — a chilling archaeological parallel to the precise situation described here.

verses.flow: The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH after King Zedekiah had made a covenant (berit) with all the people who were in Jerusalem to proclaim liberty (deror) to them —
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "deror" (H1865, liberty/release) is the technical term used in Leviticus 25:10 for the Jubilee proclamation of freedom, inscribed also on the Liberty Bell. The "berit" (covenant, H1285) made by Zedekiah invoked the Mosaic slave-release law of Deuteronomy 15:12-18, suggesting a final desperate act of covenant renewal under siege pressure.

This frames the slave-release violation not merely as a social injustice but as covenantal treachery against YHWH himself.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says YHWH: You have not listened to me by proclaiming liberty — each man to his brother and each man to his neighbor. Behold, I am proclaiming to you a liberty, declares YHWH, to the sword, to the pestilence, and to the famine; and I will make you a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine irony of YHWH's judgment is pronounced with devastating literary force: because Judah refused to proclaim "deror" (liberty) to their slaves, YHWH now proclaims a terrifying counter-"deror" — liberty to the sword, pestilence, and famine — the three classic covenant-curse instruments of Mosaic judgment (Lev 26; Deut 28). This judgment-as-ironic-mirror is a hallmark of Jeremianic rhetoric and reflects the lex talionis principle embedded in covenant theology.

verses.flow: And I will give the men who transgressed my covenant — who have not upheld the words of the covenant which they made before me — the calf which they cut in two and passed between its parts:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The covenant ratification rite described here — cutting an animal in two and passing between its halves — is a well-attested ANE treaty ceremony (cf. Gen 15:9-17, where YHWH himself passes through as a smoking firepot). The self-imprecatory meaning is: "May what happened to this animal happen to me if I break this covenant." By violating their oath, Judah's leaders had invited exactly this death-curse upon themselves. Akkadian and Aramaic treaty texts confirm this practice was widespread in the ancient Near East.

verses.flow: I will give them into the hand of their enemies and into the hand of those who seek their lives; and their dead bodies shall be food for the birds of the heavens and for the beasts of the earth.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The imagery of corpses left unburied as food for birds and beasts is a covenant-curse formula appearing in Deuteronomy 28:26 and echoed in Revelation 19:17-21, where the eschatological judgment of the beast and his armies results in a great supper of God for the birds. Jeremiah's oracle thus stands as a typological anticipation of final divine reckoning against those who violate covenant with YHWH.

verses.flow: Behold, I am commanding, declares YHWH, and I will bring them back to this city; and they shall fight against it and capture it and burn it with fire; and I will make the cities of Judah a desolation, without inhabitant."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "I am commanding" uses the participle "metzavveh," indicating YHWH's immediate sovereign decree — a present-future command that cannot be revoked. The withdrawal of Babylonian forces mentioned in verse 21 (likely the temporary retreat to face Egypt, cf. Jer 37:5-8) gave false hope to Jerusalem; YHWH declares their return certain. This underscores a core Jeremianic theme: covenant judgment, once decreed, cannot be averted by human geopolitical maneuvering.

verses.flow: The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH in the days of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Chapter 35 is set during the reign of Jehoiakim (609–598 BC), likely during the Babylonian invasion of 605–604 BC when Nebuchadrezzar's campaign would have driven outlying clans into Jerusalem (cf. v.11). The Rechabites (from Rechab, of the Kenite clan per 1 Chron 2:55) were a semi-nomadic separatist group who maintained strict ancestral disciplines as a form of covenant fidelity. Jeremiah uses their faithful obedience as a prophetic object lesson against Judah's chronic disobedience.

verses.flow: and I brought them to the house of YHWH, into the chamber of the sons of Hanan the son of Igdaliah, the man of God (ish ha-elohim), which was near the chamber of the officials, which was above the chamber of Maaseiah the son of Shallum, keeper of the threshold.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ish ha-elohim" (man of God) is a technical title for a recognized prophet or holy man (cf. 1 Kings 17:18; 2 Kings 4:7). The careful notation of the chamber's location within the Temple complex reflects the precision of an eyewitness account and underscores the setting's sanctity — the test of obedience is conducted in YHWH's own house.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Rechabites' refusal of wine is a form of covenantal loyalty to their ancestral tradition, functioning here as an enacted parable. Their obedience to a merely human father shames Israel's disobedience to the divine Father. This pattern of using human faithfulness to indict divine faithlessness recurs in prophetic rhetoric (cf. Mal 1:6).

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH of hosts (Tzva'ot), the God of Israel: Go and say to the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, 'Will you not receive instruction (musar) by listening to my words? declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Musar" (Strong's H4148) denotes discipline, correction, or moral instruction — carrying the sense of formative chastening rather than mere punishment. It is a central wisdom-tradition term (cf. Prov 1:2–3). YHWH frames His prophetic word as musar that Judah has refused to internalize, making them morally inferior even to pagans and semi-nomads.

verses.flow: The words of Jonadab the son of Rechab, which he commanded his sons to drink no wine, have been carried out — and to this day they drink none, for they have obeyed their father's command. But I have spoken to you persistently and you have not listened to me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "spoken to you persistently" (Hebrew: hashkem ve-daber, literally "rising early and speaking") is a distinctive Jeremianic expression for YHWH's persistent, urgent prophetic communication (appearing some 14 times in Jeremiah). It vividly pictures YHWH as a master who rises at dawn to deliver His message, only to be repeatedly ignored by His servants.

verses.flow: And I have sent to you all my servants the prophets, persistently, saying: "Turn now, every man from his evil way, and amend your deeds, and do not go after other gods to serve them, and then you shall dwell in the land that I gave to you and to your fathers." But you did not incline your ear or listen to me.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The call to "turn now, every man from his evil way" (shuvu na) is the core repentance summons of the prophetic tradition. The verb "shuv" (to return, to turn) is the fundamental Hebrew term for repentance, appearing throughout Jeremiah. The refusal to listen despite repeated prophetic sending anticipates the parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:33–41), where the servants of the owner are rejected before the Son is sent.

verses.flow: Therefore, thus says YHWH, the God of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing upon Judah and upon all the inhabitants of Jerusalem all the disaster that I have spoken against them, because I spoke to them and they did not listen, and I called to them and they did not answer."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine title "God of hosts" (Elohei Tzva'ot) underscores YHWH's sovereignty over all armies — heavenly and earthly — as He pronounces judgment. The judgment formula here — "I spoke and they did not listen, I called and they did not answer" — echoes covenantal curse language rooted in Deuteronomy (Deut 28) and anticipates the lament of Isaiah 65:12. The disaster is not arbitrary but the direct consequence of covenant violation.

verses.flow: But to the house of the Rechabites Jeremiah said, "Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Because you have obeyed the command of Jonadab your father and kept all his statutes and done all that he commanded you,
therefore thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Jonadab the son of Rechab shall not lack a man to stand before me all the days."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The promise that Jonadab's line "shall not lack a man to stand before me" (lo-yikaret ish) echoes the Davidic covenant formula (Jer 33:17–18; 1 Kings 2:4), typically reserved for royal or priestly continuity. Applied here to a clan of faithful lay obedience, it democratizes covenant blessing: fidelity to divine instruction — even mediated through a human father — is rewarded with perpetual standing in YHWH's presence. This stands as a counter-testimony to Judah's imminent judgment.

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the fourth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, this word came to Jeremiah from YHWH, saying,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "fourth year of Jehoiakim" (ca. 605 BC) coincides precisely with the Battle of Carchemish, when Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt and Assyria, fundamentally altering the Ancient Near Eastern political landscape. This watershed moment provides the urgent backdrop for YHWH's command to dictate the scroll. THEOLOGICAL: The divine command to write represents a pivotal moment of inscripturation — YHWH's spoken word being committed to permanent written form, reflecting the covenantal practice of written testimony established at Sinai (Exod 34:27).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: "Take for yourself a scroll of a book, and write upon it all the words which I have spoken to you concerning Israel, and concerning Judah, and concerning all the nations, from the day I spoke to you — from the days of Josiah — even until this day.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Scroll of a book" (megillat-sefer, H4039 + H5612) refers to a papyrus or leather scroll, the standard written medium of the period. The phrase "from the day I spoke to you" encompasses roughly 23 years of prophetic ministry beginning under Josiah, underscoring the comprehensive scope of the dictation. THEOLOGICAL: This commission reflects the pattern of the prophetic word being preserved against future fulfillment — the written word outlasting the spoken moment (Isa 30:8).

verses.flow: And Jeremiah called Baruch the son of Neriah, and Baruch wrote from the mouth of Jeremiah all the words of YHWH which He had spoken to him upon a scroll of a book.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "from the mouth of Jeremiah" (mi-pi Yirmeyahu) is a technical scribal formula indicating oral dictation, paralleled in ancient Near Eastern scribal practice. Baruch ben Neriah is attested extra-biblically — a clay bulla (seal impression) discovered in the 20th century bears the inscription "Berekhyahu son of Neriyahu the scribe," providing remarkable archaeological confirmation of this figure. TYPOLOGY: Baruch functions as a type of the faithful scribe who preserves divine revelation, anticipating the role of apostolic scribes in the New Testament.

verses.note: HEBREW: "Supplication" translates techinnah (H8467), from the root chanan meaning to show favor or be gracious. The term denotes an earnest plea cast before a superior, carrying connotations of utter dependence. "Wrath" (chemah, H2534) literally means heat or burning intensity, vividly conveying the fervor of divine displeasure. THEOLOGICAL: The passage reveals the evangelistic purpose behind prophetic judgment — the proclamation of wrath is itself an act of mercy designed to produce repentance, reflecting YHWH's character as one who does not delight in the death of the wicked (Ezek 18:23).

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the fifth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, in the ninth month, all the people in Jerusalem and all the people who came from the cities of Judah to Jerusalem proclaimed a fast before YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "ninth month" of the Jewish calendar corresponds to November-December (Kislev), a cold season, explaining the fire pot burning in verse 22. The fast proclaimed may have been connected to news of Nebuchadnezzar's military campaigns following Carchemish. Public fasting was a recognized penitential practice in ancient Israel (Joel 1:14, 2:15), typically involving cessation of food and water from sunrise to sunset. THEOLOGICAL: The communal gathering of "all the people in Jerusalem and all the people who came from the cities of Judah" signals the universal summons of covenant accountability.

verses.flow: And Baruch read from the book the words of Jeremiah in the house of YHWH, in the chamber of Gemariah the son of Shaphan the scribe, in the upper court at the entrance of the new gate of the house of YHWH, in the ears of all the people.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gemariah the son of Shaphan occupies a prominent chamber in the temple complex. Shaphan the scribe had been a key figure in Josiah's reformation (2 Kgs 22:8-14), and his family line shows sustained sympathy toward Jeremiah throughout this period. The "upper court" and "new gate" indicate Baruch read at a place of maximum public visibility within the sacred precinct.

verses.flow: And it came to pass, when they had heard all the words, they turned one to another in dread, and they said to Baruch, "We must surely declare to the king all these words."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The officials' reaction of mutual dread (pachad, trembling fear) demonstrates that the word of YHWH carried intrinsic power and authority even before pagan or compromised hearers. Their instinct to report to the king reflects their recognition of the words' gravity, though they ultimately failed to act on this conviction with sustained courage. HEBREW: The idiom "they turned one to another" (panah ish el-re'ehu) conveys a shared moment of stunned reckoning, a collective pause before the weight of the divine oracle.

verses.flow: Now the king was sitting in the winter house in the ninth month, and the fire pot was burning before him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "winter house" (beit ha-choreph) likely refers to a south-facing royal apartment designed to capture warmth during the cold months, possibly a separate wing or adjacent structure to the summer palace. The fire pot (ach, H249) was a brazier used for heating enclosed spaces. This domestic detail serves to contrast the king's physical comfort with the burning urgency of the divine word he is about to destroy.

verses.flow: And it came to pass, when Jehudi had read three or four columns, he would cut it with the scribe's knife and throw it into the fire that was in the fire pot, until all the scroll was consumed in the fire that was in the fire pot.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The act of cutting and burning the scroll column by column represents one of the most dramatic acts of royal defiance against the word of YHWH in the entire biblical narrative. Unlike his grandfather Josiah, who tore his garments upon hearing the law (2 Kgs 22:11), Jehoiakim tears the scroll itself — an inversion of covenant humility. TYPOLOGY: The futility of destroying the written word anticipates the indestructibility of God's word more broadly (Isa 40:8; Matt 24:35; 1 Pet 1:25), since YHWH commands the immediate rewriting of the scroll.

verses.flow: And they were not afraid, and they did not tear their garments — neither the king nor any of his servants who were hearing all these words.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The conspicuous absence of mourning — "they did not tear their garments" — stands in deliberate literary contrast to Josiah's response (2 Kgs 22:19) and to the officials' own initial dread (v. 16). This hardness of heart fulfills the pattern of covenant apostasy described in Deuteronomy 28-30 and signals that the point of no return for this generation of leadership has been reached.

verses.flow: And even though Elnathan and Delaiah and Gemariah had urged the king not to burn the scroll, he did not listen to them.

verses.flow: And the king commanded Jerahmeel the son of the king, and Seraiah the son of Azriel, and Shelemiah the son of Abdeel to take Baruch the scribe and Jeremiah the prophet, but YHWH hid them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "YHWH hid them" (yastirem YHWH) is a brief but theologically loaded statement affirming divine providential protection over the prophet and his scribe. Jeremiah's preservation despite active royal pursuit demonstrates that YHWH guards His messenger until the prophetic word is fully delivered — a pattern seen throughout the prophetic corpus (1 Kgs 18:4; Acts 12:7). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The unseen protection suggests the operation of heavenly agency in concealing the prophets from royal authority.

verses.flow: And concerning Jehoiakim king of Judah you shall say, 'Thus says YHWH: You have burned this scroll, saying, "Why have you written upon it, saying, 'The king of Babylon will surely come and destroy this land and will cause to cease from it man and beast'?"
Therefore thus says YHWH concerning Jehoiakim king of Judah: He shall have no one sitting upon the throne of David, and his corpse shall be cast out to the heat by day and to the frost by night.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The oracle that Jehoiakim "shall have no one sitting upon the throne of David" finds its qualified fulfillment in his son Jehoiachin (Coniah), who reigned only three months before being carried to Babylon (2 Kgs 24:8-12). This curse is echoed and intensified in Jeremiah's oracle against Coniah in Jer 22:30. THEOLOGICAL: The curse upon Jehoiakim's dynasty stands in stark contrast to the eternal Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16), highlighting that covenant faithlessness forfeits dynastic blessing even while the ultimate promise awaits its fulfillment in the greater Son of David (Luke 1:32-33).

verses.flow: And Jeremiah took another scroll and gave it to Baruch the son of Neriah the scribe, and he wrote upon it from the mouth of Jeremiah all the words of the book which Jehoiakim king of Judah had burned in the fire; and there were added to them still many words like these.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The reconstituted scroll — with "many words like these" added — demonstrates the inexhaustibility and indestructibility of the divine word. Human opposition to revelation does not diminish it but occasions its expansion. TYPOLOGY: This event foreshadows the pattern of persecution and textual preservation across redemptive history, culminating in the canon of Scripture itself surviving centuries of opposition. STRUCTURE: The chapter forms a chiastic narrative arc: divine word given → written → publicly read → officially rejected → divinely reaffirmed and expanded, underscoring that the word of YHWH stands forever (Isa 40:8).

verses.note: HISTORICAL: Coniah (Jeconiah/Jehoiachin) was the son of Jehoiakim and preceded Zedekiah as king; Nebuchadnezzar installed Zedekiah (Mattaniah) as a puppet king after deporting Jeconiah in 597 B.C. (2 Kgs 24:17). The curse on Coniah's line (Jer 22:30) creates a crucial theological tension resolved only through the virgin birth of Messiah, who inherits the legal Davidic right through Joseph while avoiding the blood curse.

verses.flow: Then Zedekiah the king sent and took him out, and the king asked him secretly in his house, and said, "Is there a word from YHWH?" And Jeremiah said, "There is!" And he said, "Into the hand of the king of Babylon you shall be given over."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Zedekiah's secret consultation reveals the tension between political fear of his officials and a lingering, private regard for YHWH's word. The question "Is there a word from YHWH?" (ha-yesh dabar me-et YHWH) implies genuine anxiety. Jeremiah's unflinching answer — delivering the same unwelcome oracle despite his imprisonment — demonstrates prophetic courage and integrity (cf. 1 Kgs 22:14).

verses.flow: Moreover Jeremiah said to King Zedekiah, "In what way have I sinned against you, or against your servants, or against this people, that you have put me into the house of the prison?
And where now are your prophets who prophesied to you, saying, 'The king of Babylon will not come against you, nor against this land'?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a forensic challenge to the false prophets who had promised peace and safety (shalom, H7965). Their silence and absence at the moment of crisis expose the hollowness of their oracles. Jeremiah implicitly invokes the test of true prophecy from Deut 18:21-22 — the word that does not come to pass is not from YHWH.

verses.note: HEBREW: Tachanunati (H8467, from chanan — to be gracious, to implore) denotes an earnest, humble petition for mercy or favor, distinct from a formal legal appeal. The verb "let it fall before you" (tippol-na) pictures the suppliant prostrating himself before the king. This moment of Jeremiah's vulnerability humanizes the prophet and foreshadows the suffering servant who also appeals to those with power over him.

verses.flow: So Zedekiah the king commanded, and they committed Jeremiah to the court of the guard, and they gave him a loaf of bread each day from the bakers' street, until all the bread in the city was gone. And Jeremiah remained in the court of the guard.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "court of the guard" (chatsar ha-mattarah) was an open area within the palace complex, a far less severe confinement than the dungeon cistern of Jonathan's house. The provision of a daily bread ration from the bakers' street reflects actual administrative practice known from ANE palace records. This detail also signals that even in judgment, YHWH preserves His prophet — a pattern running from Joseph to Daniel to Paul.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: The four officials named here — Shephatiah, Gedaliah, Jucal, and Pashhur — represent the political and priestly establishment of Jerusalem's final days. Jucal (also spelled Jehucal) appears earlier in Jer 37:3 sent by Zedekiah to Jeremiah. The naming of opponents was a common literary device in ancient Near Eastern court narratives establishing witnesses to prophetic fulfillment.

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH: Whoever remains in this city shall die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence; but whoever goes out to the Chaldeans shall live, and his life shall be to him as plunder, and he shall live.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeremiah's message of survival through surrender was theologically scandalous — it reframed capitulation to Babylon as the path of covenant faithfulness rather than resistance. This mirrors the broader Deuteronomic theology of Jeremiah: obedience brings life, rebellion brings death (Deut 30:15-19). The phrase "his life shall be to him as plunder" (lô leśālāl) indicates life preserved against all odds, as if snatched from enemy hands.

verses.flow: Then the officials said to the king, "Let this man be put to death, for indeed he is weakening the hands of the men of war who remain in this city, and the hands of all the people, by speaking such words to them; for this man is not seeking the welfare (shalom) of this people, but their harm."
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "weakening the hands" (merapheh et-yedê) is a well-attested ancient idiom for demoralizing troops, confirmed in the Lachish Letters (Letter VI) from this very period, where a commander complains that the same phrase is being used by officials in Jerusalem. The term shalom here is ironic — the officials accuse Jeremiah of not seeking the city's peace, but Jeremiah's message IS the true path to shalom through submission.

verses.flow: So they took Jeremiah and cast him into the cistern of Malchiah the son of the king, which was in the court of the guard. They let Jeremiah down with ropes. And in the cistern there was no water, but only mud, and Jeremiah sank in the mud.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The casting of Jeremiah into a muddy cistern with ropes, from which he is later raised up, carries typological resonance with the pit narratives of Joseph (Gen 37:24) and anticipates the burial and resurrection of Christ. The cistern (bôr) is also a Hebrew idiom for the grave/Sheol (Ps 28:1; Isa 38:18). Jeremiah's deliverance prefigures YHWH's faithfulness to raise His servant from the depths.

His name is ironic: he proves to be a true servant of the true King (YHWH) by protecting YHWH's prophet. YHWH later promises personal deliverance to Ebed-melech specifically because of his trust (Jer 39:15-18), modeling the principle that faith in YHWH transcends ethnic and social boundaries.

verses.flow: Then King Zedekiah swore secretly to Jeremiah, saying, "As YHWH lives, who made this soul (nephesh) for us, I will not put you to death, nor will I give you into the hand of these men who seek your life."
verses.note: HEBREW: The oath formula "as YHWH lives" (chai-YHWH) is the most solemn form of oath in the Hebrew Bible, invoking the living God as guarantor. The phrase "who made this soul (nephesh) for us" is remarkable — it acknowledges YHWH as the direct creator of personal life (Gen 2:7), making the oath both covenantal and creational in its appeal. Nephesh (Strong's H5315) here means the animating life-principle, not merely "soul" in its later Greek sense.

verses.flow: And Jeremiah said to Zedekiah, "Thus says YHWH, the God of hosts (tsevaot), the God of Israel: If you will indeed go out to the officials of the king of Babylon, then your soul (nephesh) shall live, and this city shall not be burned with fire, and you and your house shall live.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The title "YHWH, God of hosts (tsevaot)" invokes the divine warrior commanding heavenly armies — a reminder that behind the political-military crisis stands the LORD of all cosmic and earthly forces. The conditional structure of Jeremiah's message (if you go out… then you shall live) reflects the covenant lawsuit (rîb) pattern throughout Jeremiah, where YHWH still extends mercy before final judgment falls. (Deut 30:19; Jer 21:8-9)

verses.flow: And Jeremiah remained in the court of the guard until the day that Jerusalem was captured. And he was there when Jerusalem was captured.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse serves as a structural hinge between chapter 38 and the fall of Jerusalem narrative in chapter 39. The phrase "until the day Jerusalem was captured" appears twice — a deliberate Hebrew repetition (inclusio) that both closes this episode and opens the next, linking Jeremiah's personal preservation in the court of the guard to the fulfillment of every word he had prophesied. His survival in custody is itself a prophetic sign.

verses.flow: In the ninth year of Zedekiah king of Judah, in the tenth month, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and all his army came against Jerusalem and besieged it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The siege of Jerusalem described here began in 588 BC (the ninth year of Zedekiah) and concluded in 586 BC with the city's fall. This chronology aligns with the Babylonian Chronicle and constitutes one of the most historically verified events in the Hebrew Bible. The "tenth month" corresponds roughly to January, the season when ancient armies commonly initiated siege warfare.

verses.flow: And all the officials (sarim) of the king of Babylon came and sat in the middle gate: Nergal-sar-ezer, Samgar-nebo, Sar-sechim the Rab-saris, Nergal-sar-ezer the Rab-mag, and all the rest of the officials of the king of Babylon.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The listing of Babylonian officials with their titles provides remarkable historical specificity. "Rab-saris" (Hebrew: rav-saris) means "chief officer" (possibly "chief eunuch"), while "Rab-mag" likely denotes a high court official, perhaps chief of the magi or diviners. Archaeological cuneiform tablets from Babylon have confirmed the name Nergal-sar-ezer (Neriglissar), who later became king of Babylon (559-556 BC), lending strong historical credibility to this account.

verses.flow: But the army of the Chaldeans pursued them and overtook Zedekiah in the plains of Jericho; and they seized him and brought him up to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, at Riblah in the land of Hamath, and he pronounced judgment (mishpatim) upon him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Zedekiah's capture and judgment at Riblah fulfills YHWH's repeated warnings delivered through Jeremiah (Jer 32:4-5; 34:3). The term "mishpatim" (judgments) underscores that what Nebuchadnezzar executed was not merely political conquest but YHWH's covenantal discipline upon a faithless king. Riblah, in the land of Hamath, was a strategic command post used by both Pharaoh Necho (2 Kgs 23:33) and Nebuchadnezzar as a northern base of operations.

verses.flow: And the king of Babylon slaughtered the sons of Zedekiah before his eyes at Riblah; also all the nobles (chorim) of Judah the king of Babylon slaughtered.

verses.flow: And the eyes of Zedekiah he blinded, and he bound him with bronze fetters to bring him to Babylon.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The blinding of Zedekiah is noted alongside the earlier prophecy of Ezekiel (Ezek 12:13), which declared he would "not see" Babylon though he would "go there" — a tension reconciled perfectly by this verse. Zedekiah's fate stands in stark contrast to David's son whom YHWH would establish; kingship that abandons covenant ends in blindness and exile, while the ultimate Davidic heir, Jesus the Messiah, restores sight to the blind (Luke 4:18; Isa 61:1).

verses.flow: And the rest of the people who remained in the city, and the deserters who had deserted to him, and the rest of the people who remained — Nebuzaradan the commander of the guard (rab-tabachim) exiled to Babylon.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Rab-tabachim" (commander of the guard) literally means "chief of the slaughterers/executioners," from the root "tabach" (to slaughter). The term originally referred to royal executioners and later to palace guards. Nebuzaradan holds this title throughout the fall narrative, appearing as the primary agent of Babylonian judgment. The exile described here constitutes the climactic fulfillment of the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:36, 64.

verses.flow: But some of the poor people who had nothing, Nebuzaradan the commander of the guard left in the land of Judah, and gave them vineyards and fields on that day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The retention and provision for the poor of the land reflects a recurring biblical pattern in which YHWH preserves a remnant in the land even amid judgment. This echoes the covenant concern for the anawim (the humble/poor) and anticipates the eschatological inheritance promised to the meek (Ps 37:11; Matt 5:5). The land distribution to the poor also stands as an ironic reversal of the social injustices the prophets condemned in Judah.

verses.flow: Now the word of YHWH had come to Jeremiah while he was still confined in the court of the guard, saying,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 15-18 constitute a retrospective insertion, returning to an oracle delivered to Ebed-melech before the city's fall (see Jer 38:7-13, where Ebed-melech rescued Jeremiah from the cistern). This literary technique of flashback is characteristic of Jeremiah's complex narrative structure. The Septuagint version of Jeremiah places this material in a different order, illustrating the significant text-critical differences between the Hebrew MT and Greek traditions.

verses.flow: "Go and speak to Ebed-melech the Cushite, saying: Thus says YHWH of hosts (tseva'ot), the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing my words upon this city for disaster and not for good, and they shall be accomplished before you on that day.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "YHWH of hosts" (YHWH tseva'ot) invokes the divine warrior and commander of heavenly armies, underscoring that the coming disaster is not merely Babylonian military action but the advance of YHWH's heavenly host executing covenant judgment. This title appears over 280 times in the Hebrew Bible and carries the connotation of YHWH presiding over and deploying both celestial and terrestrial armies in accomplishment of his sovereign purposes.

verses.flow: But I will deliver you on that day, declares YHWH, and you shall not be given into the hand of the men of whom you are afraid.

verses.flow: For I will surely rescue you, and by the sword you shall not fall, but your life shall be to you as a prize of war (shalal), because you have trusted in me, declares YHWH."
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "your life shall be to you as a prize of war" uses the word "shalal" (Strong's H7998), meaning spoil or plunder taken in battle. The idiom recurs in Jeremiah (21:9; 38:2; 45:5) as a promise of bare but certain survival — one escapes with nothing more than one's life, as a soldier escaping with only the plunder. THEOLOGICAL: Ebed-melech's deliverance is explicitly grounded in his trust (batach) in YHWH, making him a Gentile exemplar of saving faith within the covenant community's darkest hour — a typological anticipation of Gentile inclusion in the new covenant community through faith (Rom 4:11; Gal 3:8).

verses.flow: The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH, after Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard had let him go from Ramah, when he had taken him bound in chains among all the exiles of Jerusalem and Judah who were being exiled to Babylon.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ramah (modern er-Ram, north of Jerusalem) served as a staging point where Babylonian forces assembled Judean captives for deportation. The scene of Jeremiah's release here is charged with irony — the very prophet who had counseled submission to Babylon is among the chained, only to be released by the pagan commander himself. THEOLOGICAL: This chapter opens with a superscription-like formula ("The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH"), anchoring even political events within the framework of divine revelation and sovereign orchestration.

verses.flow: The captain of the guard took Jeremiah and said to him, "YHWH your God spoke this disaster against this place.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Nebuzaradan's speech is remarkable — a Babylonian military officer accurately articulates YHWH's covenant judgment upon Jerusalem. This parallels texts such as Isaiah 10:5-7, where a foreign power is unwittingly the instrument of YHWH's will. The pagan's confession of YHWH's action underscores the universal lordship of Israel's God over the nations.

verses.flow: And now, behold, I release you today from the chains that are upon your hands. If it seems good to you to come with me to Babylon, come, and I will set my eye upon you. But if it seems evil to you to come with me to Babylon, do not come. See, all the land is before you; wherever it seems good and right to you to go, go there."
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "I will set my eye upon you" (Hebrew: wesimti et-eyniy alecha) is a covenant idiom expressing protective watchfulness (cf. Amos 9:4, where the same phrase describes divine judgment in reverse). The offer of free passage anywhere in the land reflects both genuine imperial magnanimity and the fulfillment of YHWH's earlier promise to preserve Jeremiah's life (Jer 1:8, 19).

verses.flow: Now when all the commanders of the forces in the open country and their men heard that the king of Babylon had appointed Gedaliah the son of Ahikam over the land, and had entrusted to him men, women, and children — even from the poorest of the land who had not been exiled to Babylon —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "The commanders of the forces in the open country" (Hebrew: sarey hachayalim) refers to guerrilla units of Judean soldiers who had avoided Babylonian capture by taking refuge in the countryside and in neighboring territories. This verse reflects the fragmented social conditions following the fall of Jerusalem in 586 BC, with scattered military remnants and displaced civilians across the entire region.

verses.flow: And Gedaliah the son of Ahikam, son of Shaphan, swore to them and to their men, saying, "Do not be afraid to serve the Chaldeans; dwell in the land and serve the king of Babylon, and it shall be well (tov) with you.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word tov (H2896, "good, well, pleasant") here carries covenantal weight — Gedaliah promises communal flourishing under submission. His exhortation echoes Jeremiah's earlier letter to the exiles (Jer 29:5-7), urging them to "seek the shalom (welfare) of the city." THEOLOGICAL: Gedaliah's governorship represents a fragile opportunity for covenant remnant-survival in the land, a theme central to the Deuteronomistic theology of land, exile, and restoration.

verses.flow: As for me, behold, I will dwell at Mizpah to stand before the Chaldeans who come to us. But you, gather wine and summer fruit and oil and put them in your vessels, and dwell in your cities that you have taken."

Likewise, when all the Judeans who were in Moab and among the Ammonites and in Edom and who were in all the lands heard that the king of Babylon had left a remnant in Judah and had appointed over them Gedaliah the son of Ahikam, son of Shaphan,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The return of scattered Judeans from Moab, Ammon, Edom, and other lands foreshadows the great eschatological ingathering promised in Jeremiah 31 and 32. This small-scale return is a typological anticipation of the ultimate restoration of Israel, which the New Testament understands as fulfilled both in the return from Babylon and proleptically in the messianic age (cf. Matt 24:31; Rom 11:26). HISTORICAL: The presence of Judean refugees in these specific nations reflects well-documented ANE patterns of population displacement, also attested in extra-biblical sources such as the Elephantine papyri from Egypt.

verses.flow: and said to him, "Do you at all know that Baalis the king of the Ammonites has sent Ishmael the son of Nethaniah to take your life?" But Gedaliah the son of Ahikam did not believe them.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Baalis, king of the Ammonites, is attested in an ancient seal inscription ("Milkom'ur, servant of Ba'alyasha") discovered at Tell el-Umeiri, providing rare extra-biblical confirmation of the biblical account. His motivation for eliminating Gedaliah was likely to destabilize the remaining Judean population and extend Ammonite territorial influence into the vacuum left by Babylon's destruction.

verses.flow: Then Johanan the son of Kareah spoke secretly to Gedaliah at Mizpah, saying, "Please let me go and strike down Ishmael the son of Nethaniah, and no one will know it. Why should he take your life, so that all the Judeans who are gathered to you would be scattered, and the remnant of Judah would perish?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Johanan's proposal, though practically reasonable in outcome, represents the recurring human temptation to secure God's purposes through unsanctioned means. Gedaliah's refusal, rooted in a tragic trust, ultimately leads to the destruction of the very remnant he sought to preserve (Jer 41). This tension between providential realism and moral restraint is a perennial theme in prophetic literature. TYPOLOGY: The vulnerable remnant in the land, threatened by conspirators from within and without, reflects the condition of the faithful people of God in every age, whose preservation depends ultimately not on human strategy but on divine faithfulness.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: But Gedaliah the son of Ahikam said to Johanan the son of Kareah, "You shall not do this thing, for you are speaking falsely (sheger) of Ishmael."
verses.note: HEBREW: The word sheger (H8267, "falsehood, lie, deception") appears frequently in Jeremiah as a theological category — false prophecy, false peace, and false trust are all condemned with this term (cf. Jer 5:2; 6:13; 7:4). Gedaliah's tragic accusation of Johanan reveals not moral relativism but misplaced trust, a cautionary example of naïve leadership in a time of political crisis. THEOLOGICAL: The chapter ends in unresolved tension — the warning is given, refused, and the reader is left anticipating the catastrophe of chapter 41 that will dissolve this fragile remnant community.

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the seventh month, that Ishmael the son of Nethaniah, the son of Elishama, of the royal seed, and of the chief officers of the king — even ten men with him — came to Gedaliah the son of Ahikam at Mizpah. And they ate bread together there at Mizpah.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "seventh month" is Tishri (September/October), placing this massacre during or near the festivals of the Day of Atonement and Tabernacles — a bitter irony. Ishmael's royal lineage made him a rival claimant to leadership; his connection to Baalis king of Ammon (Jer 40:14) suggests this was a politically motivated assassination funded by a foreign power seeking to destabilize the Babylonian-appointed governance of Judah.

verses.flow: that there came men from Shechem, from Shiloh, and from Samaria, eighty men, with their beards shaved and their garments torn, and having cut themselves — with grain offerings (minchah) and incense in their hand — to bring them to the house of YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "minchah" (מִנְחָה, Strong's H4503) refers to a grain or tribute offering. The self-mutilation, shaved beards, and torn garments are signs of mourning — likely for the destruction of the Temple (586 BC). These men were making a pilgrimage to the ruins of the Temple mount, demonstrating that even amid national catastrophe, a remnant maintained their devotion to YHWH.

verses.flow: And it came to pass when they came into the midst of the city that Ishmael the son of Nethaniah slaughtered them — into the pit — he and the men who were with him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The casting of bodies into a pit (bor) was an act of profound dishonor in the ancient Near East, denying proper burial rites. The deceptive weeping of Ishmael in verse 6 mirrors the duplicity of those who use religious or emotional pretense to perpetrate violence — a theme Jeremiah consistently confronts in those who profess covenant loyalty while practicing treachery.

verses.flow: Now the pit into which Ishmael had cast all the corpses of the men whom he had struck down on account of Gedaliah — it was the great pit that King Asa had made because of Baasha king of Israel; that pit Ishmael the son of Nethaniah filled with the slain.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The pit (bor) constructed by King Asa (1 Kings 15:22) during his fortification of Mizpah against Baasha of Israel now becomes a mass grave — a grim repurposing of military infrastructure for atrocity. This detail anchors the narrative to verifiable Israelite history and lends the text its characteristically precise historical texture.

verses.flow: Then Ishmael took captive all the remnant of the people who were in Mizpah — the daughters of the king and all the people who remained in Mizpah, whom Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard had committed to Gedaliah the son of Ahikam. Ishmael the son of Nethaniah took them captive and set out to cross over to the Ammonites.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "daughters of the king" and the surviving remnant represent the final thread of continuity with the Davidic household in the land. Their capture and intended deportation to Ammon signals the near-total dissolution of the post-destruction community that Babylon had sought to preserve. The scattering of even this remnant foreshadows the flight to Egypt that follows in chapters 42-44, setting the stage for YHWH's warning against that course.

verses.flow: Then Johanan the son of Kareah and all the commanders of the forces who were with him took all the remnant of the people whom he had recovered from Ishmael the son of Nethaniah, from Mizpah — after he had struck down Gedaliah the son of Ahikam — mighty men of war, and the women, and the children, and the court officials, whom he had brought back from Gibeon.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The recovery of the captives by Johanan is a small act of preservation within a narrative of cascading catastrophe. The list — warriors, women, children, court officials — reflects the cross-section of the surviving community. Johanan's rescue, though well-intentioned, is overshadowed by his subsequent disobedience to YHWH's word (Jer 43:4), showing that human deliverance without divine obedience ultimately leads to further ruin.

verses.flow: And they went and settled in Geruth Chimham, which is near Bethlehem, to set out to go to Egypt,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Geruth Chimham" (גֵּרוּת כִּמְהָם) likely refers to an estate associated with Chimham, son of Barzillai the Gileadite, who received land near Bethlehem as a reward from David (2 Sam 19:37-38). Bethlehem's proximity to Egypt made it a logical staging point. The choice signals the group's intent to flee YHWH's appointed land — an act that the following chapters will frame as direct disobedience to the divine command.

verses.flow: because of the Chaldeans; for they were afraid of them, because Ishmael the son of Nethaniah had struck down Gedaliah the son of Ahikam, whom the king of Babylon had appointed over the land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The fear of Chaldean reprisal for the assassination of Gedaliah drives the remnant toward Egypt — a theologically laden destination representing the inversion of the Exodus and covenant faithfulness. Ironically, the very act of political violence committed by Ishmael now endangers the innocent remnant. This demonstrates how covenant rupture and human treachery produce exile not only geographically but spiritually, as the people move away from the land YHWH promised to restore to them (Jer 32:37-44).

verses.flow: and said to Jeremiah the prophet, "Let our supplication (techinnah) fall before you, and pray for us to YHWH your God, even for all this remnant — for we are left but a few of many, as your eyes do see us —
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "techinnah" (Strong's H8467, from chanan, to be gracious) denotes a supplication for grace, often used in contexts of desperate pleading before a superior. The phrase "let our supplication fall before you" is a Hebraism expressing humble entreaty, casting themselves prostrate in petition. The designation of this group as a "remnant" (she'erit, H7611) is theologically significant, as it connects to the prophetic theme of a preserved remnant through judgment (Isa 10:20-22; Rom 9:27).

verses.flow: Whether it is good or evil, we will obey the voice of YHWH our God to whom we are sending you, in order that it may go well with us when we obey the voice of YHWH our God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The people's vow is expressed with a remarkable comprehensiveness — "whether it is good or evil, we will obey." This unconditional pledge ironically contrasts with their subsequent disobedience (v. 13ff.), demonstrating the human tendency to make grand covenantal promises without true intention to keep them. The tragedy of this chapter is that the people had already determined their course before seeking the word of YHWH, a pattern of false inquiry condemned throughout the prophets (Ezek 14:3-4).

verses.flow: And it came to pass at the end of ten days that the word of YHWH came to Jeremiah.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The ten-day interval between the request and the divine response is notable. Jeremiah does not manufacture an immediate oracle but genuinely waits upon YHWH. This period of waiting would have created tension among the refugees, who were likely eager to flee to Egypt and may have viewed the delay with impatience. It underscores Jeremiah's prophetic integrity — the word comes in YHWH's time, not man's schedule.

verses.flow: 'If you will indeed remain in this land, then I will build you up and not tear you down, and I will plant you and not uproot you; for I relent (nacham) concerning the disaster that I have brought upon you.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "nacham" (Strong's H5162) is rendered "I relent" or "I am moved to compassion." It is an anthropopathic expression describing YHWH's genuine responsiveness to the changed circumstances of his people. The agricultural metaphors "build/tear down, plant/uproot" echo Jeremiah's foundational commission (Jer 1:10), indicating that the hour of uprooting is past and the hour of planting can now begin — if the people will trust YHWH and remain in the land.

verses.flow: Do not fear the king of Babylon, of whom you are afraid; do not fear him, declares YHWH, for I am with you to save you and to deliver you from his hand.

verses.flow: And I will grant you compassion (rachamim), and he will have compassion on you and restore you to your own soil.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Rachamim" (Strong's H7356, plural of rechem, womb) denotes the deepest emotional compassion, etymologically connected to the word for womb, evoking a mother's tender love for a child. YHWH promises to "give" (natan) them this compassion — a sovereign grant of favor — so that even Nebuchadnezzar would show them mercy. This is covenant-kindness operating even through a pagan king, paralleling Joseph's experience in Egypt (Gen 39:21) and Daniel's in Babylon (Dan 1:9).

verses.flow: then hear now the word of YHWH, O remnant of Judah: Thus says YHWH of hosts (Tzva'ot), the God of Israel: If you indeed set your faces to enter Egypt and go to sojourn there,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH is here addressed as "YHWH Tzva'ot" — YHWH of Hosts or Armies — invoking his role as commander of the heavenly armies and the divine council (Ps 82:1; 1 Kgs 22:19). This title appears at moments of solemn prophetic declaration, reminding the hearers that behind the human affairs of Egypt and Babylon stands the sovereign general of all cosmic forces. No earthly army or king can provide the security that YHWH Tzva'ot alone commands.

verses.flow: then the sword that you fear shall overtake you there in the land of Egypt, and the famine of which you are afraid shall follow close after you there in Egypt, and there you shall die.

verses.flow: And all the men who set their faces to go to Egypt to sojourn there shall die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence; they shall have no survivor or fugitive from the disaster that I will bring upon them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triple judgment formula "sword, famine, and pestilence" (cherev, ra'av, dever) is a Jeremianic refrain that echoes the covenant curse structure of Deuteronomy 28 (Deut 28:21-22, 25). By fleeing to Egypt to escape these very judgments, the remnant would only meet them in a foreign land, cut off from the covenantal promises attached to the land of Israel. Flight from YHWH's discipline leads not to safety but to heightened judgment — a theological axiom illustrated also in the book of Jonah.

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH of hosts (Tzva'ot), the God of Israel: As my anger and my wrath were poured out upon the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so my wrath shall be poured out on you when you go to Egypt. And you shall become a curse (alah), an object of horror, a byword, and a taunt, and you shall see this place no more.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "alah" (Strong's H423) refers to an oath of cursing or imprecation, sometimes translated "execration." To become an "alah" means to be cited as a proverbial example of divine curse, so that others would say "may you become like them" when pronouncing a curse. The fourfold description — curse, horror, byword (shammah), taunt (cherepah) — echoes the Deuteronomic curse vocabulary of national apostasy (Deut 28:37) and has already been applied to Jerusalem (Jer 24:9), now threatened upon those who flee to Egypt.

verses.flow: YHWH has spoken concerning you, O remnant of Judah: "Do not go to Egypt." Know certainly that I have warned you this day.

verses.flow: For you have gone astray at the cost of your lives, for you yourselves sent me to YHWH your God, saying, "Pray for us to YHWH our God, and whatever YHWH our God says, declare to us and we will do it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 20 contains a solemn indictment using the rare phrase translated "you have gone astray at the cost of your lives" (literally "you have erred against your souls"). The Hebrew "ta'ah" (H8582) means to wander or go astray, as sheep going off the path. Jeremiah declares that their self-deception in seeking divine counsel while already committed to their own plan constitutes a fatal error — they have brought destruction upon themselves by their own willful deception. This verse is a profound warning against the sin of seeking God's word with a predetermined answer already in mind (cf. Ezek 20:3).

verses.flow: And I have declared it to you this day, but you have not obeyed the voice of YHWH your God in anything that he sent me to tell you.
Now therefore know certainly that by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence you shall die in the place where you desire to go to sojourn there.'"'
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with one of Jeremiah's most solemn and final warnings, sealing the fate of those who refuse to hear. The certainty expressed — "know certainly" (yado' teda') — is the same intensified infinitive absolute construction used throughout Hebrew prophecy to underscore absolute divine certainty. The promised blessings of v. 10-12 are now fully foreclosed. This closing verse sets the stage for chapter 43, where the remnant's defiant disobedience is confirmed, and Jeremiah and Baruch are taken to Egypt against their will — a tragic fulfillment of the very apostasy Jeremiah warned against.

verses.flow: that Azariah son of Hoshaiah, and Johanan son of Kareah, and all the proud men spoke, saying to Jeremiah, "A lie you are speaking! YHWH our God has not sent you to say, 'Do not go to Egypt to sojourn there!'
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "proud men" (Hebrew: anashim zedim, from zed/zadon — H2086/H2087) carries a strong moral charge, denoting insolent presumption against divine authority. Their accusation that Jeremiah speaks falsely (shaqer — falsehood, H8267) is deeply ironic: the very charge leveled against true prophets by false ones throughout Jeremiah's ministry (cf. Jer 5:31; 20:6). The accusation inverts reality — it is they who have refused the true word of YHWH.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The accusation against Baruch son of Neriah is historically significant. Baruch served as Jeremiah's scribe and secretary (Jer 36:4-32), and a clay bulla bearing his name ("Berekhyahu son of Neriyahu the scribe") was discovered in the antiquities market and is widely authenticated. The people project their fear and rebellion onto a scapegoat rather than reckon with the divine word itself.

verses.flow: and they came into the land of Egypt, for they did not obey the voice of YHWH; and they came as far as Tahpanhes.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Tahpanhes (Hebrew: Tahpanhes; Egyptian: Daphnae) was a frontier fortress city in the northeastern Nile Delta, identified with modern Tell Defenneh. Greek historian Herodotus mentions a "fort of Pelusium" in this region. Archaeological excavations by Petrie in 1886 uncovered a large brick platform near the city gate, possibly corresponding to the pavement of verse 9. This is a remarkable case of potential archaeological confirmation of a prophetic symbolic act.

verses.flow: "Take in your hand great stones and hide them in the mortar in the pavement of brick that is at the entrance of the house of Pharaoh in Tahpanhes, in the sight of the men of Judah,
verses.note: HEBREW: The term rendered "mortar" or "pavement of brick" (Hebrew: malben — H4404) is uncertain; it may denote a brick kiln, a pavement of brick, or a mortar of clay. The LXX renders it differently. The symbolic act of hiding stones in the pavement at Pharaoh's entrance functions as a prophetic sign-act (Hebrew: ot — sign), a characteristic mode of Jeremianic prophecy (cf. Jer 13; 19; 27), making the invisible decree of YHWH tangible and enacted in time.

verses.flow: and say to them: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I am sending and I will take Nebuchadrezzar the king of Babylon, my servant, and I will set his throne above these stones that I have hidden, and he will spread his royal canopy over them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The designation of Nebuchadrezzar (the more historically accurate spelling of Nebuchadnezzar, preserved in Jeremiah and Ezekiel from the Akkadian Nabu-kudurri-usur) as YHWH's "servant" (Hebrew: eved — H5650) is theologically striking. The same term used of the patriarchs, Moses, and David is here applied to a pagan king, underscoring that YHWH exercises sovereign lordship over all nations and employs whom He will as instruments of judgment. Compare Cyrus as YHWH's "anointed" in Isaiah 45:1.

verses.flow: And he will come and strike down the land of Egypt — those destined for death, to death; and those destined for captivity, to captivity; and those destined for the sword, to the sword.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The tripartite formula — "death...captivity...sword" — mirrors the covenant curse language of Deuteronomy (Deut 28:21-25) and appears throughout Jeremiah as a refrain of divine judgment (Jer 15:2; 18:21). Its application now to Egypt confirms that the refugees who fled there to escape Babylon have not escaped the decree of YHWH; they have carried the judgment with them. Disobedience cannot outrun divine sovereignty.

verses.flow: And I will kindle a fire in the temples of the gods of Egypt, and he will burn them and carry them away captive; and he will wrap himself in the land of Egypt as a shepherd wraps himself in his garment, and he will go out from there in peace (shalom).
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The image of YHWH kindling fire in the temples of the gods of Egypt is not merely political but cosmological — it is divine warfare against the pantheon of Egypt. The gods of Egypt are implicitly treated as real spiritual entities, now to be defeated by YHWH through His human instrument. Compare the plagues of Exodus as judgments on the gods of Egypt (Exod 12:12; Num 33:4). The phrase "he will go out in shalom (peace, completeness)" is ironic — Egypt offers no refuge, and the conqueror departs unscathed.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he will shatter the pillars of Beth-shemesh, which is in the land of Egypt, and the temples of the gods of Egypt he will burn with fire."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Beth-shemesh" (Hebrew: Beit-Shemesh — "House of the Sun") is the Hebrew name for Heliopolis (Egyptian: Iunu; Greek: Heliopolis), the great cult center of the Egyptian sun god Ra/Atum, located near modern Cairo. The "pillars" (Hebrew: matstsevot — sacred standing stones, H4676) were the famous obelisks of Heliopolis, some of which survive today (including "Cleopatra's Needles" in New York and London). The smashing of these cultic pillars represents the decisive humiliation of Egypt's chief deity, whom YHWH here declares He will overthrow.

verses.flow: The word that came to Jeremiah concerning all the Jews dwelling in the land of Egypt, who were dwelling in Migdol, and in Tahpanhes, and in Noph, and in the land of Pathros, saying,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Jewish communities addressed are scattered across Egypt's Delta and Nile regions. Migdol (fortress city in the northeastern Delta), Tahpanhes (Tell Defenneh, a border garrison town where the royal court had settled, cf. Jer 43:8), Noph (Memphis, the ancient capital), and Pathros (Upper Egypt, south of Memphis) represent the full extent of the Jewish diaspora in Egypt. These refugees had fled after the Babylonian conquest and the assassination of Gedaliah (Jer 41-43).

verses.flow: because of their wickedness which they committed to provoke me to anger, going to burn incense, to serve other gods that they did not know — they, nor you, nor your fathers.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "that they did not know" (Hebrew: lo yeda'um) is a covenantal formula marking these deities as utterly foreign to Israel's covenant relationship with YHWH. Worship of gods one does not "know" in the covenantal sense represents a complete rupture of the Sinai bond. This phrase echoes Deut 13:2, 6 and highlights the apostasy as relational betrayal, not merely ritual error.

verses.flow: Therefore my fury and my anger were poured out and burned in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem, and they became a waste and a desolation, as it is this day.

And now thus says YHWH, the God of hosts, the God of Israel: Why are you doing this great evil against your own souls, to cut off from you man and woman, child and nursing infant from the midst of Judah, leaving yourselves no remnant,
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "against your own souls" (Hebrew: el-nafshekhem, from nefesh, H5315) is startlingly direct — YHWH characterizes their idolatry as self-destructive, a form of spiritual suicide. The term nefesh encompasses the whole living person, their very life-breath. To sin in this way is to war against oneself. This anticipates the NT principle that sin is ultimately self-harm (Rom 6:23).

verses.flow: Have you forgotten the wickedness of your fathers, and the wickedness of the kings of Judah, and the wickedness of their wives, and your own wickedness, and the wickedness of your wives, which they committed in the land of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem?
They have not been humbled to this day, nor have they feared, nor have they walked in my law and in my statutes that I set before you and before your fathers.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "They have not been humbled" (Hebrew: lo daka'u, from daka, H1792) implies a crushing or pulverizing — the spiritual posture of contrition completely absent. The verb echoes the Psalms' language of a broken and contrite heart (Ps 51:17). Their continued stiff-neckedness despite the catastrophe of Jerusalem's fall is presented as inexcusable, given the manifest warnings of YHWH through his prophets.

verses.flow: But we will certainly do every word that has gone forth from our mouth, to burn incense to the queen of heaven and to pour out drink offerings to her, as we did — we and our fathers, our kings and our officials — in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem. For then we had plenty of food, and we were well, and we saw no evil.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "queen of heaven" (Hebrew: melekheth hashamayim, H4446) is almost certainly Ishtar/Astarte, the Mesopotamian and Canaanite goddess of love and war, whose cult involved the baking of crescent-shaped or image-bearing cakes (kawwanim), libations, and incense. Her veneration had deeply penetrated Judean popular religion (cf. Jer 7:18). The people's theological argument — that prosperity came when they worshiped her — represents a dangerous inversion of the covenant blessings-and-curses framework of Deuteronomy 28.

words.g: in/with at that time or place

verses.flow: But since we ceased burning incense to the queen of heaven and pouring out drink offerings to her, we have lacked everything and have been consumed by the sword and by famine."

verses.flow: And the women said, "When we burned incense to the queen of heaven and poured out drink offerings to her, was it without our husbands' approval that we made cakes for her bearing her image and poured out drink offerings to her?"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The women's assertion that their husbands were complicit ("without our husbands' approval") is legally significant. In the ancient Near East, a husband could annul his wife's vows (Num 30:6-15). By stating that their husbands approved, the women argue the vows to the queen of heaven were lawfully established and therefore binding. This reveals an organized, family-level apostasy, not merely individual religious deviation.

verses.flow: "Is it not the incense that you burned in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem — you and your fathers, your kings and your officials and the people of the land — is it not that YHWH remembered it and it came up onto his heart?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "came up onto his heart" (Hebrew: al-libbo, H3820) is a vivid anthropomorphism indicating that YHWH's memory of their idolatry was not passive data retrieval but active moral concern. YHWH did not simply observe; he was personally and deeply affected. This language of divine pathos is characteristic of Jeremiah (cf. Jer 31:20) and stands against any reading of God as detached or impassible in the face of covenant betrayal.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: You and your wives have spoken with your mouths and fulfilled it with your hands, saying, 'We will surely perform our vows that we vowed, to burn incense to the queen of heaven and to pour out drink offerings to her.' You shall surely establish your vows and surely perform your vows!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's sardonic command — "Surely establish your vows and surely perform your vows!" — is a form of judicial irony, releasing the people to the full consequences of their chosen path. It echoes the prophetic pattern of God giving people over to their desires in judgment (cf. Ps 81:12; Rom 1:24). The doubled infinitive absolute construction in Hebrew ('aso ta'asu, qayem taqemu) emphasizes the intensified, irrevocable nature of their commitment to destruction.

verses.flow: Therefore hear the word of YHWH, all Judah who dwell in the land of Egypt: Behold, I have sworn by my great name, says YHWH, that my name shall no more be invoked by the mouth of any man of Judah in all the land of Egypt, saying, 'As the Lord YHWH lives.'
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's oath "by my great name" is the most solemn form of divine self-pledge available, as there is no higher authority by which he can swear (Heb 6:13). The consequence — that his name shall no longer be invoked in Egypt among these exiles — is tantamount to a complete covenantal excommunication. This is the inverse of the Aaronic blessing and the covenantal promise of Num 6:27 that YHWH would "put his name" upon his people.

verses.flow: And those who escape the sword shall return from the land of Egypt to the land of Judah — few in number; and all the remnant of Judah who came to the land of Egypt to sojourn there shall know whose word shall stand — mine or theirs.
And this shall be the sign to you, declares YHWH, that I will punish you in this place, so that you may know that my words will surely stand against you for evil:

Thus says YHWH: Behold, I am giving Pharaoh Hophra, king of Egypt, into the hand of his enemies and into the hand of those who seek his life, just as I gave Zedekiah king of Judah into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, his enemy who sought his life."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The prophecy concerning Pharaoh Hophra (Apries, reigned 589-570 BC) is remarkably specific. Hophra was eventually overthrown by the general Amasis (Ahmose II) following a military mutiny circa 570 BC and was subsequently strangled, fulfilling this oracle. The explicit parallel drawn between Zedekiah's fall to Nebuchadnezzar and Hophra's coming defeat reinforces the theological claim that YHWH, not the gods of Egypt, governs the rise and fall of nations (cf. Dan 2:21; Ps 82:8).

verses.flow: The word that Jeremiah the prophet spoke to Baruch the son of Neriah, when he wrote these words in a book at the dictation of Jeremiah, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This chapter is chronologically displaced — the events described occurred in the fourth year of Jehoiakim (605 BC), the same year as the composition of the scroll in Jeremiah 36, yet the chapter appears near the end of the book. This editorial placement may be intentional, functioning as a personal colophon to Baruch's scroll collection. Baruch ben Neriah was Jeremiah's secretary and faithful companion; a clay seal (bulla) bearing the inscription "Berekhyahu son of Neriyahu the scribe" was discovered in the late 20th century, providing remarkable archaeological confirmation of this individual.

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel, concerning you, O Baruch:
You said, "Woe is me now! For YHWH has added sorrow (yagon) to my pain (mak'ov); I am weary in my groaning, and rest I have not found."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Yagon" (H3015, from yagah, to grieve) denotes deep sorrow or anguish, while "mak'ov" (H4341) refers to pain or suffering. The pairing intensifies the weight of Baruch's lament. His complaint echoes the confessions of Jeremiah himself (Jer 20:7-18), suggesting that the prophet's suffering had become contagious to his scribe. The phrase "rest I have not found" uses the verb matzati, a perfect of negation, underscoring total absence of relief.

words.g: to pull down or in pieces

verses.flow: Thus you shall say to him: Thus says YHWH — Behold, what I have built I am tearing down, and what I have planted I am plucking up — even this whole land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's response reframes Baruch's personal suffering within the cosmic scope of divine judgment. The language of "building and tearing down, planting and plucking up" directly recalls Jeremiah's inaugural commission (Jer 1:10), binding this personal word to the prophet's foundational calling. God does not dismiss Baruch's pain but situates it within a larger redemptive-historical frame: if YHWH is dismantling the entire covenant nation, individual hardship is not an anomaly but a participation in that greater reckoning.

verses.flow: And do you seek great things for yourself? Seek them not. For behold, I am bringing disaster upon all flesh, declares YHWH, but your life (naphshekha) I will give to you as a prize of war in all the places to which you may go."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The promise that Baruch's life (naphshekha — his very soul/self) will be given to him "as a prize of war" (leshallal, lit. as plunder or spoil) is a minimalist but genuine act of grace. In the context of national catastrophe, mere survival is itself a gift. This phrase recurs in Jeremiah (21:9; 38:2; 39:18) as a formula of covenantal mercy amid judgment. TYPOLOGY: The pattern of the faithful servant preserved through national ruin anticipates the remnant theology developed through the exile and ultimately fulfilled in Christ, who passes through death and emerges as the firstfruits of a new creation (1 Cor 15:20), securing life for all who are united to him.

verses.flow: That which came as the word of YHWH to Jeremiah the prophet concerning the nations.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verse 1 serves as a superscription to the entire "Oracles Against the Nations" (OAN) section (chapters 46-51), which in the Hebrew text concludes the book before chapter 52. Notably, the LXX places these oracles in a different order and location (after 25:13), raising important questions about the compositional history of the book. Compare Isaiah 13-23 and Ezekiel 25-32 for parallel OAN collections in the major prophets.

verses.flow: Concerning Egypt: against the army of Pharaoh Neco, king of Egypt, which was by the river Euphrates at Carchemish, which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon struck in the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Battle of Carchemish (605 BC) was one of the decisive engagements of the ancient Near East, in which Nebuchadnezzar II of Babylon decisively defeated the coalition of Egypt and Assyria, effectively ending Assyrian power and establishing Babylonian dominance over the Levant. Pharaoh Neco II (Necho) had previously slain King Josiah at Megiddo (2 Kgs 23:29). The fourth year of Jehoiakim corresponds to the first year of Nebuchadnezzar, a synchronism important for biblical chronology.

verses.flow: Harness the horses, and mount, O horsemen! Take your stations with your helmets, burnish the spears, put on the coats of mail!
Why do I see it? They are dismayed and turned backward; their mighty ones are beaten down and have fled in haste, and look not back — terror on every side! declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "terror on every side" (magôr missabîb, מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב) is a signature expression of Jeremiah's own personal laments and prophetic vocabulary (Jer 6:25; 20:3, 10; 49:29), here applied to Egypt with ironic force. YHWH's declaration turns the war cry back upon the aggressor. The phrase was so characteristic of Jeremiah that his opponent Pashhur received it as a name-oracle (Jer 20:3).

verses.flow: For that day belongs to the Lord YHWH of hosts, a day of vengeance, to avenge himself on his foes; and the sword shall devour and be satisfied and drink its fill of their blood. For the Lord YHWH of hosts holds a sacrifice in the north country by the river Euphrates.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "day of YHWH of hosts" (yôm la-YHWH elohim tseba'ôt) frames this military disaster as a theophanic act of divine vengeance rather than mere geopolitical accident. The imagery of sacrifice (zebach, זֶבַח) applied to the slaughter at Carchemish is a striking inversion — the powerful Egyptian army becomes the sacrificial victim. Compare Isaiah 34:6 and Zephaniah 1:7-8 for parallel uses of sacrificial imagery in Day of YHWH contexts. DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH of hosts (tseba'ôt, צְבָאוֹת) evokes the divine warrior leading the heavenly armies into battle; this title appears over 280 times in the Hebrew Bible and reflects the council theology in which YHWH commands both celestial and terrestrial armies.

verses.flow: Why has Apis fled? Why did your mighty one not stand? Because YHWH thrust him down.
verses.note: HEBREW: The reference to "Apis" (Hebrew: 'Apis, אַפִּיס in some manuscripts, though the MT reads 'abbîr, "your mighty one") refers to the sacred Apis bull, the chief animal deity of Memphis, whose cult was central to Egyptian religion. The LXX reads "Apis" explicitly. The question "why has Apis fled?" is devastatingly ironic — the deity who cannot protect himself cannot protect Egypt. THEOLOGICAL: This verse exemplifies the prophetic polemic against idolatry: the defeat of a nation is simultaneously the defeat and humiliation of its gods (cf. Exod 12:12; Num 33:4; Jer 43:12).

verses.flow: As I live, declares the King, whose name is YHWH of hosts, for like Tabor among the mountains and like Carmel by the sea, he shall come.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine self-oath formula "As I live, declares the King, whose name is YHWH of hosts" is among the most solemn utterances in the prophetic corpus. YHWH's title as "the King" (ha-melek, הַמֶּלֶךְ) asserts his sovereign supremacy over all earthly monarchs, including Pharaoh who was worshiped as a divine king in Egypt. The mountains Tabor and Carmel — both visible landmarks towering above their surroundings — become images of the unstoppable, towering certainty of Nebuchadnezzar's advance as YHWH's instrument.
