verses.flow: Prepare for yourself baggage for exile, O daughter dwelling in Egypt! For Memphis shall become a waste, a desolation, without inhabitant.
Egypt is a very beautiful heifer, but a horsefly from the north has come — it has come.
verses.note: HEBREW: Egypt is compared to a "very beautiful heifer" (eglah yaphah-phiyyah, עֶגְלָה יְפֵה-פִיָּה), the superlative formed by reduplication being characteristic of elevated Hebrew poetry. The "horsefly" or "gadfly" (qerets, קֶרֶץ) from the north — a rare term in the MT — refers to the Babylonian invasion as a stinging, tormenting pest that disturbs even a prized animal. The image may evoke the Egyptian plagues in reverse, as Egypt now suffers what she once inflicted.

verses.flow: Even her hired soldiers in her midst are like fattened calves; for they also have turned and fled together — they did not stand, for the day of their calamity has come upon them, the time of their punishment.

verses.flow: Her voice goes forth like a serpent, for they march with an army and come against her with axes like those who fell trees.
They shall cut down her forest, declares YHWH, though it is impenetrable, because they are more numerous than locusts, and are without number.
The daughter of Egypt shall be put to shame; she shall be delivered into the hand of a people from the north.

YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, has said: Behold, I am bringing punishment upon Amon of No, and upon Pharaoh and upon Egypt and her gods and her kings, even upon Pharaoh and upon those who trust in him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Amon of No" refers to Amun (Amon-Ra), the chief deity of Thebes (Hebrew: No-Amon, נֹא אָמוֹן), the great capital of Upper Egypt. Thebes was the religious and cultural heart of Egypt, home to the magnificent temple complex at Karnak. Nahum 3:8 uses "No-Amon" as a comparison for Nineveh. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's judgment explicitly targets not only Egypt's political structures (Pharaoh, kings) but also her religious system ("her gods"), demonstrating that the conflict is simultaneously historical and cosmic — a contest between YHWH and the divine beings associated with the nations (cf. Deut 32:8-9; Ps 82).

verses.flow: I will deliver them into the hand of those who seek their life, into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and into the hand of his servants; and afterward it shall be inhabited as in the days of old, declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The oracle closes with a remarkable note of restoration — Egypt shall eventually be reinhabited "as in the days of old." This reflects Jeremiah's broader theology that YHWH's judgments, while severe, are not necessarily final for the nations. Compare the similar restorations promised to Moab (Jer 48:47), Ammon (Jer 49:6), and Elam (Jer 49:39), though notably not Babylon (Jer 51). TYPOLOGY: This pattern of judgment-then-restoration foreshadows the eschatological hope for all nations, which finds its ultimate fulfillment in the ingathering of peoples under Messiah's reign (Isa 19:23-25; Rev 21:24-26).

verses.flow: But as for you, fear not, O my servant Jacob, and be not dismayed, O Israel; for behold, I will save you from far away, and your offspring from the land of their captivity. Jacob shall return and be at rest (shalom) and at ease, and none shall make him afraid.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Verses 27-28 form a "salvation oracle" (Heilsorakel) for Israel embedded within the oracles against Egypt, likely an intentional literary contrast — while Egypt falls without remedy, Jacob shall be saved, returned, and given shalom (שָׁלוֹם). The term shalom here carries its full weight: not mere absence of conflict but completeness, wholeness, covenant flourishing. This oracle is nearly identical to Jeremiah 30:10-11, forming a doublet that reinforces the theological structure of the book. TYPOLOGY: The promise "I will save you from far away" anticipates the eschatological ingathering of Israel, which Paul understands as pointing toward the ultimate salvation of "all Israel" in the messianic age (Rom 11:26).

verses.flow: Fear not, O Jacob my servant, declares YHWH, for I am with you; for I will make a full end of all the nations to which I have driven you, but of you I will not make a full end. I will discipline you in just measure, and I will by no means leave you unpunished.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing verse holds in precise tension two great attributes of YHWH: his absolute sovereignty in making a "full end" (kalah, כָּלָה) of the nations, and his covenantal faithfulness that will not make a full end of Jacob. The phrase "I will discipline you in just measure" (mishpat, מִשְׁפָּט — in justice/judgment) ensures that the exile is neither arbitrary cruelty nor mere abandonment but purposeful covenant discipline. HEBREW: The verb yissarticha (יִסַּרְתִּיךָ, "I will discipline/chasten you") derives from the root yasar (יָסַר), used of a father's corrective discipline of a son (Deut 8:5; Prov 3:11-12), reinforcing the Father-son covenant relationship between YHWH and Israel that undergirds the entire book.

verses.flow: The word of YHWH that came to Jeremiah the prophet concerning the Philistines, before Pharaoh struck down Gaza.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The superscription ties this oracle to a specific moment — Pharaoh's striking of Gaza — likely referencing Pharaoh Neco II's campaign (c. 609 BC) during his northern march, though some scholars connect it to earlier Egyptian activity. The Philistine cities were frequently caught between Egyptian and Mesopotamian imperial ambitions throughout the 7th-6th centuries BC. Gaza was the southernmost and most strategically significant of the Philistine pentapolis cities, sitting astride the Via Maris trade and military route.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: Behold, waters are rising from the north, and they shall become an overflowing torrent (nachal shoteף); and they shall overflow the land and all that fills it, the city and those who dwell in it. And the men shall cry out, and all the inhabitants of the land shall wail.
verses.note: HEBREW: The image of "waters rising from the north" (mayim olim mitsaphon) is a recurring Jeremianic motif for Babylonian invasion (cf. Jer 1:14; 4:6; 6:1). The word rendered "torrent" (nachal) can mean a wadi, a seasonal stream, or an overwhelming flood; paired with the participle shoteף ("overflowing"), it evokes an unstoppable inundation. This cosmic-flood imagery for military conquest echoes ANE battle rhetoric and may deliberately invoke the chaotic waters of divine judgment.

verses.flow: At the sound of the stamping of the hooves of his mighty stallions, at the rumbling of his chariots, the thundering of his wheels — fathers do not look back to their children, because of the feebleness of hands,

on account of the day that is coming to destroy all the Philistines, to cut off from Tyre and Sidon every survivor who helps them. For YHWH is destroying the Philistines, the remnant of the coastland of Caphtor (Kaftor).
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "YHWH is destroying" (shodhed YHWH) presents YHWH as the direct agent behind Babylonian military action, consistent with the divine council theology in which YHWH deploys nations as instruments of judgment. "Caphtor" (Kaftor) is the ancient homeland of the Philistines, most likely identified with Crete or the broader Aegean region (cf. Amos 9:7; Deut 2:23), underscoring that even those who migrated under divine providence now face divine judgment. The cutting off of Tyre and Sidon from rendering aid signals the isolation of Philistia in its hour of destruction.

verses.flow: Baldness has come upon Gaza; Ashkelon has been silenced. O remnant of their valley, how long will you gash yourselves?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Baldness" (qorchah) was a sign of extreme mourning in the ancient Near East, often connected with shaving the head in lamentation (cf. Amos 8:10; Mic 1:16). The verb "gash yourselves" (titgodedi) refers to the practice of self-laceration in mourning, a rite explicitly forbidden to Israel (Deut 14:1) but practiced among surrounding peoples. The rhetorical question "how long?" (ad-matay) introduces a note of bitter irony — the mourning will not cease because the devastation is complete.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: Ah, sword (cherev) of YHWH! How long until you are quiet? Gather yourself into your scabbard; rest and be still!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The personification of YHWH's sword as a quasi-independent agent crying out to be sheathed is a striking rhetorical device. The prophet himself — or perhaps a sympathetic voice — intercedes for the sword to cease, only to be answered in verse 7. This tension between divine judgment and the yearning for its cessation reflects Jeremiah's own tortured prophetic identity throughout the book (cf. Jer 20:7-9). The sword (cherev) as YHWH's instrument of judgment is a consistent theological metaphor across the Latter Prophets.

verses.flow: How can it be quiet, when YHWH has given it a command? Against Ashkelon and against the seacoast — there He has appointed it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The oracle closes with an unanswerable divine warrant: the sword cannot rest because YHWH Himself has commanded it. This final verse grounds the entire oracle not in geopolitical fate but in covenantal divine sovereignty — YHWH has "appointed" (tsivvah) the sword to specific targets. The rhetorical structure (question in v.6, answer in v.7) is a classic prophetic disputation form, asserting that human pleas cannot override divine decree. The nations exist within the sphere of YHWH's governance, not outside it.

verses.flow: For because you trusted in your works and in your treasures, you also shall be taken; and Chemosh shall go into exile — his priests and his princes together.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Chemosh was the national deity of Moab (cf. Num 21:29; 1 Kgs 11:7), a member of the nations' divine allotment under the sons of God (Deut 32:8-9). His exile signifies the defeat of the territorial spirit governing Moab, not merely political humiliation. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The judgment on Chemosh reflects YHWH's sovereignty over all divine beings assigned to the nations — his going into exile mirrors the defeat of Babylon's gods in Isa 46:1-2.

verses.flow: Cursed is the one who does the work of YHWH with slackness, and cursed is the one who withholds his sword from blood.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is remarkable in its explicit condemnation of half-hearted execution of divine judgment. The term rendered "slackness" (remiyah, H7423) denotes deceit or negligence. YHWH frames his instrument of judgment (likely Babylon) as under obligation — to withhold the sword is to be cursed. This principle undergirds the theology of holy war (cherem) throughout the Hebrew Bible.

verses.flow: Moab has been at ease from his youth and has settled on his dregs; he has not been emptied from vessel to vessel, nor has he gone into exile; so his taste has remained in him, and his scent (reyach) has not changed.
verses.note: HEBREW: The imagery of wine settled on its dregs (shemarim, H8105) draws from winemaking — undisturbed wine retains a harsh, undeveloped character. Moab's lack of exile and suffering had left her spiritually and morally stagnant. The term "scent" (reyach) refers to character or essence, suggesting Moab's identity was unchanged by the refining processes YHWH uses for his covenant people (cf. Zeph 1:12).

verses.flow: Then Moab shall be ashamed of Chemosh, as the house of Israel was ashamed of Bethel, their confidence.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Bethel, the site of Jeroboam's golden calf (1 Kgs 12:28-29), became a byword for false confidence. The comparison between Chemosh and the Bethel calf-idol draws a deliberate parallel: both were objects of misplaced national trust. Archaeologically, Bethel has been identified with modern Beitin, where cultic installations consistent with the biblical narrative have been discovered.

verses.flow: Leave the cities and dwell in the rock, O inhabitants of Moab; and be like the dove that nests in the walls of the mouth of the gorge.
We have heard of the pride (gaavah) of Moab — he is very proud — of his loftiness, his pride (gaon), his arrogance (gaavah), and the haughtiness of his heart.
verses.note: HEBREW: Three distinct Hebrew roots for pride appear in rapid succession: gaavah (H1346, pride as arrogant posturing), gaon (H1347, majesty turned to haughtiness), and rum lev (lifted heart). The concentration of pride vocabulary is unparalleled in the OAN (Oracles Against the Nations) and deliberately indicts Moab's fundamental sin as arrogance — echoing the pride of Satan (Isa 14) and Babylon. THEOLOGICAL: Pride against YHWH is the root transgression of both human and spiritual rebels throughout Scripture (cf. Ezek 28:2; Isa 14:13-14).

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH: "Behold, one shall fly like an eagle and spread his wings against Moab.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The eagle (nesher, H5404) descending upon Moab recalls the eagle imagery used of Nebuchadnezzar in Ezek 17:3 and the eschatological eagle of Matt 24:28. In the ANE, the eagle was a symbol of divine judgment and imperial power. The spreading of wings (poreis kenaphayim) evokes both a bird of prey and, ironically, the protective wings of YHWH (Ps 91:4; Ruth 2:12) — here inverted into an image of destruction.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Kerioth is taken, and the strongholds are seized; and the heart of the warriors of Moab shall be in that day like the heart of a woman in her birth pains.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And Moab shall be destroyed from being a people, because he magnified himself against YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "magnified himself against YHWH" (ki al-YHWH higdil) is the stated theological cause of Moab's destruction. This same charge is leveled against Pharaoh (Exod 9:17), the king of Assyria (2 Kgs 19:22), and the eschatological antichrist figure (Dan 11:36-37). It marks Moab's sin as not merely political or military but cosmically rebellious — a direct challenge to YHWH's sovereignty.

verses.flow: Terror (pachad) and pit (pachat) and snare (pach) are before you, O inhabitant of Moab,' declares YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: Verses 43-44 employ a celebrated wordplay in Hebrew: pachad (terror, H6343), pachat (pit, H6354), and pach (snare, H6341) — three phonetically similar terms creating an inescapable cascade of judgment. This literary device, known as paronomasia, is also found in Isa 24:17-18 in an eschatological context, suggesting Jeremiah deliberately echoes that cosmic judgment passage. STRUCTURE: The tripling of alliterative doom-words functions as a literary intensifier, leaving no escape in any direction.

words.m: conjunction + particle + verb qal participle active

verses.flow: 'He who flees from the terror shall fall into the pit, and he who climbs up out of the pit shall be caught in the snare; for I will bring upon her, upon Moab, the year of their punishment,' declares YHWH.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

words.g: and the crown of the head

verses.flow: 'In the shadow of Heshbon the fugitives stand without strength; for fire went out from Heshbon and a flame from the midst of Sihon, and it devoured the forehead of Moab and the crown of the head of the sons of tumult.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Verse 45 cites an ancient poem, likely drawn from the archaic Song of Heshbon tradition (cf. Num 21:27-28), which described Heshbon's earlier conquest of Moab under Sihon the Amorite. Jeremiah repurposes this older material prophetically — what once happened historically to Moab at human hands will now happen by YHWH's direct decree. The reuse of ancient poetry gives the oracle an air of inevitable, recurring judgment.

verses.flow: Woe to you, O Moab! The people of Chemosh are undone; for your sons have been taken captive and your daughters into captivity.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: 'Yet I will restore the fortunes (shevut) of Moab in the latter days,' declares YHWH. Thus far is the judgment on Moab."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The oracle closes with a remarkable note of eschatological hope — YHWH will "restore the fortunes" (shevut) of Moab in the latter days (be'acharit hayyamim). The phrase be'acharit hayyamim is a technical eschatological marker used throughout the prophets for the messianic age. Similar restoration promises are extended to Egypt (Jer 46:26) and Elam (Jer 49:39), indicating that YHWH's ultimate purposes encompass the redemption of Gentile nations — anticipating the universal scope of the New Covenant (cf. Rom 11:25-26; Rev 21:24). TYPOLOGY: This restoration hope points forward to the gathering of all nations under the reign of Messiah (Isa 19:24-25; Zeph 3:9).

words.g: in/with + in/with a city in the widest sense

verses.flow: Concerning the sons of Ammon. Thus says YHWH: Has Israel no sons? Has he no heir? Why then has Milcom dispossessed Gad, and his people settled in its cities?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Milcom" (Strong's H4445, מִלְכֹּם) is the national deity of Ammon, also rendered "Molech" in some texts. The name derives from the root מֶלֶךְ (melek, "king"), indicating a royal divine patron. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Milcom functions as a territorial deity within the divine council framework — a "son of God" assigned oversight of Ammon (cf. Deut 32:8-9), now judged for usurping Israelite territory, demonstrating that the geopolitical is also the cosmological.

conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Therefore, behold, days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will cause a war cry (teruah) to be heard against Rabbah of the sons of Ammon; and it shall become a desolate mound, and its daughter-villages shall be burned with fire; then Israel shall dispossess those who dispossessed him, says YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Teruah" (תְּרוּעָה, Strong's H8643) is the war-shout or battle-cry, also used of the trumpet blast that announces divine war. "Rabbah" (modern Amman, Jordan) was the Ammonite capital. HISTORICAL: Rabbah of the Ammonites sat on the eastern plateau of Transjordan, a significant commercial and military center throughout the Iron Age, attested in numerous extrabiblical sources.

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpolel imperative 2nd fem plur

verses.flow: Wail, O Heshbon, for Ai is laid waste! Cry out, O daughters of Rabbah! Gird yourselves with sackcloth, lament, and run back and forth among the sheepfolds! For Milcom shall go into exile, his priests and his officials together.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The statement "Milcom shall go into exile" frames the judgment theologically — the deity is disgraced along with his people. In ANE thought, the defeat of a nation meant the defeat or captivity of its patron god. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's sovereignty over all nations means even foreign divine patrons are subject to his decrees and judgments (cf. Ps 82:1-8; Isa 46:1-2).

verses.flow: Why do you boast in the valleys? Your valley flows away, O backsliding daughter, who trusted in her treasures, saying, "Who will come against me?"

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Behold, I am bringing terror upon you, declares the Lord YHWH of hosts, from all your neighbors; and you shall be driven out, each man straight before him, with no one to gather the fugitives.
But afterward I will restore the fortunes of the sons of Ammon, declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The pattern of judgment followed by restoration appears repeatedly in the oracles against the nations (cf. Jer 48:47; 49:39). This reflects the covenantal hope that YHWH's purposes extend even to enemy nations, foreshadowing the eschatological inclusion of all peoples (cf. Amos 9:12; Acts 15:17).

verses.flow: Concerning Edom. Thus says YHWH of hosts: Is there no longer wisdom in Teman? Has counsel perished from the prudent? Has their wisdom vanished?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Teman was a region or city in Edom renowned for its wisdom tradition (cf. Job 2:11; Obad 8). THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question "Is there no longer wisdom in Teman?" signals total collapse — Edom's celebrated intellectual heritage cannot save her from divine judgment. The oracle against Edom is closely paralleled in Obadiah, suggesting shared prophetic tradition or a common source.

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Flee, turn back, dwell in the depths, O inhabitants of Dedan! For I will bring the calamity of Esau upon him, the time when I punish him.
If grape-gatherers came to you, would they not leave gleanings? If thieves came by night, would they not destroy only as much as they wanted?

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: But I have stripped Esau bare; I have uncovered his hiding places, and he is not able to conceal himself. His offspring is destroyed, and his brothers and his neighbors; and he is no more.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Leave your fatherless children; I will keep them alive; and let your widows trust in me.
For thus says YHWH: Behold, those who did not deserve to drink the cup must drink it; and are you the one who will altogether go unpunished? You shall not go unpunished, but you shall surely drink.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "cup" (כּוֹס, kos) of YHWH's wrath is a recurring prophetic metaphor for the outpouring of divine judgment upon nations (cf. Jer 25:15-29; Isa 51:17; Rev 14:10). The logic is covenantal: if even those undeserving of the cup must drink, Edom — guilty of violence against his brother Jacob — cannot escape. TYPOLOGY: The cup imagery reaches its fullest expression in Christ's Gethsemane prayer (Matt 26:39), where he voluntarily drinks the cup of divine wrath on behalf of his people.

words.g: in/with a city in the widest sense

verses.flow: For I have sworn by myself, declares YHWH, that Bozrah shall become a horror, a reproach, a ruin, and a curse; and all her cities shall be perpetual ruins.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Bozrah (modern Buseirah in Jordan) was the chief city of Edom, situated in the highlands southeast of the Dead Sea. Archaeological excavations confirm its prominence as an administrative center during the Iron Age II period. THEOLOGICAL: The oath formula "I have sworn by myself" (cf. Gen 22:16; Heb 6:13) underscores the absolute certainty of the judgment — YHWH can swear by no greater authority.

verses.flow: I have heard a message from YHWH, and an envoy has been sent among the nations: "Gather yourselves together and come against her, and rise up for battle!"

verses.flow: For behold, I will make you small among the nations, despised among mankind.

verses.flow: The terror you inspire and the pride of your heart have deceived you, you who live in the clefts of the rock, who hold the height of the hill. Though you make your nest as high as the eagle's, I will bring you down from there, declares YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Edomites were famous for their cliff-city strongholds — most notably Petra (Sela), carved into the red sandstone cliffs of the Wadi Musa. The imagery of nesting "as high as the eagle" reflects the literal topography of Edomite territory. THEOLOGICAL: Pride (גַּבְהוּת, gabhut) is consistently identified in prophetic literature as the root sin that brings down nations and individuals (cf. Obad 3-4; Prov 16:18; Isa 14:13-15).

verses.flow: Edom shall become a horror; everyone who passes by it shall be horrified and shall hiss because of all its wounds.
As when Sodom and Gomorrah and their neighboring cities were overthrown, says YHWH, no man shall dwell there, and no son of man shall sojourn in her.

verses.flow: Behold, like a lion coming up from the thicket of the Jordan against a strong dwelling place, I will suddenly make them run away from her; and I will appoint over her whomever I choose. For who is like me? Who will summon me? And what shepherd can stand before me?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The lion rising from the Jordan thicket is a vivid image of YHWH as the divine warrior who personally executes judgment. THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical challenge "Who is like me? Who will summon me?" asserts YHWH's absolute incomparability and sovereign authority — no nation, deity, or power can call him to account (cf. Isa 40:25; Job 38:3). This language parallels divine council challenges elsewhere in the prophets.

verses.flow: Therefore hear the counsel (etsah) of YHWH that he has devised against Edom, and his purposes that he has formed against the inhabitants of Teman: Even the little ones of the flock shall be dragged away; surely their dwelling place shall be appalled at them.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The counsel (etsah) of YHWH" (עֲצַת יְהוָה, Strong's H6098) refers to the deliberations of the divine council — the heavenly assembly where YHWH decrees his purposes (cf. Jer 23:18, 22; 1 Kgs 22:19-22; Ps 82:1). The prophets present themselves as those admitted to this council to receive and announce divine decrees. HEBREW: "Etsah" carries both the sense of counsel/advice and of deliberate plan or purpose.

verses.flow: At the sound of their fall the earth shall quake; the sound of their cry shall be heard at the Red Sea.

verses.flow: Behold, one shall mount up and fly swiftly like an eagle and spread his wings against Bozrah, and the heart of the warriors of Edom shall be in that day like the heart of a woman in her birth pangs.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The eagle-image for a conquering king or army appears frequently in prophetic literature (cf. Deut 28:49; Ezek 17:3; Hab 1:8; Matt 24:28). HISTORICAL: The historical referent for the Edomite judgment is likely the Babylonian campaigns of Nebuchadnezzar, confirmed by extrabiblical texts and later Edomite displacement documented through the 6th-5th centuries BCE.

verses.flow: Concerning Damascus. Hamath and Arpad are put to shame, for they have heard bad news; they melt in fear; there is distress like on the sea that cannot be quiet.

verses.flow: Damascus has become feeble; she turned to flee, and panic seized her; anguish and sorrows have taken hold of her, as of a woman in labor.

verses.flow: How is the famous city not forsaken, the city of my joy?
Therefore her young men shall fall in her squares, and all her soldiers shall be destroyed in that day, declares YHWH of hosts.

words.g: and to burn or set on fire

verses.flow: And I will kindle a fire in the wall of Damascus, and it shall devour the strongholds of Ben-hadad.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ben-hadad was a dynastic throne-name used by several Aramean kings of Damascus (cf. 1 Kgs 15:18; 20:1; 2 Kgs 13:3). The mention of his "strongholds" recalls earlier prophetic oracles against Aram (cf. Amos 1:4). THEOLOGICAL: The destruction of city walls by fire is a standard covenant-curse for covenant-breaking nations throughout the ancient Near East.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Concerning Kedar and the kingdoms of Hazor that Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon struck. Thus says YHWH: Rise up, advance against Kedar! Destroy the people of the east!

words.g: and a collective name for a flock

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: Their tents and their flocks shall be taken; their curtains and all their vessels and their camels shall be carried off for themselves; and men shall cry to them: "Terror on every side!"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Terror on every side" (מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב, magor missabib, Strong's H4032 + H5439) is the distinctive phrase Jeremiah uses repeatedly, sometimes as a nickname for Pashhur (Jer 20:3) and as a summary of judgment (Jer 6:25; 20:10; 46:5). Its appearance here in the oracle against Kedar shows the phrase functions as a theological signature of YHWH's comprehensive judgment.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Flee, wander far away, dwell in the depths, O inhabitants of Hazor! declares YHWH. For Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon has devised a scheme against you and formed a purpose against you.
Rise up, advance against a nation at ease, that dwells securely, declares YHWH, that has no gates or bars, that dwells alone.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The description of a nation "at ease, that dwells securely, that has no gates or bars, that dwells alone" matches the lifestyle of nomadic Arab tribes. THEOLOGICAL: Paradoxically, their perceived security — no fortifications, no alliances — becomes their vulnerability. True security belongs only to those whose trust is in YHWH (cf. Ps 127:1; Zech 2:4-5).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Their camels shall become plunder, their herds of livestock a spoil. I will scatter to every wind those who cut the corners of their hair, and I will bring their calamity from every side of them, declares YHWH.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Hazor shall become a haunt of jackals, an everlasting waste; no man shall dwell there; no son of man shall sojourn in her.

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: The word of YHWH that came to Jeremiah the prophet concerning Elam, in the beginning of the reign of Zedekiah king of Judah, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Elam was the ancient civilization centered on the Iranian plateau east of Mesopotamia (modern Khuzestan, Iran), with its capital at Susa. Elam was renowned for its archers and military power (cf. Isa 22:6). HISTORICAL: This oracle is specifically dated to "the beginning of the reign of Zedekiah" (c. 597 BCE), making it one of the more precisely dated oracles in the collection.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, I will break the bow of Elam, the mainstay of their might.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The bow of Elam" is a metonymy for their primary military strength — Elamite archers were famous in the ancient world (cf. Isa 22:6). Breaking the bow symbolizes total disarmament and military defeat (cf. Ps 46:9; Hos 1:5). HISTORICAL: Elam's power was periodically broken — by Ashurbanipal of Assyria in the 7th century, and later absorbed into the Achaemenid Persian empire.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: And I will bring upon Elam the four winds from the four quarters of heaven, and I will scatter them to all those winds, and there shall be no nation to which those driven out of Elam shall not come.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "four winds from the four quarters of heaven" (cf. Dan 7:2; Rev 7:1) signifies the totality of divine action — comprehensive, directional, and cosmic in scope. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The winds in biblical cosmology are often associated with divine messengers or agents executing heavenly decrees (cf. Ps 104:4; Heb 1:7; Dan 7:2), suggesting a council-ordered cosmic judgment.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing
preposition + verb piel sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: I will terrify Elam before their enemies and before those who seek their life; I will bring disaster upon them, my fierce anger, declares YHWH. I will send the sword after them until I have consumed them.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I will set my throne in Elam and destroy from there king and officials, declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's declaration "I will set my throne in Elam" is a bold assertion of divine sovereignty over a foreign power far beyond Israel's political reach. It parallels similar language concerning Babylon (cf. Jer 43:10) and asserts that YHWH's kingly authority is universal, not geographically limited to Zion. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The divine throne is the seat of the heavenly council; its establishment in Elam implies the complete subjugation of Elam's territorial patron to YHWH's council authority.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: But in the latter days I will restore the fortunes of Elam, declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The oracle closes with restoration promise, mirroring the endings of the Ammon (49:6) and Moab (48:47) oracles. This eschatological pattern — judgment then restoration — points toward YHWH's ultimate redemptive purposes for all nations. TYPOLOGY: The restoration of Elam's fortunes anticipates the ingathering of all nations under Messiah's reign (cf. Isa 11:11, where Elam is specifically named among the scattered whom YHWH will recover; Acts 2:9, where Elamites are present at Pentecost).

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: The word that YHWH spoke concerning Babylon, concerning the land of the Chaldeans, by the hand of Jeremiah the prophet.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This superscription introduces the longest single oracle against any foreign nation in the Hebrew prophetic corpus (Jer 50-51), comprising 110 verses. The phrase "by the hand of Jeremiah" (beyad Yirmeyahu) indicates prophetic agency and authoritative transmission. Historically, these oracles likely date to ca. 594 BC, connected to the incident described in Jer 51:59-64. THEOLOGICAL: Babylon functions in Scripture as the archetypal anti-God empire, a theme carried into the NT (Rev 17-18).

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Declare among the nations and proclaim, and lift up a banner; proclaim, do not conceal it. Say: "Babylon is taken, Bel is put to shame, Merodach is shattered. Her images are put to shame, her idols are shattered."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Bel" (H1078) is a title meaning "lord," a Babylonian form of Ba'al applied to Marduk. "Merodach" (H4781, Marduk) was the chief deity of Babylon. "Images" (atsabim, H6091) and "idols" (gillulim, H1544, lit. "dung-pellets") are both terms of contempt for carved divine representations. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The shaming of Babylon's gods signals YHWH's supremacy over all divine beings in the heavenly assembly — a direct cosmic polemic (cf. Ps 82:1; Deut 32:8-9).

words.g: from ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: For a nation has come up against her from the north; it shall make her land a desolation, and none shall dwell in it; both man and beast shall flee — they shall depart.
In those days and in that time, declares YHWH, the sons of Israel shall come, they and the sons of Judah together; weeping as they go, they shall seek YHWH their God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "in those days and in that time" marks an eschatological horizon extending beyond the immediate return from exile. The reunion of Israel and Judah weeping before YHWH anticipates the new covenant renewal of Jer 31:31-34 (cf. Heb 8:8-12). TYPOLOGY: This seeking of YHWH in tears prefigures the repentance of Israel at the end of days (Zech 12:10-14; Rom 11:26).

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: They shall ask the way to Zion, with faces turned toward it, saying: "Come, let us join ourselves to YHWH in an everlasting covenant (berit) that shall not be forgotten."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Berit" (H1285, covenant) is the foundational term of Israel's covenantal relationship with YHWH. The descriptor "everlasting" (olam, H5769) elevates this beyond the Mosaic covenant to the new covenant anticipated in Jer 31:31-34. TYPOLOGY: The return to Zion with covenant renewal is fulfilled eschatologically in Christ, who is the mediator of the new and everlasting covenant (Heb 13:20).

verses.flow: My people have been lost sheep. Their shepherds have led them astray; on the mountains they have turned them aside; from mountain to hill they have gone; they have forgotten their resting-place.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: All who found them have devoured them, and their enemies said, "We are not guilty," because they sinned against YHWH, the habitation of righteousness (tsedek), and YHWH, the hope (miqveh) of their fathers.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Miqveh" (H4723) meaning "hope" or "reservoir/collection" — YHWH is the gathering-place and source of Israel's hope, a vivid double image. "Tsedek" (H6664, righteousness) characterizes YHWH's dwelling place, indicating his nature permeates his habitation. THEOLOGICAL: The enemies' self-exoneration ("we are not guilty") reveals the moral danger of being instruments of divine judgment — an agent of punishment is not thereby innocent.

verses.flow: For behold, I am stirring up and bringing against Babylon a company of great nations from the land of the north, and they shall array themselves against her. From there she shall be captured. Their arrows are like those of a mighty warrior who does not return empty-handed.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And Chaldea shall become plunder; all who plunder her shall be satisfied, declares YHWH.

verses.flow: "Because you rejoice, because you exult, O plunderers of my heritage (nachalah), because you frolic like a heifer treading grain, and neigh like stallions —
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nachalah" (H5159, heritage/inheritance) is a covenantal term for Israel as YHWH's possession — a people belonging to him by divine right. Israel's subjugation is not mere political defeat but desecration of YHWH's personal inheritance. THEOLOGICAL: That YHWH calls Israel "my heritage" while they are in exile demonstrates covenantal fidelity transcending circumstance (cf. Deut 32:9).

verses.flow: your mother shall be utterly shamed; she who bore you shall be disgraced. Behold, she shall be the last of the nations, a wilderness, a dry land, and a desert.

verses.flow: Because of the wrath (qetseph) of YHWH she shall not be inhabited, but she shall be wholly desolate. Every one who passes by Babylon shall be appalled and hiss because of all her wounds.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qetseph" (H7110, wrath/indignation) denotes the passionate divine anger that arises from violated holiness and covenant breach. The desolation of Babylon is explicitly attributed to this divine wrath. HISTORICAL: Archaeological surveys confirm that after the Persian and subsequent Hellenistic periods, Babylon's population declined dramatically, and the site was largely abandoned by the early Christian era, fulfilling this oracle.

verses.flow: Take up your positions against Babylon all around, all you who bend the bow; shoot at her, spare no arrows, for she has sinned against YHWH.

verses.flow: Israel is a scattered sheep; lions have driven him away. First the king of Assyria devoured him, and last this Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon has gnawed his bones.
Therefore thus says YHWH of hosts (Tseva'ot), the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing punishment on the king of Babylon and his land, as I brought punishment on the king of Assyria.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tseva'ot" (H6635, hosts/armies) is the divine title invoking YHWH's command over heavenly armies — both angelic forces and cosmic powers. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title YHWH Tseva'ot positions him as the supreme commander of the divine council's military forces, placing his judgment on Babylon within a cosmic-warfare framework (cf. 1 Kgs 22:19; Ps 103:21). TYPOLOGY: The parallel punishment of Assyria and Babylon establishes a pattern: all anti-God empires fall under the same divine judgment.

verses.flow: And I will restore Israel to his pasture, and he shall graze on Carmel and in Bashan, and his soul (nephesh) shall be satisfied on the hills of Ephraim and in Gilead.

In those days and in that time, declares YHWH, the iniquity of Israel shall be sought, and there shall be none, and the sins of Judah, and they shall not be found, for I will pardon those whom I leave as a remnant.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The complete erasure of Israel's iniquity "sought and not found" is among the most radical forgiveness declarations in the Hebrew Bible. This points beyond the historical return from exile to the eschatological atonement accomplished by the Messiah (Isa 53:5-6; Heb 10:17). The "remnant" (sherit, H7611) language connects to the faithful remnant theology running throughout the prophets.

verses.flow: Go up against the land of Merathaim, and against the inhabitants of Pekod. Destroy and devote them to destruction (cherem), declares YHWH, and do all that I have commanded you.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Cherem" (H2764, devotion to destruction/sacred ban) is the strongest term for total consecrated destruction in the Hebrew Bible, used for the Canaanite conquest (Josh 6:17). Its application to Babylon elevates this judgment to holy-war status. HISTORICAL: "Merathaim" (double rebellion) and "Pekod" (punishment) are both wordplays: Merathaim puns on the name of the southern Babylonian marshlands, and Pekod is the name of an Aramean tribe east of the Tigris.

verses.flow: The sound of war is in the land, and great destruction.

verses.flow: How the hammer of the whole earth is cut off and broken! How Babylon has become a horror (shammah) among the nations!
I set a snare for you and you were caught, O Babylon, and you did not know it; you were found and also seized, because you strove against YHWH.

verses.flow: YHWH has opened his armory and brought out the weapons of his wrath (za'am), for the Lord YHWH of hosts (Adonai YHWH Tseva'ot) has a work to do in the land of the Chaldeans.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The opening of YHWH's "armory" (otzar, H214, treasury/storehouse) and bringing forth "weapons of his wrath" (za'am, H2195) reflects the cosmic-warrior imagery of the divine council where YHWH commands supernatural forces of judgment (cf. Ps 78:49; Job 38:22-23). HEBREW: "Za'am" (H2195) is a term for fierce divine indignation, distinct from the more general af (anger), connoting burning hot wrath.

verses.flow: Kill all her bulls; let them go down to the slaughter. Woe to them, for their day has come, the time of their punishment.
The voice of those who flee and escape from the land of Babylon, to declare in Zion the vengeance (naqam) of YHWH our God, the vengeance for his temple.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The declaration in Zion of YHWH's "vengeance for his temple" (naqam miqdashо) links Babylon's destruction directly to the desecration of the Jerusalem sanctuary in 586 BC. The temple's destruction was not merely political catastrophe but cosmic affront to YHWH's dwelling place. TYPOLOGY: NT theology identifies believers as the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 3:16), whose vindication is equally assured.

verses.flow: Call together the archers against Babylon, all those who bend the bow. Encamp all around her; let no one escape. Repay her according to her deeds; according to all that she has done, do to her. For she has acted arrogantly against YHWH, against the Holy One (Qadosh) of Israel.
Therefore her young men shall fall in her squares, and all her soldiers shall be destroyed in that day, declares YHWH.

Thus says YHWH of hosts (Tseva'ot): The sons of Israel are oppressed, and the sons of Judah with them. All who took them captive have held them fast; they have refused to let them go.

verses.flow: Their Redeemer (Go'el) is strong; YHWH of hosts is his name. He will surely plead their cause, that he may give rest (nuach) to the earth, but unrest (rogez) to the inhabitants of Babylon.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Go'el" (H1350, Kinsman-Redeemer) is the most intimate and legally specific redemption term in the Hebrew Bible — the go'el is the nearest male relative obligated to redeem family members from slavery, debt, or danger (Ruth 4; Lev 25:25). TYPOLOGY: Applying this title to YHWH in the context of exile-redemption is profoundly anticipatory of Christ's work as the ultimate Go'el who redeems enslaved humanity through his own blood (Gal 4:4-5; 1 Pet 1:18-19). The strength of this Go'el is contrasted with the powerlessness of Babylon's gods.

verses.flow: A sword upon the Chaldeans, declares YHWH, and upon the inhabitants of Babylon, and upon her officials and her wise men.

verses.flow: A drought (chorev) upon her waters, that they may be dried up. For it is a land of images (pesilim), and they are mad (yitholelu) over idols.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chorev" (H2721, drought/drying up) — the pun on "cherev" (sword, H2719) may be intentional, as verses 35-38 form a sword-refrain now climaxing in a drought oracle. "Pesilim" (H6456, carved images) and "yitholelu" (they are mad/go mad over) together paint Babylon's idolatry as collective insanity. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The drying of Babylon's waters likely alludes to the draining of the Euphrates channel, which Persian forces reportedly used to enter Babylon — a judgment enacted through natural and military means simultaneously.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: Therefore wild beasts shall dwell with hyenas in Babylon, and ostriches shall dwell in her. She shall never again be inhabited, nor shall she be dwelt in from generation to generation.
As when God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah and their neighboring cities, declares YHWH, so no man shall dwell there, and no son of man shall sojourn in her.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The explicit comparison to the divine overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19) elevates Babylon's judgment to the category of archetypal divine catastrophe. As Sodom became the paradigm of irreversible judgment in prophetic literature (Isa 1:9; Amos 4:11), so Babylon inherits that paradigmatic status. The NT book of Revelation fuses both images (Rev 18:2, 10). THEOLOGICAL: The permanence of "no man shall dwell there" signals not merely political defeat but eschatological erasure — the end of an empire as a spiritual power.

verses.flow: Behold, a people comes from the north; a great nation and many kings are being stirred up from the farthest parts of the earth.

verses.flow: Behold, like a lion coming up from the thicket of the Jordan against a perennial pasture, I will suddenly make them run away from her, and I will appoint over her whomever I choose. For who is like me? Who will summon me? What shepherd can stand before me?
verses.note: HEBREW: The lion-from-Jordan imagery (also in Jer 49:19) functions as a divine-warrior motif — the unpredictable, sovereign predator who cannot be resisted or questioned. The rhetorical questions "Who is like me? Who will summon me?" assert YHWH's absolute incomparability, echoing the Exodus doxology (Exod 15:11) and anticipating NT Christology (Rev 5:5, where Christ is the Lion of Judah). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The question "What shepherd can stand before me?" may allude to the divine council challenge — no heavenly ruler (shepherd-king) can countermand YHWH's sovereign appointment.

verses.flow: Therefore hear the plan (etsah) of YHWH that he has made against Babylon, and the purposes that he has purposed against the land of the Chaldeans: Surely the little ones of their flock shall be dragged away; surely their pasture shall be appalled at them.
At the sound of the capture of Babylon the earth shall tremble, and a cry shall be heard among the nations.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "sound of the capture" anticipating earth-trembling is consistent with the magnitude of Babylon's fall in 539 BC under Cyrus II — an event that reorganized the entire ancient Near East. TYPOLOGY: The cosmic resonance of Babylon's fall in this verse is taken up in Revelation 18:9-10, where the kings of the earth weep at the fall of eschatological Babylon. The "cry heard among the nations" reflects the divine-council proclamation spreading through the heavenly and earthly realms simultaneously.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "Behold, I am stirring up against Babylon, and against the inhabitants of Leb-kamai, a destroying wind (ruach mashchit)."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Leb-kamai" (לֵב קָמָי) is a cryptographic device called atbash (substitution cipher), where each letter is replaced by its mirror counterpart in the Hebrew alphabet, here encoding "Kasdim" (Chaldea/Chaldeans). Strong's H3820/H6965. This same cipher appears in Jer 25:26 for "Sheshach" encoding Babylon. HISTORICAL: The use of atbash may reflect prophetic caution or literary artistry; it signals that the oracle targets the heartland of Babylonian power.

verses.flow: "For Israel and Judah are not widowed of their God, of YHWH of hosts (YHWH Tzva'ot), though their land is full of guilt before the Holy One of Israel."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The declaration that Israel and Judah are "not widowed" (lo' alman) from YHWH, even amid exile, affirms the unbreakable covenantal bond (hesed) despite national sin. YHWH Tzva'ot denotes the LORD as commander of heavenly armies, not merely earthly ones. The phrase anticipates the New Covenant of Jer 31:31-34, where YHWH permanently re-unites His people to Himself (Heb 8:8-12).

verses.flow: "Flee from the midst of Babylon, and let every man save his life; be not cut off in her iniquity, for this is the time of YHWH's vengeance; He is rendering to her her due recompense."
"Babylon was a golden cup in the hand of YHWH, making all the earth drunk; from her wine the nations drank, therefore the nations went mad."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of Babylon as a "golden cup" (kos zahav) in YHWH's hand presents a striking sovereignty paradox: Babylon's world domination was itself an instrument of divine purpose, yet she is held responsible for her intoxicating influence over the nations. TYPOLOGY: Revelation 17:4 and 18:6 directly echo this image, identifying eschatological Babylon with the golden cup of abominations, confirming the typological function of historical Babylon as a pattern for end-time opposition to God.

verses.flow: "Sharpen the arrows, take up the shields! YHWH has stirred up the spirit (ruach) of the kings of the Medes, because His purpose against Babylon is to destroy it; for it is the vengeance of YHWH, the vengeance of His temple."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Medes (Madai) were an Indo-Iranian people whose empire, under Cyaxares and later allied with Cyrus the Great of Persia, formed the coalition that defeated Babylon in 539 BC. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The stirring of the "ruach" (spirit/wind) of foreign kings is consistent with divine council theology — YHWH commissions and releases spiritual energy upon human rulers to accomplish His cosmic purposes (cf. Isa 44:28; 45:1; Ezra 1:1). THEOLOGICAL: "Vengeance of His temple" ties Babylon's destruction directly to the desecration of the Jerusalem sanctuary (cf. 2 Chr 36:17-19).

verses.flow: "Raise a standard against the walls of Babylon; make the watch strong; post the sentinels; prepare the ambushes; for YHWH has both purposed and done what He has spoken concerning the inhabitants of Babylon."
"O you who dwell by many waters, rich in treasures, your end has come; the thread of your life is cut."

verses.flow: "YHWH of hosts (YHWH Tzva'ot) has sworn by Himself: Surely I will fill you with men as with locusts, and they shall raise a shout against you."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's swearing "by Himself" (Hebrew: benaphsho, "by His soul/self") is the highest possible oath, used when there is no greater being by whom to swear (cf. Gen 22:16; Heb 6:13). This formula guarantees the absolute certainty of Babylon's judgment. DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH Tzva'ot, the LORD of hosts, here asserts His supreme authority over all heavenly armies, making His word irrevocable and binding across all realms.

verses.flow: "It is He who made the earth by His power, who established the world by His wisdom, and by His understanding He stretched out the heavens."
verses.note: HEBREW: These verses (15-19) are nearly identical to Jer 10:12-16, functioning as a doxological refrain that frames YHWH's power over creation against the impotence of idols. The repetition is deliberate, forming an inclusio that anchors the judgment of idol-worshiping Babylon. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's creation of the earth "by His power" (bekocho), the world "by His wisdom" (bechokhmato), and the heavens "by His understanding" (bitvunato) reflects the Trinitarian attributes of divine action found in Prov 8 and later developed Christologically (Col 1:16; John 1:3).

verses.flow: "When He utters His voice there is a tumult of waters in the heavens, and He makes the mist rise from the ends of the earth. He makes lightnings for the rain, and He brings forth the wind (ruach) from His storehouses."
"Every man is brutish and without knowledge; every goldsmith is put to shame by his carved image, for his cast image is falsehood, and there is no breath (ruach) in them."
"They are vanity (hevel), a work of delusion; in the time of their punishment they shall perish."
"Not like these is He who is the Portion of Jacob, for He is the One who formed all things, and Israel is the tribe of His inheritance; YHWH of hosts (YHWH Tzva'ot) is His name."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The designation of YHWH as "the Portion of Jacob" (chelek Ya'akov) stands in sharp antithesis to the nations' idol portions. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In the background lies the Deuteronomy 32:8-9 divine council worldview, where YHWH reserved Jacob/Israel as His own allotment when He assigned the nations to the sons of God (bene Elohim). Israel's identity as YHWH's "tribe of inheritance" (shevet nachalato) reflects this unique covenantal election within a cosmic framework (Deut 32:9; cf. Ps 82).

verses.flow: "Behold, I am against you, O destroying mountain, declares YHWH, which destroys the whole earth; I will stretch out My hand against you, and roll you down from the crags, and make you a burnt-out mountain."
verses.note: HEBREW: Babylon is called a "destroying mountain" (har hamashchit), a striking inversion — great empires were likened to mountains in ANE cosmology as seats of divine power and stability. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's declaration "I am against you" (hineni elayich) is a covenant-lawsuit formula of utter divine opposition. The image of the mountain becoming a "burnt-out mountain" (har serepha) suggests total, permanent judgment with no residual power remaining. TYPOLOGY: Mountains as cosmic thrones echo Ezek 28:14-16 (Tyre's "holy mountain of God") and apocalyptic traditions about the dethronement of wicked powers.

verses.flow: "And no stone shall be taken from you for a cornerstone, and no stone for a foundation, but you shall be a desolation forever, declares YHWH."

"Raise a standard in the land; blow a trumpet among the nations; consecrate the nations against her; summon against her the kingdoms of Ararat, Minni, and Ashkenaz; appoint a marshal against her; bring up horses like bristling locusts."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ararat corresponds to the ancient kingdom of Urartu (modern Armenia); Minni to the Manneans southeast of Lake Urmia; and Ashkenaz to a people associated with the ancient Scythians or related northern groups. These northern peoples joined the Median-Persian coalition that overthrew Babylon. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "consecrating" (qaddesh) of nations for warfare echoes the concept of holy war commissioned by YHWH's divine council decree, where human armies serve as instruments of heavenly judgment.

verses.flow: "Consecrate the nations against her, the kings of the Medes, their governors and all their deputies, and every land of their dominion."

verses.flow: "For thus says YHWH of hosts (YHWH Tzva'ot), the God of Israel: The daughter of Babylon is like a threshing floor at the time when it is trodden; yet a little while and the time of her harvest will come."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The threshing floor (goren) as a metaphor for judgment appears throughout Scripture — Ruth 3 (redemption), 2 Sam 24 (plague/atonement), and Matt 3:12 where John the Baptist describes the Messiah's judgment with a winnowing fork. The "time of harvest" for Babylon anticipates the final eschatological harvest of Rev 14:14-20. HEBREW: "Daughter of Babylon" (bat Bavel) personifies the city as a feminine entity, standard ANE rhetorical convention for inhabited cities.

verses.flow: "Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon has devoured me; he has crushed me; he has made me an empty vessel; he has swallowed me like a sea monster (tannin); he has filled his belly with my delicacies; he has rinsed me out."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tannin" (תַּנִּין) is the great sea creature/dragon of ancient Near Eastern mythology, associated with chaos and devouring power (cf. Ps 74:13-14; Isa 27:1; Job 7:12). Nebuchadnezzar's swallowing of Jerusalem is depicted in terms borrowed from ancient chaos dragon imagery, giving his conquest cosmic-mythological dimensions. THEOLOGICAL: Zion's complaint in this verse mirrors the lament psalms, and YHWH's response in v. 36 follows the classic pattern of divine vindication: accusation of the enemy, divine answer, and promised judgment.

verses.flow: Therefore thus says YHWH: "Behold, I will plead your cause and take vengeance for you; I will dry up her sea and make her fountain dry;"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's pledge to "plead your cause" (rav et rivekem) uses the legal vocabulary of a kinsman-redeemer (go'el), the nearest male relative obligated to defend family honor and rights. This frames YHWH as the divine Go'el of Israel, a role fulfilled ultimately through Jesus the Messiah, who as the incarnate Son redeems His people from the greatest bondage (Isa 59:20; Rom 11:26-27). TYPOLOGY: The drying up of Babylon's "sea and fountain" may allude to the cosmic combat imagery of YHWH drying up the sea at the Exodus (Ps 74:13-15), recasting the conquest of Babylon as a new Exodus.

verses.flow: "How Sheshach is taken, the praise of the whole earth seized! How Babylon has become a horror among the nations!"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sheshach" is again the atbash cipher for Babylon (cf. v. 1 note on Leb-kamai). The phrase "praise of the whole earth" (tehillat kol ha'aretz) echoes Ps 48:2's description of Jerusalem as "the joy of all the earth," here inverted — Babylon claimed for itself the glory that belongs to Zion. THEOLOGICAL: The ironic collapse of the city celebrated as "the praise of all the earth" demonstrates YHWH's consistent pattern of humbling the exalted (cf. Isa 14:12-15; Luke 14:11).

verses.flow: "And I will punish Bel in Babylon, and take out of his mouth what he has swallowed. The nations shall no longer flow to him; the wall of Babylon has fallen."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Bel (בֵּל), the chief deity of Babylon (equivalent to Marduk), is here directly confronted by YHWH. This is not merely political polemic but reflects divine council theology — YHWH judges the patron deity of Babylon, rendering him powerless and disgorging what he swallowed (the nations' tribute and Israel's wealth). Compare Ps 82 where YHWH judges the divine council members, and Isa 46:1-2 where Bel and Nebo bow before YHWH. HISTORICAL: The Marduk cult was the religious-political heart of the Neo-Babylonian empire; its defeat signals the total collapse of Babylon's sacred order.

verses.flow: "Then the heavens and the earth, and all that is in them, shall sing for joy over Babylon, for the destroyers shall come against her from the north, declares YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: That "the heavens and the earth and all that is in them" (השמים והארץ וכל אשר בהם) shall sing for joy over Babylon's fall indicates the cosmic scope of this judgment. Creation itself rejoices when wickedness is judged, a theme running from Ps 96-98 through Rev 18:20 where heaven, saints, and apostles are called to rejoice at eschatological Babylon's fall. TYPOLOGY: This verse is directly echoed in Rev 18:20, confirming that historical Babylon's fall serves as the prophetic type for the eschatological destruction of all that opposes God's kingdom.

verses.flow: "And I will make drunk her officials and her wise men, her governors, her commanders, and her warriors; they shall sleep a perpetual sleep and not wake, declares the King, whose name is YHWH of hosts (YHWH Tzva'ot)."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH closes the judgment oracle by identifying Himself as "the King" (HaMelech) — asserting His sovereignty directly against the claims of Babylon's king. The title "YHWH of hosts" (YHWH Tzva'ot) frames this as a decree from the supreme commander of heavenly armies. TYPOLOGY: The "perpetual sleep" of Babylon's officials echoes the language of Ps 76:5-6 and anticipates the final judgment where the enemies of God's people face permanent, irreversible defeat (Rev 19:17-21).

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH of hosts (YHWH Tzva'ot): The broad wall of Babylon shall be leveled to the ground, and her high gates shall be burned with fire. The peoples labor for nothing, and the nations' work is only for fire — they weary themselves in vain."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The leveling of Babylon's "broad wall" (chomat Babel harechava) was one of the wonders of the ancient world, reportedly wide enough for chariots to pass abreast. Its demolition fulfills the principle that no human achievement can stand when YHWH decrees judgment. The phrase "they weary themselves in vain" (be-die esh) anticipates Hab 2:13, which quotes it almost verbatim — human toil that serves proud empire is ultimately futile. HISTORICAL: Herodotus describes Babylon's walls as among the greatest fortifications ever built; archaeologically, Nebuchadnezzar's massive building program confirms the grandeur described here.

verses.flow: The word that Jeremiah the prophet commanded Seraiah the son of Neriah, son of Mahseiah, when he went with Zedekiah the king of Judah to Babylon, in the fourth year of his reign. Seraiah was the quartermaster (sar menuchah).
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Seraiah ben Neriah is the brother of Baruch, Jeremiah's secretary (Jer 32:12; 36:4). His title "sar menuchah" (שַׂר מְנוּחָה) is debated: it may mean "quartermaster" (one responsible for provisions and lodging for the royal party) or "officer of the resting place." His journey to Babylon with Zedekiah in the fourth year (ca. 594 BC) likely reflects a diplomatic mission, possibly related to the anti-Babylonian conspiracy referenced in Jer 27-29. STRUCTURE: This colophon passage (vv. 59-64) forms a deliberate narrative bracket with Jer 36, where Baruch read a scroll and it was cast into fire; here Seraiah reads a scroll and casts it into water.

verses.flow: "When you have finished reading this scroll, tie a stone to it and cast it into the midst of the Euphrates,"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The symbolic act of binding a stone to the scroll and casting it into the Euphrates enacts prophetic word as reality — the weight of judgment sinks Babylon irrevocably. This acted prophecy (sign-act) is characteristic of Jeremiah's ministry (cf. Jer 13, 19, 27-28). TYPOLOGY: Revelation 18:21 directly mirrors this sign-act: "A mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone and threw it into the sea, saying, 'So will Babylon the great city be thrown down with violence, and will be found no more'" — confirming the typological-apocalyptic function of this passage for the eschatological destruction of all Babylon-like opposition to God.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: "and say: 'So shall Babylon sink, to rise no more, because of the disaster that I am bringing upon her, and they shall be exhausted.'" Thus far are the words of Jeremiah.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The colophon "Thus far are the words of Jeremiah" (ad hennah divrei Yirmeyahu) formally closes the original collection of Jeremiah's prophecies, indicating that chapter 52 (which follows) is a historical appendix drawn from 2 Kings 24-25, not attributed to Jeremiah's own words. This literary seam is significant for understanding the compositional history of the book. THEOLOGICAL: That the book ends — in its prophetic substance — with the total and irreversible judgment of Babylon is theologically deliberate: the arc of Jeremiah's ministry moves from the call to faithfulness, through judgment of unfaithful Israel, to the ultimate vindication of YHWH's justice against all who oppose His people and His name.

verses.flow: For because of the anger of YHWH it came to pass in Jerusalem and Judah until he had cast them out from before his face. And Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "because of the anger of YHWH" (Hebrew: al-aph YHWH, referring to divine wrath) frames the fall of Jerusalem not as a political accident but as covenantal judgment. YHWH's "casting them from before his face" echoes the expulsion language of Eden (Gen 3) and Cain (Gen 4:16), signaling a typological exile pattern. (Deut 29:24-28; Lam 1:5)

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the ninth year of his reign, in the tenth month, on the tenth day of the month, that Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon came, he and all his army, against Jerusalem, and they encamped against it and built siege-works against it all around.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Nebuchadrezzar" is the more accurate transliteration of the Akkadian Nabu-kudurri-usur, as used consistently in Jeremiah (cf. the variant "Nebuchadnezzar" in Kings and Daniel). Archaeological confirmation of this siege comes from the Babylonian Chronicles (BM 21946), which record military campaigns against Judah. "Siege-works" (Hebrew: dayeq) refers to siege ramps or assault towers constructed around the city.

verses.flow: then the city was breached, and all the men of war fled and went out from the city by night by the way of the gate between the two walls, which was by the king's garden — and the Chaldeans were all around against the city — and they went by the way of the Arabah.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "gate between the two walls" (Hebrew: sha'ar bayn ha-chomot) likely refers to a gate near the Kidron Valley providing egress toward the Jordan Arabah. The "way of the Arabah" is the Jordan Rift Valley escape route toward Jericho, confirming the topographic detail preserved in the narrative. This flight mirrors the flight patterns of earlier Judahite fugitives under Assyrian pressure.

verses.flow: And the eyes of Zedekiah he blinded, and he bound him with bronze fetters, and the king of Babylon brought him to Babylon and put him in the house of confinement until the day of his death.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The blinding of Zedekiah and his imprisonment "until the day of his death" (Hebrew: ad-yom moto) fulfilled precisely the oracles of Jeremiah (Jer 34:3) and Ezekiel simultaneously — Zedekiah would see the king of Babylon face to face (Jer 34:3) yet die in Babylon without seeing it (Ezek 12:13). This dual fulfillment demonstrates the complementary nature of prophetic revelation rather than contradiction.

verses.flow: Now in the fifth month, on the tenth day of the month — that was the nineteenth year of King Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon — Nebuzaradan the commander of the guard, who stood before the king of Babylon, came into Jerusalem.

verses.flow: And he burned the house of YHWH and the house of the king, and all the houses of Jerusalem; even every great house he burned with fire.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The burning of "the house of YHWH" (Hebrew: beyt YHWH) marks the catastrophic end of the Solomonic temple, the dwelling place of the divine Name and the focal point of covenant worship. This destruction is the culmination of the warnings embedded in Solomon's dedicatory prayer (1 Kgs 8:46-53) and the repeated prophetic calls to repentance. TYPOLOGY: The temple's destruction and the later return point typologically to Christ's body as the new temple destroyed and raised (John 2:19-21).

verses.flow: And the pillars of bronze that were in the house of YHWH, and the stands and the bronze sea that were in the house of YHWH, the Chaldeans broke in pieces and carried all their bronze to Babylon.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Pillars of bronze" (Hebrew: ammudey ha-nechoshet) refers to the twin pillars Jachin and Boaz (1 Kgs 7:15-22), monumental freestanding columns at the temple entrance. Their destruction symbolizes the total dismantling of the Solomonic cultic infrastructure. HISTORICAL: Bronze pillars of comparable description have been identified in Syro-Phoenician temple archaeology, consistent with the Phoenician craftsman Hiram's involvement in temple construction (1 Kgs 7:13-14).

verses.flow: The two pillars, the one sea, and the twelve bronze bulls that were under the stands, which King Solomon had made for the house of YHWH — the bronze of all these vessels was beyond weight.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "twelve bronze bulls" under the sea recall the cosmological symbolism of ANE temples, where bull imagery evoked divine strength and the ordering of creation. The removal of these items to Babylon represents a trophy transfer of divine patronage in ancient Near Eastern ideology — though Jeremiah's theology insists that YHWH, not the conquering gods, orchestrated this judgment. (Jer 27:6; Hab 1:5-11)

verses.flow: And the commander of the guard took Seraiah the chief priest, and Zephaniah the second priest, and the three keepers of the threshold.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Seraiah the chief priest (Hebrew: kohen harosh) is identified as the grandfather of Ezra the scribe (Ezra 7:1), connecting the final priestly leadership of the first temple to the reconstituted priestly line of the restoration period. His execution at Riblah marked the end of the Zadokite high priestly succession in Jerusalem for the duration of the exile.

verses.flow: And the king of Babylon struck them down and put them to death at Riblah in the land of Hamath. So Judah was carried into exile out of its land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "So Judah was carried into exile out of its land" (Hebrew: vayigel Yehudah me'al admato) constitutes the formal theological verdict of the chapter and of the book: the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28-29 has been fully enacted. The land itself has been defiled by covenant infidelity and now "vomits out" its inhabitants (Lev 18:28), yet the exile is not the final word — Jeremiah's own New Covenant oracle (Jer 31:31-34) promises restoration.

verses.flow: This is the people whom Nebuchadrezzar carried into exile: in the seventh year, three thousand and twenty-three people of Judah;
verses.note: HEBREW: The deportation figures in verses 28-30 differ from those recorded in 2 Kings 24-25, likely because Jeremiah's numbers count only adult male deportees while Kings gives total population estimates. Three distinct deportations are enumerated: 597 BC (seventh year of Nebuchadrezzar's reign by Babylonian reckoning), 587/586 BC (the fall of Jerusalem), and 582 BC (a subsequent punitive deportation, perhaps following the assassination of Gedaliah). HISTORICAL: The 582 BC deportation is otherwise unattested and represents a uniquely preserved datum.

verses.flow: in the eighteenth year of Nebuchadrezzar, eight hundred and thirty-two persons from Jerusalem;

in the twenty-third year of Nebuchadrezzar, Nebuzaradan the commander of the guard carried into exile of Judah seven hundred and forty-five persons — all the persons were four thousand and six hundred.

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the thirty-seventh year of the exile of Jehoiachin king of Judah, in the twelfth month, on the twenty-fifth day of the month, that Evil-merodach king of Babylon, in the year he became king, lifted up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah and brought him out from the house of confinement.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The release of Jehoiachin (Hebrew: yehoyakhin) from prison in Babylon by Evil-merodach functions as an intentional narrative grace note, closing the book with a thread of hope amid judgment. TYPOLOGY: Jehoiachin's elevation from prison to the king's table echoes Joseph's exaltation in Egypt (Gen 41) and anticipates the ultimate exaltation of the Davidic heir, Jesus the Messiah, whose resurrection constitutes the final lifting of the head of the royal prisoner. (Matt 1:12; Luke 1:32-33)

verses.flow: And he changed his prison garments, and he ate bread before him continually all the days of his life.
And his allowance — a continual allowance — was given to him from the king of Babylon, the portion of each day on its day, until the day of his death, all the days of his life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing image of a "continual allowance" (Hebrew: aruchah tamid) given to the Davidic king "all the days of his life" preserves the inextinguishable nature of the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:16; Ps 89:3-4). Though the king sits at a foreign table, not upon David's throne in Jerusalem, the line endures — pointing toward the Messiah through whom the covenant finds its ultimate and eternal fulfillment. (Luke 1:32-33; Rev 22:16)
